topics.title: Review of Neural Networks - Biological Neuron vs. Artificial Neuron
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT503 - DEEP LEARNING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Review of Neural Networks - Biological Neuron vs. Artificial Neuron

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Biological Neuron — Formal Definition

A **biological neuron** is the fundamental signaling unit of the human nervous system, specialized for receiving, processing, and transmitting electrochemical information. Structurally, it consists of three primary regions: the **dendrites** (input receptors), the **cell body (soma)** (processing hub containing the nucleus), and the **axon** (output transmission line) terminating in **synaptic terminals** that connect to downstream neurons.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Biological Property — All-or-None Action Potential**
> A biological neuron either fires a full-amplitude signal along its axon or does not fire at all. There is no "half-firing." This threshold-based behavior is the direct biological inspiration for the **step activation function** in early artificial neuron models.

## 1.2 Artificial Neuron — Formal Definition

An **artificial neuron** (also called a *perceptron* or *threshold logic unit*, originally proposed by **McCulloch and Pitts in 1943**) is a mathematical abstraction that mimics the input–processing–output behavior of a biological neuron. It accepts a vector of numerical inputs, multiplies each by a learnable weight, sums them with a bias term, and passes the result through a non-linear **activation function** to produce a single scalar output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> The KTU 2024 Scheme places significant emphasis on understanding the *biological-to-artificial* mapping because every deep learning architecture (CNN, RNN, Transformer) is ultimately composed of cascading artificial neurons. The exam frequently asks for a comparative tabulation.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Office Mailroom**
> Think of a biological neuron as an **office mailroom clerk**:
> - **Incoming letters (dendrites):** Each letter carries a different *importance* — the boss's letter matters more than a peon's.
> - **Sorting desk (soma):** The clerk weighs all letters together. If the total importance crosses a threshold (say, "highly urgent"), the clerk forwards a summary to the next department.
> - **Outgoing courier (axon):** The summary travels to the next office.
> - **Receptionist of next office (synapse):** Passes the message along, possibly amplifying or dampening it.
>
> An **artificial neuron** is the *mathematical photocopy* of this clerk: each letter is an input $x_i$, its importance is the weight $w_i$, the desk's threshold is the bias $b$, the "highly urgent" rule is the activation function $f(\cdot)$, and the courier's summary is the output $y$.

## 1.4 The Three Operative Stages (Common to Both)

| Stage | Biological Equivalent | Artificial Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Input Reception** | Dendrites receive neurotransmitters from many upstream neurons | Inputs $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$ enter the neuron |
| **Integration / Processing** | Soma accumulates post-synaptic potentials in the membrane | Weighted sum $z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b$ is computed |
| **Output Generation** | Axon hillock triggers an action potential if threshold crossed | Activation function $y = f(z)$ produces the scalar output |

## 1.5 Why This Mapping Matters in Deep Learning

The biological neuron's energy efficiency (~20 W for the entire human brain doing complex vision/language tasks) is the *aspirational benchmark* for artificial neural networks. While today's GPU clusters consume **megawatts**, the *architectural principles* — many simple units, weighted connections, threshold-based firing, layered composition — are directly inherited. Understanding this analogy enables students to reason about why modern innovations (e.g., **dropout** ≈ random synaptic silencing; **batch normalization** ≈ homeostatic regulation) were conceived.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Artificial Neuron (Forward Pass)

The forward computation of a single artificial neuron proceeds through **four sequential sub-operations**:

1. **Input Reception:** A vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T \in \mathbb{R}^n$ arrives from upstream neurons or the input layer.

2. **Synaptic Weighting:** Each input is scaled by a corresponding learnable parameter $w_i \in \mathbb{R}$. Collectively, these form the weight vector $\mathbf{w} = [w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n]^T$.

3. **Linear Aggregation with Bias:** A pre-activation scalar is computed by the dot product plus a bias term $b \in \mathbb{R}$:
   $$z = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b$$

4. **Non-linear Activation:** A differentiable (or piecewise) function $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ maps $z$ to the final output:
   $$y = f(z) = f\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b\right)$$

### 2.1.1 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why weighting?** Different inputs carry different *relevance*. The weight represents the *strength* of the connection — analogous to synaptic efficacy in biology.
- **Why a bias?** It shifts the decision boundary away from the origin, giving the neuron flexibility to fire even when all inputs are zero. Biologically, it represents the *resting potential offset*.
- **Why non-linearity?** Without $f$, stacking layers collapses to a single linear transformation. Non-linearity is the *mathematical engine* of representational depth in deep networks.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, \dots, x_n]^T$ | Input feature vector | $x_i \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| $\mathbf{w} = [w_1, \dots, w_n]^T$ | Learnable weight vector | $w_i \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| $b$ | Bias term (shifts activation) | $b \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| $z = \sum w_i x_i + b$ | Pre-activation (net input) | $z \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| $y = f(z)$ | Post-activation output | $y \in \mathbb{R}$ (or $[0,1]$) |
| $f(z) = \text{step}(z)$ | Step function (McCulloch-Pitts) | $y \in \{0, 1\}$ |
| $f(z) = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Sigmoid (logistic) | $y \in (0, 1)$ |
| $f(z) = \tanh(z)$ | Hyperbolic tangent | $y \in (-1, 1)$ |
| $f(z) = \max(0, z)$ | ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit) | $y \in [0, \infty)$ |
| $\eta$ (eta) | Learning rate | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| $E = \frac{1}{2}(y_{\text{pred}} - y_{\text{true}})^2$ | Squared-error loss (single sample) | $E \geq 0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "write the mathematical model of an artificial neuron," the *complete* answer is $y = f\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b\right)$. Students frequently omit the activation function $f$ and lose marks. The activation is **non-optional** in any modern formulation.

## 2.3 Biological vs. Artificial — Detailed Analytical Comparison

| Property | Biological Neuron | Artificial Neuron |
|---|---|---|
| **Basic Unit** | Nerve cell (neuron) | Mathematical node (perceptron) |
| **Number in human brain** | ~$8.6 \times 10^{10}$ (86 billion) | Unlimited (defined by model) |
| **Input signals** | Neurotransmitter chemicals at dendrites | Numerical values $x_i$ |
| **Connection strength** | Synaptic efficacy (variable) | Weight $w_i$ (learnable) |
| **Processing mechanism** | Ion-channel electrochemistry | Arithmetic summation |
| **Threshold** | ~$-55\,\text{mV}$ (membrane potential) | Bias $b$ (shifted by learning) |
| **Output** | Action potential (frequency-modulated) | Scalar $y$ (amplitude-modulated) |
| **Signal type** | Spikes (temporal) | Continuous real number |
| **Learning mechanism** | Hebbian plasticity (LTP/LTD) | Gradient descent (back-prop) |
| **Speed** | ~$1\text{ ms}$ per spike | ~$\text{ns}$ per FLOP (GPU) |
| **Energy per operation** | ~$10^{-15}$ J | ~$10^{-9}$ J |
| **Topology** | Mostly feed-forward + recurrent loops | Defined by architecture (FFN/CNN/RNN) |
| **Fault tolerance** | High (degeneracy) | Low (sensitive to weight corruption) |
| **Activation** | All-or-none firing | Smooth or hard threshold $f(z)$ |
| **Connectivity per neuron** | Up to $\sim 10^4$ synapses | Hundreds to thousands typically |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Neuroscience-inspired AI:** Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) explicitly mimic the *temporal spike trains* of biological neurons for ultra-low-power edge AI.
- **Medical Imaging:** CNNs — built from cascaded artificial neurons — detect tumors by learning hierarchical visual features analogous to the human visual cortex (V1 $\to$ V2 $\to$ V4).
- **Neuromorphic Hardware:** Intel's **Loihi** and IBM's **TrueNorth** chips are silicon implementations of the biological-neuron abstraction, achieving orders-of-magnitude better energy efficiency.
- **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI):** Decoding motor cortex neuron firing patterns to control prosthetic limbs requires understanding both the biological signal origin and the artificial decoder.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Biological Firing Rule to the McCulloch-Pitts Model (1943)

The original McCulloch-Pitts neuron was a *binary threshold device*. The derivation below maps the biological firing rule to its mathematical form.

### Step 1 — Observe the biological rule
A biological neuron fires (output = 1) if and only if the *total excitatory input* exceeds a critical threshold $\theta$, and there is *no inhibitory input* present. Mathematically, this is observed as:

$$\text{Fire} \iff \left(\sum_{i} \text{ExcitatoryInput}_i \geq \theta\right) \;\wedge\; \left(\text{No Inhibitory Input}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Replace inputs with numerical values
Let the neuron receive $n$ binary inputs $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$. Each input is *excitatory* (contributes positively) with weight $w_i = +1$, or *inhibitory* (contributes negatively) with weight $w_i = -1$.

$$z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i$$

### Step 3 — Introduce the threshold parameter
The biological threshold $\theta$ is mapped to a parameter that, in modern notation, is reformulated as a *negative bias* term. Define the bias as $b = -\theta$. Then:

$$z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i - \theta = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b$$

**Conversion logic:** The threshold $\theta$ becomes the negative of the bias so that the activation condition $z \geq 0$ (firing) replaces the original condition $z \geq \theta$.

### Step 4 — Apply the binary step activation
The output is:

$$y = f(z) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } z \geq 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This is the **Heaviside step function** applied to the net input.

### Step 5 — Final canonical form
Combining all the above, the McCulloch-Pitts neuron is fully specified as:

$$y = f\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b\right), \quad f(z) = \begin{cases} 1, & z \geq 0 \\ 0, & z < 0 \end{cases}$$

**This is the canonical "artificial neuron" equation** that all subsequent deep learning models (perceptron, MLP, CNN, RNN) generalize.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Why the Sigmoid is a Smooth Generalization of the Step Function

A major limitation of the McCulloch-Pitts neuron is that its step activation is **non-differentiable**, so gradient-based learning is impossible. The sigmoid was introduced as a smooth, differentiable approximation.

### Step 1 — Start with the step function
For reference, the step function has the form:

$$f_{\text{step}}(z) = \begin{cases} 1, & z \geq 0 \\ 0, & z < 0 \end{cases}$$

### Step 2 — Introduce a "sharpness" parameter $k$
Replace the hard threshold with a soft threshold controlled by steepness $k > 0$:

$$f_k(z) = \begin{cases} 1, & kz \geq 0 \\ 0, & kz < 0 \end{cases} = \begin{cases} 1, & z \geq 0 \\ 0, & z < 0 \end{cases}$$

For *finite* $k$, we can use the logistic family. Consider the family:

$$f_k(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-kz}}$$

### Step 3 — Take the limit as $k \to \infty$
As the steepness parameter $k$ grows large, the sigmoid becomes infinitely steep at $z=0$ and approaches the step function. Formally:

$$\lim_{k \to \infty} \frac{1}{1 + e^{-kz}} = \begin{cases} 1, & z > 0 \\ \tfrac{1}{2}, & z = 0 \\ 0, & z < 0 \end{cases} \approx f_{\text{step}}(z)$$

**Conversion logic:** Increasing $k$ sharpens the transition region around $z=0$, recovering the discontinuous step in the limit.

### Step 4 — Compute the useful derivative
A key advantage of the sigmoid is its self-referential derivative, which makes back-propagation efficient:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma(z) &= \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} \\
\sigma'(z) &= \sigma(z)\bigl(1 - \sigma(z)\bigr)
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion logic:** Differentiate using the quotient rule or the chain rule on $e^{-z}$. The result is the *probability of the output times one minus that probability*, which has maximum value $0.25$ at $z = 0$ and decays to $0$ as $\vert z \vert \to \infty$.

### Step 5 — Why this matters for deep learning
Because $\sigma'(z)$ is *cheap to compute* (reuses $\sigma(z)$) and *bounded*, gradients are stable. This property made the sigmoid the **default activation from 1986 to ~2010**, before being largely replaced by ReLU in modern deep networks due to the vanishing-gradient problem at large $\vert z \vert$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

A single artificial neuron receives three inputs. Compute its output for a given weight/bias setting using both the step and sigmoid activations.

**Given:** $x_1 = 1,\; x_2 = 0.5,\; x_3 = -1$, weights $w_1 = 0.8,\; w_2 = -0.4,\; w_3 = 0.6$, bias $b = 0.1$.

### Step 1 — Compute the weighted sum
$$z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + w_3 x_3 + b$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values
$$\begin{aligned}
z &= (0.8)(1) + (-0.4)(0.5) + (0.6)(-1) + 0.1 \\
  &= 0.8 - 0.2 - 0.6 + 0.1 \\
  &= 0.1
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the step activation
$$y_{\text{step}} = \begin{cases} 1, & z \geq 0 \\ 0, & z < 0 \end{cases} \implies y_{\text{step}} = 1 \quad (\text{since } 0.1 \geq 0)$$

### Step 4 — Apply the sigmoid activation
$$\begin{aligned}
y_{\text{sigmoid}} &= \sigma(0.1) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.1}} \\
                   &= \frac{1}{1 + 0.9048} \\
                   &= \frac{1}{1.9048} \\
                   &\approx 0.5250
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Interpret the result
- The **step function** makes a hard decision: fire ($y = 1$).
- The **sigmoid** returns a *probability-like* confidence of $\approx 52.5\%$.

Both models give the same qualitative verdict (input is "more positive than negative"), but the sigmoid provides a graded, differentiable output that supports learning.

---

## 3.4 Learning in an Artificial Neuron — Gradient Descent Update Rule

A perceptron learns by adjusting its weights using the **Perceptron Learning Rule** (Rosenblatt, 1958). For a misclassified sample $(x^{(j)}, y^{(j)}_{\text{true}})$ with predicted output $\hat{y}^{(j)}$, the update is:

$$\begin{aligned}
w_i &\leftarrow w_i + \Delta w_i \\
\Delta w_i &= \eta \bigl(y^{(j)}_{\text{true}} - \hat{y}^{(j)}\bigr) x_i^{(j)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion logic:**
- $\bigl(y_{\text{true}} - \hat{y}\bigr)$ is the *error signal* — it is positive if the neuron under-fired, negative if it over-fired.
- Multiplying by $x_i$ ensures that *large inputs get larger corrections* (Hebbian principle: "neurons that fire together, wire together").
- $\eta$ scales the step size to prevent oscillations.

This rule has a famous limitation: it **only converges for linearly separable data** (Minsky & Papert, 1969). This historical setback motivated the development of multi-layer networks trained via back-propagation — the foundation of modern deep learning.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Side-by-Side Mapping: Biological vs. Artificial Neuron

The following Mermaid diagram presents a structural architecture-flow schematic mapping each biological component to its artificial counterpart, including the signal flow direction and the operational stage each component belongs to.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph bioLeft["BIOLOGICAL NEURON"]
        direction TB
        dend1[Dendrites] --> soma1[Soma Cell Body]
        soma1 --> axon1[Axon Hillock]
        axon1 --> action1{Threshold Check}
        action1 -->|fires| axonOut1[Axon Terminal]
        action1 -->|silent| silent1[No Output]
        axonOut1 --> syn1[Synaptic Terminals]
        syn1 -->|neurotransmitters| nextN1[Next Neuron]
    end

    subgraph artRight["ARTIFICIAL NEURON"]
        direction TB
        x1[Input x1] --> mul1[Multiply by w1]
        x2[Input x2] --> mul2[Multiply by w2]
        xN[Input xn] --> mulN[Multiply by wn]
        mul1 --> sum1[Weighted Sum plus Bias b]
        mul2 --> sum1
        mulN --> sum1
        sum1 --> act1[Activation Function f]
        act1 --> out1[Output y]
        out1 --> nextN2[Next Layer Neuron]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**
> The *biological side* traces the physical path of an electrochemical signal from dendrite reception, through somatic integration, to axonal transmission. The *artificial side* traces the equivalent mathematical path from input vector, through weighted summation, to activation-function output. Each block on the right has a *direct conceptual analog* on the left.

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Information Flow Through an Artificial Neuron

The following diagram elaborates the **four sub-operations** of the forward pass, including the dimensions of each intermediate tensor and the location where learning (weight updates) occurs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Vector x shape n times 1] --> B[Synaptic Weighting Layer w shape n times 1]
    B --> C[Dot Product w transpose x yields scalar]
    C --> D[Add Bias b scalar]
    D --> E[Pre-activation z scalar in R]
    E --> F[Activation Function f dot]
    F --> G[Output y scalar in R]
    G --> H[Loss Computation L of y y_true]
    H --> I[Backpropagation Gradient partial L partial w]
    I --> J[Weight Update w new w minus eta times gradient]
    J --> K[Updated Weights Return to B]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight**
> The diagram highlights the **two phases** of learning:
> 1. **Forward pass (top to bottom):** Input flows to output, loss is computed.
> 2. **Backward pass (bottom to top via dashed return path):** Gradients of loss w.r.t. weights are computed (chain rule), and weights are updated via gradient descent.
>
> This forward-backward cycle is the **core engine** of all deep learning, regardless of architecture (CNN, RNN, Transformer).

---

## 4.3 Comparison Schematic: Learning Paradigms

The following diagram contrasts the *biological learning mechanism* (Hebbian plasticity) with the *artificial learning mechanism* (gradient descent), emphasizing that both are fundamentally *correlation-based* update rules.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph bioLearn["BIOLOGICAL LEARNING HEBB 1949"]
        preS[Presynaptic Activity] --> corr[Correlation Detector]
        postS[Postsynaptic Activity] --> corr
        corr --> ltp[Long Term Potentiation Weight Increases]
        corr --> ltd[Long Term Depression Weight Decreases]
        ltp --> synStr[Synaptic Strength Modified]
        ltd --> synStr
    end

    subgraph artLearn["ARTIFICIAL LEARNING GRADIENT DESCENT"]
        predOut[Predicted Output] --> errCalc[Error Computation y true minus y pred]
        trueLab[True Label] --> errCalc
        errCalc --> gradCalc[Gradient Computation partial L partial w]
        gradCalc --> wUpdate[Weight Update w new w minus eta grad]
        wUpdate --> newW[New Weights Applied]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Bridge**
> Hebb's rule — *"`\textbf{Cells that fire together, wire together}`"* — is a special case of gradient descent under specific loss assumptions. Modern biologically-inspired learning rules (e.g., **Feedback Alignment**, **Direct Feedback Alignment**) depart from strict gradient descent to better match the cortex's observed learning signals, which do **not** require exact back-propagation of errors.

---

## 4.4 Architectural Diagram: From Single Neuron to Deep Network

The following schematic shows how individual artificial neurons compose into the **multi-layer perceptron (MLP)**, the foundational deep learning architecture referenced throughout KTU Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputLayer["INPUT LAYER"]
        i1[x1] 
        i2[x2]
        i3[x3]
    end

    subgraph hiddenLayer1["HIDDEN LAYER 1"]
        h1a[Neuron 11]
        h1b[Neuron 12]
        h1c[Neuron 13]
    end

    subgraph hiddenLayer2["HIDDEN LAYER 2"]
        h2a[Neuron 21]
        h2b[Neuron 22]
    end

    subgraph outputLayer["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        o1[Output y1]
        o2[Output y2]
    end

    i1 --> h1a
    i1 --> h1b
    i1 --> h1c
    i2 --> h1a
    i2 --> h1b
    i2 --> h1c
    i3 --> h1a
    i3 --> h1b
    i3 --> h1c

    h1a --> h2a
    h1a --> h2b
    h1b --> h2a
    h1b --> h2b
    h1c --> h2a
    h1c --> h2b

    h2a --> o1
    h2a --> o2
    h2b --> o1
    h2b --> o2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Deep"?**
> A network is "deep" when it has **two or more hidden layers**. The depth enables *hierarchical feature learning*: Layer 1 learns edges, Layer 2 learns textures/shapes, Layer 3 learns object parts, Layer 4 learns whole objects. This compositional structure is the *defining advantage* of deep learning over shallow models.

---
