# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**"List any three differences between a biological neuron and an artificial neuron."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| # | Biological Neuron | Artificial Neuron |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Signal is a frequency-modulated *spike train* along the axon | Signal is a continuous *real-valued scalar* output |
| 2 | Learning occurs via *synaptic plasticity* (LTP/LTD) | Learning occurs via *gradient descent* on a loss function |
| 3 | Connection strength is governed by *neurotransmitter release probability* | Connection strength is governed by a *learned weight parameter* $w_i$ |

*(Any three valid differences earn full marks. The most commonly expected pair is "spike train vs. continuous value" and "synaptic plasticity vs. gradient descent.")*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**"Write the mathematical model of an artificial neuron and explain the role of the activation function."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The artificial neuron computes its output as:

$$y = f\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b\right)$$

**Role of the activation function $f(\cdot)$:**

1. **Introduces non-linearity:** Without $f$, a cascade of neurons collapses mathematically to a single linear transformation $\mathbf{y} = W\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}$, which cannot model complex input–output mappings such as XOR.
2. **Bounds the output:** Functions like sigmoid constrain $y \in (0,1)$, providing a *probability-like* interpretation useful for classification.
3. **Enables gradient-based learning:** Smooth activations (sigmoid, tanh, ReLU) are differentiable, allowing back-propagation to compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_i}$ via the chain rule.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examinations award **14 marks** to a single Part B question, with internal choice. The structure below presents two alternative questions; in the actual exam, the student answers *one*.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **structure of a biological neuron**. Discuss how each biological component inspires a corresponding element in the **mathematical model of an artificial neuron**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **McCulloch-Pitts neuron model** starting from the biological all-or-none firing rule, clearly showing how the threshold parameter maps to the bias term. Further, compute the output of a single artificial neuron for the input vector $\mathbf{x} = [1, 0, 1, 1]^T$ with weights $\mathbf{w} = [0.5, -0.3, 0.8, 0.2]^T$ and bias $b = -0.4$, using **both the step and sigmoid activations**. **(7 Marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a)

**1. Biological Neuron Structure (Labelled Description, 4 Marks):**

A biological neuron consists of:
- **Dendrites:** Branch-like extensions that receive input signals from upstream neurons in the form of neurotransmitter release at synapses.
- **Cell Body (Soma):** Contains the nucleus and cellular machinery; integrates all incoming post-synaptic potentials in the membrane.
- **Axon Hillock:** The decision-making region of the soma; if the integrated membrane potential exceeds the critical threshold (~$-55\,\text{mV}$), an action potential is triggered.
- **Axon:** A long tubular structure that propagates the action potential to the axon terminals.
- **Synaptic Terminals:** Release neurotransmitters onto the dendrites of the next neuron, with the *release strength* (synaptic efficacy) being modifiable by experience.

**2. Mapping to Artificial Neuron (3 Marks):**

| Biological Component | Artificial Equivalent | Mathematical Role |
|---|---|---|
| Dendrites (input receivers) | Input ports $x_1, \dots, x_n$ | Carry the feature values into the neuron |
| Synaptic efficacy (variable strength) | Weights $w_1, \dots, w_n$ | Scale each input by its learned importance |
| Soma (integrator) | Summation unit $\sum w_i x_i + b$ | Aggregates all weighted inputs plus bias |
| Axon hillock threshold | Bias $b$ + activation threshold | Determines the firing condition |
| Action potential (binary spike) | Output of $f(z)$ | Produces a scalar output value |

The canonical mathematical model is:

$$y = f\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b\right)$$

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b)

**1. Derivation of McCulloch-Pitts Model (4 Marks):**

*Step 1 — Biological firing rule:* A biological neuron fires if and only if the *sum of excitatory inputs* exceeds the threshold $\theta$ and *no inhibitory input* is active.

*Step 2 — Mathematical formulation:* With $n$ binary inputs $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$, assign weight $w_i = +1$ for excitatory and $w_i = -1$ for inhibitory connections. The net input is:

$$z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i$$

*Step 3 — Threshold to bias conversion:* The firing condition is $z \geq \theta$. Define $b = -\theta$, so the condition becomes $z + b \geq 0$. The complete net input is:

$$z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b$$

*Step 4 — Step activation:* Output is $1$ if $z \geq 0$, else $0$:

$$y = f(z) = \begin{cases} 1, & z \geq 0 \\ 0, & z < 0 \end{cases}$$

**2. Numerical Computation (3 Marks):**

*Step 1 — Compute the weighted sum:*

$$\begin{aligned}
z &= w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + w_3 x_3 + w_4 x_4 + b \\
  &= (0.5)(1) + (-0.3)(0) + (0.8)(1) + (0.2)(1) + (-0.4) \\
  &= 0.5 + 0 + 0.8 + 0.2 - 0.4 \\
  &= 1.1
\end{aligned}$$

*Step 2 — Apply the step activation:*

$$y_{\text{step}} = \begin{cases} 1, & z \geq 0 \\ 0, & z < 0 \end{cases} \implies y_{\text{step}} = 1$$

*Step 3 — Apply the sigmoid activation:*

$$\begin{aligned}
y_{\text{sigmoid}} &= \sigma(1.1) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-1.1}} \\
                   &= \frac{1}{1 + 0.3329} \\
                   &= \frac{1}{1.3329} \\
                   &\approx 0.7503
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $y_{\text{step}} = 1$ and $y_{\text{sigmoid}} \approx 0.7503$. Both indicate a "fire" decision, with the sigmoid providing a $\approx 75\%$ confidence.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]

**(a)** Compare and contrast a **biological neuron** with an **artificial neuron** under the headings: (i) signal type, (ii) learning mechanism, (iii) activation rule, and (iv) energy efficiency. Prepare a comparison table with at least **four rows**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **sigmoid activation function** in detail. Show mathematically that it is a **smooth, differentiable approximation** of the step function. Compute the derivative $\sigma'(z)$ and discuss why this differentiability is essential for **back-propagation learning**. **(7 Marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a)

**Comparison Table (7 Marks — 1.75 per row):**

| Property | Biological Neuron | Artificial Neuron |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Signal type** | Frequency-modulated spike trains propagating along the axon | Continuous real-valued scalar output $y = f(z)$ |
| **(ii) Learning mechanism** | Synaptic plasticity: long-term potentiation (LTP) and long-term depression (LTD) of synaptic efficacy | Gradient descent: weights updated as $w_i \leftarrow w_i - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial w_i}$ via back-propagation |
| **(iii) Activation rule** | All-or-none firing at the axon hillock when membrane potential exceeds $\approx -55\,\text{mV}$ | Step / sigmoid / ReLU function $f(z)$ applied to the net input $z = \sum w_i x_i + b$ |
| **(iv) Energy efficiency** | $\approx 10^{-15}$ joules per operation; entire brain runs on $\sim 20\,\text{W}$ | $\approx 10^{-9}$ joules per FLOP; large models require $\sim \text{MW}$ of GPU power |

**Brief Justification (any 2–3 sentences per row in the actual answer):**

- (i) Biological neurons use *spikes* — discrete temporal events — whereas artificial neurons use a *single continuous number*. The biological model is thus a *rate code* approximation in most ANNs.
- (ii) Hebbian plasticity is *local* (depends only on pre- and post-synaptic activity); gradient descent is *global* (requires end-to-end error signal via chain rule). Modern "biologically plausible" algorithms attempt to bridge this gap.
- (iii) The biological threshold is fixed by ion-channel biophysics; the artificial threshold (bias) is a *learned parameter* that shifts with training.
- (iv) Biology is roughly **six orders of magnitude** more energy-efficient per operation, which is the *primary motivator* for neuromorphic computing research.

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b)

**1. Sigmoid Function Definition (1 Mark):**

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

The sigmoid maps any real number $z \in \mathbb{R}$ into the open interval $(0, 1)$. It is **monotonically increasing**, **S-shaped (sigmoidal)**, with $\sigma(0) = 0.5$, $\sigma(z) \to 1$ as $z \to +\infty$, and $\sigma(z) \to 0$ as $z \to -\infty$.

**2. Smooth Approximation of the Step Function (3 Marks):**

Introduce a steepness parameter $k > 0$ into the logistic family:

$$\sigma_k(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-kz}}$$

As $k$ increases, the curve becomes steeper around $z = 0$. In the limit:

$$\lim_{k \to \infty} \sigma_k(z) = \begin{cases} 1, & z > 0 \\ \tfrac{1}{2}, & z = 0 \\ 0, & z < 0 \end{cases}$$

This limit recovers the Heaviside step function (with a minor caveat at $z = 0$ where the sigmoid gives $\tfrac{1}{2}$ rather than a discontinuous jump). For all $z \neq 0$, however, $\sigma_k(z) \to f_{\text{step}}(z)$. Hence the sigmoid is the *smooth, differentiable, parameterized soft-step* used in lieu of the discontinuous step to enable gradient-based learning.

**3. Derivative Computation (2 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma'(z) &= \frac{d}{dz}\!\left[\frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}\right] \\
           &= \frac{d}{dz}\!\left[(1 + e^{-z})^{-1}\right] \\
           &= -1 \cdot (1 + e^{-z})^{-2} \cdot (-e^{-z}) \\
           &= \frac{e^{-z}}{(1 + e^{-z})^2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion logic:** Apply the chain rule. Rewrite $\frac{e^{-z}}{(1+e^{-z})^2}$ by multiplying numerator and denominator by $e^{2z}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma'(z) &= \frac{e^{-z} \cdot e^{2z}}{(1 + e^{-z})^2 \cdot e^{2z}} \\
           &= \frac{e^{z}}{(e^{z} + 1)^2}
\end{aligned}$$

Now factor the denominator: $(e^z + 1)^2 = (1 + e^z)(1 + e^z)$. Use the identity $\sigma(z) = \frac{e^z}{1 + e^z}$ (multiply numerator and denominator of original by $e^z$) and $1 - \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^z}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma'(z) &= \frac{e^z}{1 + e^z} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + e^z} \\
           &= \sigma(z)\bigl(1 - \sigma(z)\bigr)
\end{aligned}$$

This compact form is computationally cheap because it *reuses* the already-computed forward-pass value $\sigma(z)$.

**4. Importance for Back-Propagation (1 Mark):**

Back-propagation applies the chain rule to compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_i}$ for every weight. Each layer's gradient requires multiplying by $\sigma'(z)$ of the next layer. Because $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ is bounded in $(0, 0.25]$, gradients are *stable in magnitude* and *easy to compute*, making sigmoid-based networks trainable. *(Caveat: for very large $\vert z \vert$, $\sigma'(z) \to 0$ — the **vanishing gradient** problem — which is why ReLU has largely replaced sigmoid in modern deep networks.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on This Topic**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the activation function $f(\cdot)$:** Writing $y = \sum w_i x_i + b$ without $f$ is *incomplete*. The activation is a mandatory part of the model. **Penalty: 1–2 marks lost.**
>
> 2. **Confusing bias with threshold:** Many students interchange $b$ and $\theta$. Remember: **bias $b = -\theta$**, and the firing condition is $z + b \geq 0$, *not* $z \geq b$.
>
> 3. **Omitting dimensions/shapes in derivations:** When writing $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}$, the examiner expects to see that $\mathbf{w}, \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ and the result is a scalar. State this explicitly.
>
> 4. **Treating "biological" and "artificial" as identical:** Examiners *deliberately* mark down answers that conflate the two. Always present biological neuron in *anatomical* terms (dendrite, soma, axon, synapse) and the artificial neuron in *mathematical* terms (input, weight, sum, activation).
>
> 5. **Forgetting the biological to mathematical mapping table:** A common KTU question is "Explain how the artificial neuron is inspired by the biological neuron." The full-credit answer *requires* a mapping table — not just prose.
>
> 6. **Numerical computation errors:** In computing $z = \sum w_i x_i + b$, students often drop the bias or mis-sign the inhibitory weights. **Always recompute $z$ from scratch during the exam.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read This the Night Before the Exam**

- ✅ A **biological neuron** has dendrites (input), soma (integration), axon hillock (threshold), axon (transmission), and synaptic terminals (variable-strength output).
- ✅ The **action potential** is **all-or-none** — this is the biological origin of the *binary step activation* in early artificial models.
- ✅ The **artificial neuron** computes $y = f\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b\right)$ where $f$ is the activation, $w_i$ are the weights, and $b$ is the bias.
- ✅ The **bias $b$** is mathematically equivalent to a *negative threshold*; it shifts the activation function along the $z$-axis.
- ✅ The **sigmoid** $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ is a smooth, differentiable approximation of the step function, with derivative $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\bigl(1 - \sigma(z)\bigr)$.
- ✅ **ReLU** $f(z) = \max(0, z)$ is the modern default in deep networks due to its *non-vanishing gradient* for $z > 0$.
- ✅ The **Perceptron Learning Rule** is $w_i \leftarrow w_i + \eta(y_{\text{true}} - \hat{y})x_i$, but it only converges for *linearly separable* data.
- ✅ **Hebbian plasticity** ("fire together, wire together") is the biological analog of correlation-based weight updates in artificial neurons.
- ✅ The **human brain** has ~$8.6 \times 10^{10}$ neurons, each with up to $\sim 10^{4}$ synapses, consuming only ~$20\,\text{W}$ — a benchmark modern AI cannot match.
- ✅ **Deep networks** = artificial neurons stacked into $\geq 2$ hidden layers, enabling *hierarchical feature learning* (edges $\to$ textures $\to$ parts $\to$ objects).
- ✅ **McCulloch-Pitts (1943)** = first mathematical neuron model; **Rosenblatt (1958)** = perceptron with learning; **Rumelhart et al. (1986)** = back-propagation — the three historical milestones to remember.
- ✅ **Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs)** are modern biologically-faithful models that use *temporal spike trains* instead of continuous activations, bridging the gap back to biology for energy-efficient neuromorphic computing.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Activation Functions (Step, Sign, Sigmoid, Softmax, Tanh, ReLU, Leaky ReLU, ELU)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT503 - DEEP LEARNING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Activation Functions (Step, Sign, Sigmoid, Softmax, Tanh, ReLU, Leaky ReLU, ELU)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Activation Function** is a mathematical *gating operator* applied to the pre-activation (weighted sum of inputs plus bias) of a neuron in an Artificial Neural Network (ANN). Formally, for a neuron receiving input vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ with weight vector $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ and bias $b \in \mathbb{R}$, the activation function $\phi: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ transforms the linear combination $z = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + b$ into a non-linear output $a = \phi(z)$.

$$a = \phi\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{i}x_{i} + b\right) = \phi(z)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Without a non-linear activation function, a multi-layer neural network collapses mathematically into a single linear transformation (Universal Approximation is **violated**). Non-linearity is the *sine qua non* of Deep Learning.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a neuron as a **light switch with a dimmer**:

- The inputs ($x_i$) and weights ($w_i$) decide *how much electricity* flows into the switch.
- The **activation function** decides *how much of that electricity* is actually allowed to pass through to the bulb.
- A **Step function** is like a hard on/off switch (binary).
- A **Sigmoid** is like a *smooth dimmer* that gradually brightens.
- A **ReLU** is like a dimmer that *only allows the bulb to brighten* but never dims below zero.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we need them:** If every neuron just passed its input straight through (identity function), stacking 100 layers would be mathematically equivalent to using 1 layer. Activation functions introduce **non-linearity**, enabling the network to learn complex, curved decision boundaries (e.g., separating a spiral from a circle).

## 1.3 Classification of Activation Functions

| Family | Examples | Key Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Binary (Discrete)** | Step, Sign | Output is restricted to two values |
| **Sigmoidal (Smooth, Bounded)** | Sigmoid, Tanh | Differentiable, squashes input into a finite range |
| **Probabilistic (Multi-class)** | Softmax | Outputs a valid probability distribution |
| **Rectified (Piecewise Linear)** | ReLU, Leaky ReLU | Sparse activation, mitigates vanishing gradient |
| **Exponential (Smoother Rectified)** | ELU, SELU | Zero-mean, smoother negative side |

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Reference Values to Memorize:**
> - $\sigma(0) = 0.5$
> - $\tanh(0) = 0$
> - $\text{ReLU}(0) = 0$
> - Softmax of a vector sums to **1**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Binary Activation Functions

### 2.1.1 Step Function (Heaviside)
The simplest and historically earliest activation. Used in the original **McCulloch-Pitts Neuron (1943)**.

- **Operational Logic:** Outputs 1 if input $\geq$ threshold, else 0.
- **The 'Why':** Creates a hard decision boundary. Neuron is either *fully firing* or *silent*.
- **The 'How' (Drawback):** The derivative is **zero everywhere** (except undefined at 0). This means **Gradient Descent fails** — backpropagation cannot update weights. Hence, *cannot be used in modern deep networks*.

### 2.1.2 Sign Function
- **Operational Logic:** Outputs $+1$ for $z > 0$, $-1$ for $z < 0$, and $0$ at exactly $z=0$.
- **Utility:** Often used in the output layer of **SVMs** or for **binary classification problems** where class labels are encoded as $\{-1, +1\}$.
- **Drawback:** Same as Step — non-differentiable at 0, gradient is zero elsewhere.

---

## 2.2 Sigmoidal (S-shaped) Activation Functions

### 2.2.1 Sigmoid (Logistic Function)
- **Operational Logic:** Smoothly maps any real number into the interval $(0, 1)$.
- **The 'Why':** Differentiable everywhere, monotonic, historically the default choice.
- **The 'How' (Drawback):** **Vanishing Gradient Problem** — for large positive/negative $z$, the gradient approaches $0$, causing slow or stalled training in deep networks. Outputs are **not zero-centered** (always positive), causing zig-zagging of gradient updates.
- **Usage Today:** Primarily in **binary classification output layers** (since output is a probability).

### 2.2.2 Hyperbolic Tangent (Tanh)
- **Operational Logic:** A rescaled and shifted version of sigmoid. Maps to $(-1, 1)$.
- **The 'Why':** **Zero-centered** output, which makes optimization easier and convergence faster than sigmoid.
- **The 'How' (Drawback):** Still suffers from vanishing gradient for large $\vert z \vert$.
- **Usage Today:** Often preferred over sigmoid in **hidden layers of RNNs/LSTMs**.

### 2.2.3 Softmax (Normalized Exponential)
- **Operational Logic:** A generalization of sigmoid to $K$ classes. Converts a vector of $K$ real numbers into a probability distribution.
- **The 'Why':** Ensures all outputs sum to 1 and are non-negative, ideal for **multi-class classification**.
- **The 'How':** Computationally expensive for large $K$ (involves exponentials and normalization), but numerically stable implementations exist (subtracting the max for stability).

---

## 2.3 Rectified Linear Family

### 2.3.1 ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit) — The Default Modern Choice
- **Operational Logic:** $f(z) = \max(0, z)$. Passes positive values unchanged, kills negatives.
- **The 'Why':** *Massively accelerates convergence* (≈6x in Krizhevsky et al., 2012). Sparse activation (≈50% of neurons are zero at any time). Computationally cheap (just a threshold at zero).
- **The 'How' (Drawback):** **Dying ReLU Problem** — once a neuron outputs 0, the gradient is 0, so weights never update and the neuron *permanently dies*.
- **Usage Today:** **Default activation for hidden layers** in CNNs, MLPs, and most feedforward networks.

### 2.3.2 Leaky ReLU
- **Operational Logic:** Allows a small negative slope $\alpha$ (typically $\alpha = 0.01$) when $z < 0$.
- **The 'Why':** Fixes the *Dying ReLU* problem by ensuring the gradient is never exactly zero on the negative side.
- **The 'How':** Introduces a hyperparameter $\alpha$. PReLU (Parametric ReLU) learns $\alpha$ via backpropagation.

### 2.3.3 ELU (Exponential Linear Unit)
- **Operational Logic:** Smoothly saturates to a negative value $-\alpha$ for $z < 0$ via an exponential.
- **The 'Why':** *Pushes mean activations closer to zero* (like batch normalization) → faster learning. Smoother gradient near zero → more robust to noise.
- **The 'How' (Drawback):** Computationally more expensive due to the exponential. Output can become very negative.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Function | Forward Equation $\phi(z)$ | Derivative $\phi'(z)$ | Output Range | Zero-Centered? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Step** | $\phi(z) = \begin{cases} 1 & z \geq 0 \\ 0 & z < 0 \end{cases}$ | $0$ (almost everywhere) | $\{0, 1\}$ | No |
| 2 | **Sign** | $\phi(z) = \text{sgn}(z) \in \{-1, 0, 1\}$ | $0$ (almost everywhere) | $\{-1, 1\}$ | Yes |
| 3 | **Sigmoid** | $\phi(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | $\phi(z)\bigl(1 - \phi(z)\bigr)$ | $(0, 1)$ | No |
| 4 | **Tanh** | $\phi(z) = \dfrac{e^{z} - e^{-z}}{e^{z} + e^{-z}}$ | $1 - \tanh^{2}(z)$ | $(-1, 1)$ | Yes |
| 5 | **Softmax** | $\phi(z_{i}) = \dfrac{e^{z_{i}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_{j}}}$ | $\phi_{i}(\delta_{ij} - \phi_{j})$ | $(0, 1)$, sum $= 1$ | Yes |
| 6 | **ReLU** | $\phi(z) = \max(0, z)$ | $\begin{cases} 1 & z > 0 \\ 0 & z \leq 0 \end{cases}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | No |
| 7 | **Leaky ReLU** | $\phi(z) = \max(\alpha z,\, z),\ \alpha \approx 0.01$ | $\begin{cases} 1 & z > 0 \\ \alpha & z \leq 0 \end{cases}$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | No |
| 8 | **ELU** | $\phi(z) = \begin{cases} z & z \geq 0 \\ \alpha(e^{z} - 1) & z < 0 \end{cases}$ | $\begin{cases} 1 & z \geq 0 \\ \alpha e^{z} & z < 0 \end{cases}$ | $(-\alpha, \infty)$ | Yes (approx.) |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION REMINDER:** The Softmax derivative uses the Kronecker delta $\delta_{ij} = 1$ if $i = j$ and $0$ otherwise. This compact form $\phi_{i}(\delta_{ij} - \phi_{j})$ is the **Jacobian matrix** form used in backpropagation.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sigmoid** $\rightarrow$ Logistic regression, **LSTM gate mechanisms** (forget/input/output gates use sigmoid).
- **Softmax** $\rightarrow$ Final layer of **ImageNet classifiers** (ResNet, VGG), **language model vocabulary prediction**.
- **ReLU** $\rightarrow$ Backbone of **AlexNet (2012)** — the catalyst of the Deep Learning revolution.
- **Leaky ReLU / ELU** $\rightarrow$ Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) where the *dying ReLU* issue is severe, and in very deep networks (e.g., ResNet-1000+).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sigmoid Function and Its Derivative

The sigmoid function is defined as:

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

**Step 1 — Reformulate using a substitution.**
Let $u = 1 + e^{-z}$. Then $\sigma(z) = u^{-1}$.

**Step 2 — Differentiate using the chain rule.**

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = \frac{d}{dz}\left(u^{-1}\right) = -u^{-2} \cdot \frac{du}{dz}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\frac{du}{dz}$.**

$$\frac{du}{dz} = \frac{d}{dz}\left(1 + e^{-z}\right) = -e^{-z}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the derivative expression.**

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = -u^{-2} \cdot (-e^{-z}) = \frac{e^{-z}}{u^{2}} = \frac{e^{-z}}{(1 + e^{-z})^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of $\sigma(z)$ itself.**
Multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{z}$:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = \frac{e^{-z}}{(1 + e^{-z})^{2}} \cdot \frac{e^{z}}{e^{z}} = \frac{1}{e^{z}(1 + e^{-z})^{2}} \cdot e^{z} \cdot e^{-z} \cdot \frac{e^{z}}{e^{z}}$$

A cleaner route: split the fraction:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} \cdot \frac{e^{-z}}{1 + e^{-z}} = \sigma(z) \cdot \frac{e^{-z}}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

Note that $\dfrac{e^{-z}}{1 + e^{-z}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} = 1 - \sigma(z)$.

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z) \cdot \bigl(1 - \sigma(z)\bigr)}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the maximum derivative.**
Since $\sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ is maximized at $z = 0$:

$$\sigma'(0) = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum gradient of sigmoid is only 0.25.** For multi-layer networks, multiplying many such values (each $< 1$) leads to **exponential decay of gradients** — this is the formal reason for the vanishing gradient problem.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Tanh in Terms of Sigmoid

The hyperbolic tangent can be written as a shifted/scaled sigmoid.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition.**

$$\tanh(z) = \frac{e^{z} - e^{-z}}{e^{z} + e^{-z}}$$

**Step 2 — Divide numerator and denominator by $e^{z}$.**

$$\tanh(z) = \frac{1 - e^{-2z}}{1 + e^{-2z}}$$

**Step 3 — Rewrite using the sigmoid identity** $\sigma(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$.

Notice that $1 - \sigma(x) = \sigma(-x)$. Thus:

$$1 - \sigma(2z) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{-2z}} = \frac{e^{-2z}}{1 + e^{-2z}}$$

**Step 4 — Combine to get the closed form.**

$$\tanh(z) = 2\sigma(2z) - 1$$

This is the **fundamental relationship** that the KTU examiner loves to test.

**Step 5 — Derivative via chain rule.**

$$\frac{d}{dz}\tanh(z) = \frac{d}{dz}\bigl[2\sigma(2z) - 1\bigr] = 2 \cdot 2 \cdot \sigma(2z)\bigl(1 - \sigma(2z)\bigr) = 4\sigma(2z)\bigl(1 - \sigma(2z)\bigr)$$

Substituting back: since $\tanh(z) = 2\sigma(2z) - 1$, we have $\sigma(2z) = \dfrac{\tanh(z) + 1}{2}$. After substitution:

$$\boxed{\tanh'(z) = 1 - \tanh^{2}(z)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Softmax Jacobian (Backpropagation Form)

The Softmax function for class $i$:

$$S_{i} = \frac{e^{z_{i}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_{j}}}$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate $S_{i}$ with respect to $z_{k}$.**

Apply the quotient rule:

$$\frac{\partial S_{i}}{\partial z_{k}} = \frac{\frac{\partial e^{z_{i}}}{\partial z_{k}} \cdot \sum_{j} e^{z_{j}} - e^{z_{i}} \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial z_{k}}\sum_{j} e^{z_{j}}}{\left(\sum_{j} e^{z_{j}}\right)^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the partial derivatives.**

- $\dfrac{\partial e^{z_{i}}}{\partial z_{k}} = e^{z_{i}}\delta_{ik}$ (using the Kronecker delta).
- $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial z_{k}}\sum_{j} e^{z_{j}} = e^{z_{k}}$.

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$\frac{\partial S_{i}}{\partial z_{k}} = \frac{e^{z_{i}}\delta_{ik}\sum_{j} e^{z_{j}} - e^{z_{i}} e^{z_{k}}}{\left(\sum_{j} e^{z_{j}}\right)^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Factor out $S_{i} = \dfrac{e^{z_{i}}}{\sum_{j} e^{z_{j}}}$.**

$$\frac{\partial S_{i}}{\partial z_{k}} = S_{i}\left(\delta_{ik} - \frac{e^{z_{k}}}{\sum_{j} e^{z_{j}}}\right) = S_{i}\bigl(\delta_{ik} - S_{k}\bigr)$$

**Result (Jacobian matrix form):**

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial S_{i}}{\partial z_{k}} = S_{i}\bigl(\delta_{ik} - S_{k}\bigr)}$$

When $i = k$ (diagonal elements): $\partial S_{i}/\partial z_{i} = S_{i}(1 - S_{i})$.

When $i \neq k$ (off-diagonal): $\partial S_{i}/\partial z_{k} = -S_{i}S_{k}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** In backpropagation with Cross-Entropy loss, this Jacobian combined with the loss gradient **simplifies beautifully** to $\partial L/\partial z_{i} = S_{i} - y_{i}$, which is a major reason Softmax + Cross-Entropy is the *de facto* multi-class output pair.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Tanh Activation

**Problem:** Given inputs $x_1 = 1.0,\ x_2 = -2.0$, weights $w_1 = 0.5,\ w_2 = 0.8$, bias $b = 0.1$, compute the tanh activation and its gradient.

**Step 1 — Compute the pre-activation.**

$$z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b = (0.5)(1.0) + (0.8)(-2.0) + 0.1$$

$$z = 0.5 - 1.6 + 0.1 = -1.0$$

**Step 2 — Apply tanh activation.**

$$a = \tanh(-1.0) = \frac{e^{-1} - e^{1}}{e^{-1} + e^{1}} = \frac{0.3679 - 2.7183}{0.3679 + 2.7183} = \frac{-2.3504}{3.0862} \approx -0.7616$$

**Step 3 — Compute the gradient (for backprop).**

$$\tanh'(z) = 1 - \tanh^{2}(z) = 1 - (-0.7616)^{2} = 1 - 0.5800 = 0.4200$$

**Step 4 — Compute the gradient w.r.t. weight $w_1$.**

$$\frac{\partial a}{\partial w_1} = \tanh'(z) \cdot x_1 = 0.4200 \times 1.0 = 0.4200$$

**Step 5 — If learning rate $\eta = 0.1$ and target is $y = 0$, loss gradient $\partial L/\partial a = 2(a - y) = 2(-0.7616) = -1.5232$:**

$$w_1^{\text{new}} = w_1 - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial a} \cdot \frac{\partial a}{\partial w_1} = 0.5 - 0.1 \times (-1.5232) \times 0.4200$$

$$w_1^{\text{new}} = 0.5 + 0.06397 \approx 0.5640$$

The weight was **updated in the correct direction** (increased) because the activation was *too negative* relative to the target of 0.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Activation Function Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose Activation Function] --> B{Output Layer?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Multi-class?}
    B -- No --> D{Hidden Layer?}
    C -- Yes --> E[Use Softmax]
    C -- No --> F[Binary Classification?]
    F -- Yes --> G[Use Sigmoid]
    F -- No --> H[Use Linear / Identity]
    D -- Yes --> I{Deep Network > 10 layers?}
    I -- Yes --> J{Smooth Negative Slope?}
    I -- No --> K[Use ReLU default]
    J -- Yes --> L[Use ELU]
    J -- No --> M[Dying ReLU issue?]
    M -- Yes --> N[Use Leaky ReLU alpha 0.01]
    M -- No --> K
```

## 4.2 Neuron Computational Graph (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[Input x1] --> W1[Weight w1]
    X2[Input x2] --> W2[Weight w2]
    X3[Input xn] --> W3[Weight wn]
    B[Bias b] --> SUM
    W1 --> SUM
    W2 --> SUM
    W3 --> SUM
    SUM[Linear Sum z = wT x + b] --> PHI[Activation Function phi]
    PHI --> A[Output a]
    A --> NL[Non-linearity propagates to next layer]
```

## 4.3 Comparison of Output Ranges (Visual Block Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph "BINARY"
        S1[Step: 0 or 1]
        S2[Sign: -1 or +1]
    end
    subgraph "BOUNDED SMOOTH"
        S3[Sigmoid: 0 to 1]
        S4[Tanh: -1 to +1]
        S5[Softmax: 0 to 1 sum equals 1]
    end
    subgraph "UNBOUNDED"
        S6[ReLU: 0 to infinity]
        S7[Leaky ReLU: -infinity to infinity]
        S8[ELU: -alpha to infinity]
    end
```

## 4.4 Vanishing Gradient Process (Cause-Effect Chain)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Large positive or negative input z] --> B[Saturation Region of Sigmoid or Tanh]
    B --> C[Derivative phi prime z approaches 0]
    C --> D[Backpropagated gradient shrinks]
    D --> E[Weights in early layers update very slowly]
    E --> F[Network fails to learn or stalls]
    F --> G[Solution: Use ReLU or Leaky ReLU]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `SUM`, `PHI`) and contain no reserved keywords, markdown formatting inside labels, or HTML tables — ensuring clean compilation in standard Mermaid renderers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]

**Define the Sigmoid activation function. State its derivative and explain why it suffers from the vanishing gradient problem.**

**Model Answer:**

The **sigmoid (logistic) function** is defined as:

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

It maps any real input $z$ to the interval $(0, 1)$, making it useful for outputting probabilities. Its derivative is elegantly expressed in terms of itself:

$$\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\bigl(1 - \sigma(z)\bigr)$$

**Vanishing Gradient Problem:** The maximum value of $\sigma'(z)$ is $0.25$ (at $z=0$). For large $\vert z \vert$, $\sigma'(z) \rightarrow 0$. In a deep network during backpropagation, gradients are multiplied layer-by-layer. Since each gradient is bounded above by $0.25$, after $L$ layers the gradient can be as small as $0.25^{L}$, which is effectively **zero**. This causes weights in early layers to stop updating — the network stops learning.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often *define* the function correctly but **fail to quantify the vanishing gradient** with a numerical bound (e.g., $0.25^5 \approx 0.00098$). Always include a numeric or inequality justification for full 3 marks.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]

**Differentiate between ReLU and Leaky ReLU activation functions. Why is Leaky ReLU preferred in certain deep networks?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | ReLU | Leaky ReLU |
|---|---|---|
| Equation | $\max(0, z)$ | $\max(\alpha z, z),\ \alpha \approx 0.01$ |
| Negative side | Outputs exactly 0 | Outputs small negative $\alpha z$ |
| Derivative at $z<0$ | 0 | $\alpha$ (non-zero) |
| Dying neuron issue | Yes | Mitigated |

**ReLU** is computationally efficient and accelerates convergence, but suffers from the **Dying ReLU problem**: once a neuron's weighted sum becomes negative, its gradient is zero and its weights never update — the neuron becomes permanently inactive.

**Leaky ReLU** addresses this by allowing a small negative slope $\alpha$ (typically $0.01$), ensuring a non-zero gradient on the negative side. This keeps the neuron trainable, making Leaky ReLU the preferred choice in very deep networks (e.g., GANs, ResNet variants) and in scenarios where a significant fraction of neurons might otherwise die.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **Softmax activation function** with its mathematical formulation. Derive the gradient $\dfrac{\partial S_{i}}{\partial z_{k}}$ of Softmax and show that the off-diagonal Jacobian elements equal $-S_{i}S_{k}$. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A neural network layer receives the pre-activation vector $\mathbf{z} = [2.0,\ 1.0,\ 0.1]$. Compute the **Softmax output** $S_{i}$ for each element. Verify that the outputs sum to 1, and identify the predicted class. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

The Softmax function for $K$ classes transforms a real-valued vector $\mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R}^{K}$ into a probability distribution:

$$S_{i} = \frac{e^{z_{i}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_{j}}},\quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, K$$

**Derivation of the Jacobian (4 marks):**

Let $D = \sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_{j}}$. Then $S_{i} = e^{z_{i}} / D$.

$$\frac{\partial S_{i}}{\partial z_{k}} = \frac{\frac{\partial e^{z_{i}}}{\partial z_{k}} \cdot D - e^{z_{i}} \cdot \frac{\partial D}{\partial z_{k}}}{D^{2}}$$

Since $\partial e^{z_{i}}/\partial z_{k} = e^{z_{i}}\delta_{ik}$ and $\partial D/\partial z_{k} = e^{z_{k}}$:

$$\frac{\partial S_{i}}{\partial z_{k}} = \frac{e^{z_{i}}\delta_{ik}D - e^{z_{i}}e^{z_{k}}}{D^{2}} = \frac{e^{z_{i}}}{D}\left(\delta_{ik} - \frac{e^{z_{k}}}{D}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial S_{i}}{\partial z_{k}} = S_{i}\bigl(\delta_{ik} - S_{k}\bigr)}$$

**Off-diagonal case ($i \neq k$):** Since $\delta_{ik} = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial S_{i}}{\partial z_{k}} = -S_{i}S_{k}$$

This shows that increasing the logit $z_k$ of class $k$ *decreases* the probability of class $i$ (for $i \neq k$) — the classes compete for the probability mass. **\[1 mark\]**

**Diagonal case ($i = k$):** $\partial S_{i}/\partial z_{i} = S_{i}(1 - S_{i})$ — increasing a class's own logit increases its probability. **\[1 mark\]**

**Why use Softmax (1 mark):** It produces a valid probability distribution (non-negative, sums to 1), making it ideal for multi-class classification with the cross-entropy loss.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

Given $\mathbf{z} = [2.0,\ 1.0,\ 0.1]$ with $K = 3$.

**Step 1 — Compute exponentials (2 marks):**

$$e^{2.0} = 7.3891,\quad e^{1.0} = 2.7183,\quad e^{0.1} = 1.1052$$

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator $D$ (2 marks):**

$$D = 7.3891 + 2.7183 + 1.1052 = 11.2126$$

**Step 3 — Compute each $S_{i}$ (2 marks):**

$$S_{1} = \frac{7.3891}{11.2126} \approx 0.6590$$

$$S_{2} = \frac{2.7183}{11.2126} \approx 0.2424$$

$$S_{3} = \frac{1.1052}{11.2126} \approx 0.0986$$

**Step 4 — Verification and prediction (1 mark):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{3} S_{i} = 0.6590 + 0.2424 + 0.0986 = 1.0000 \;\checkmark$$

The predicted class is **Class 1** (highest probability $S_1 = 0.659$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Valuation Warning:** Students frequently commit **numerical overflow** by computing $e^{z_i}$ directly for large $z_i$ (e.g., $e^{1000} = \infty$). The standard mitigation is to **subtract $\max(\mathbf{z})$ from each element** before exponentiation. Always mention this stability trick in your answer for full credit.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat diagram of the ReLU function, derive its derivative. Explain the **Dying ReLU problem** and how **Leaky ReLU** solves it. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** For a neuron with inputs $\mathbf{x} = [0.5,\ -1.0,\ 2.0]$, weights $\mathbf{w} = [0.4,\ 0.6,\ -0.3]$, and bias $b = 0.2$:
  (i) Compute the pre-activation $z$.
  (ii) Apply the **Tanh** activation and find $a$.
  (iii) Compute the gradient $\dfrac{\partial a}{\partial w_{1}}$. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**ReLU Function Definition (2 marks):**

$$\phi(z) = \max(0, z) = \begin{cases} z & z > 0 \\ 0 & z \leq 0 \end{cases}$$

The graph is a horizontal line at 0 for all $z \leq 0$, then a straight line of slope 1 for $z > 0$ (forming a "bent" V-shape with the corner at the origin).

**Derivative (2 marks):**

$$\phi'(z) = \begin{cases} 1 & z > 0 \\ 0 & z \leq 0 \end{cases}$$

The function is **not differentiable at $z = 0$** in the classical sense, but in practice sub-gradients are used and $\phi'(0) = 0$ or $1$ is assigned by convention.

**Dying ReLU Problem (1.5 marks):**
If a neuron's pre-activation $z$ becomes negative due to a large weight update or aggressive learning rate, the output becomes 0. Since the derivative is also 0 in this region, **no gradient flows back** during backpropagation. The weights never update, and the neuron becomes **permanently dead** — outputting 0 for all future inputs.

**Leaky ReLU Solution (1.5 marks):**
Leaky ReLU is defined as $\phi(z) = \max(\alpha z, z)$ with $\alpha \in (0, 1)$, typically $\alpha = 0.01$. For $z < 0$, the output is a small negative value $\alpha z$ instead of 0. The gradient is non-zero (equal to $\alpha$), so the neuron **continues to receive gradient signals** and stays trainable, thereby solving the dying ReLU problem.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**(i) Pre-activation $z$ (2 marks):**

$$z = w_{1}x_{1} + w_{2}x_{2} + w_{3}x_{3} + b$$

$$z = (0.4)(0.5) + (0.6)(-1.0) + (-0.3)(2.0) + 0.2$$

$$z = 0.2 - 0.6 - 0.6 + 0.2 = -0.8$$

**(ii) Tanh activation (2 marks):**

$$a = \tanh(-0.8) = \frac{e^{-0.8} - e^{0.8}}{e^{-0.8} + e^{0.8}}$$

$$e^{0.8} \approx 2.2255,\quad e^{-0.8} \approx 0.4493$$

$$a = \frac{0.4493 - 2.2255}{0.4493 + 2.2255} = \frac{-1.7762}{2.6748} \approx -0.6640$$

**(iii) Gradient w.r.t. $w_1$ (3 marks):**

First, the derivative of tanh:

$$\tanh'(z) = 1 - \tanh^{2}(z) = 1 - (-0.6640)^{2} = 1 - 0.4409 = 0.5591$$

Then by the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial a}{\partial w_{1}} = \tanh'(z) \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial w_{1}} = \tanh'(z) \cdot x_{1} = 0.5591 \times 0.5 = 0.2796$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Sign errors in $z$:** Recheck arithmetic — a single sign flip costs **2 marks** in a 7-mark question.
> 2. **Forgetting the chain rule:** Many students compute only $\partial z / \partial w_1$ (which is just $x_1$) and forget to multiply by $\tanh'(z)$. This is the **#1 reason for partial credit loss**.
> 3. **Omitting the bias term $b$:** $b$ is part of the pre-activation. Forgetting it loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Decimal precision:** Keep at least 4 significant figures until the final answer to avoid cascading rounding errors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Activation functions are essential** — without non-linearity, deep networks are mathematically equivalent to a single linear layer.
- [x] **Sigmoid** $\sigma(z) = 1/(1+e^{-z})$ has range $(0,1)$, derivative $\sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$, max gradient $0.25$ → vanishing gradient.
- [x] **Tanh** $\in (-1, 1)$ is **zero-centered** (faster convergence than sigmoid); derivative is $1 - \tanh^{2}(z)$.
- [x] **Softmax** generalizes sigmoid to $K$ classes; outputs sum to 1; **Jacobian** is $S_i(\delta_{ik} - S_k)$.
- [x] **ReLU** $\max(0,z)$ is the **default modern choice** for hidden layers — fast, sparse, but suffers from **dying neurons**.
- [x] **Leaky ReLU** uses slope $\alpha \approx 0.01$ for $z<0$ to keep neurons alive.
- [x] **ELU** uses $\alpha(e^z - 1)$ for $z<0$ → smoother, near-zero-centered, but more expensive.
- [x] **Step and Sign** are **non-differentiable** → cannot be used with gradient-based training.
- [x] **Vanishing gradient** is solved by switching from sigmoid/tanh to **ReLU-family** activations in deep networks.
- [x] **Numerical stability tip:** subtract $\max(\mathbf{z})$ from logits before computing softmax exponentials.
- [x] **Pairing rule:** Softmax + Cross-Entropy in the output layer for multi-class; Sigmoid + Binary Cross-Entropy for binary.
- [x] **KTU 2024 favorite derivations:** (1) $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$; (2) $\tanh(z) = 2\sigma(2z) - 1$; (3) Softmax Jacobian; (4) ReLU sub-gradient.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Layer Perceptron
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT503 - DEEP LEARNING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Single Layer Perceptron

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Single Layer Perceptron (SLP)** is the foundational computational unit of artificial neural networks, originally proposed by **Frank Rosenblatt in 1958** as a mathematical model of biological neurons. Formally, it is a **linear binary classifier** consisting of a single trainable layer of artificial neurons that maps an input vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ to a single output $y \in \{0, 1\}$ (or $\{-1, +1\}$) by computing a weighted linear combination of the inputs, adding a bias term, and passing the result through a non-linear activation function.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The Single Layer Perceptron is a *feed-forward* network with exactly *one trainable weight layer* (no hidden layers), used for solving **linearly separable** binary classification problems under supervised learning.

Mathematically, the perceptron output is defined as:

$$y = \phi\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b\right) = \phi(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)$$

where $\mathbf{w} = [w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n]^T$ is the **weight vector**, $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$ is the **input feature vector**, $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the **bias term** (also called *threshold offset*), and $\phi(\cdot)$ is the **activation function** (typically a Heaviside step function for the classical perceptron).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Mail Sorting Room

Imagine a postal sorting room with a single clerk standing at the conveyor belt. The clerk receives letters (inputs) with several observable features — weight, size, postage stamp color, sender location. Each feature is assigned an **importance weight** based on past experience (a heavier weight means that feature matters more). The clerk mentally multiplies each feature by its weight, sums them up, and asks: *"Does this total exceed my decision threshold?"* If yes, the letter goes to the "Urgent" tray; otherwise, to "Regular."

The Single Layer Perceptron is exactly this clerk. The **inputs** are the letter features, the **weights** are the experience-based importances, the **bias** is the clerk's personal default tendency, and the **step function** is the decision threshold that produces a binary verdict. Training the perceptron is equivalent to the clerk refining their weights every time they sort a letter and receive feedback from the supervisor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The SLP draws a single straight line (in 2D), plane (in 3D), or hyperplane (in n-dimensions) to separate two classes. This is precisely why it can only solve **linearly separable** problems — a limitation famously demonstrated by the inability of an SLP to learn the XOR function (Minsky & Papert, 1969).

### 1.3 Components of a Single Layer Perceptron

A Single Layer Perceptron has the following structural components:

- **Input Layer (Layer 0):** Receives the $n$-dimensional feature vector $\mathbf{x}$. This layer is *not* trainable; it merely passes inputs forward.
- **Weight Matrix $\mathbf{W}$:** A vector of $n$ synaptic weights $\{w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n\}$ that scale each input.
- **Bias $b$:** An additional learnable parameter that shifts the decision boundary away from the origin.
- **Summation Unit ($\Sigma$):** Computes the net input $u = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b$.
- **Activation Function $\phi$:** A non-linear (usually step) function that converts the net input into a class label.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric:** The *decision boundary* of an SLP in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is the hyperplane defined by $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$, whose **geometric margin** from any point $\mathbf{x}_0$ is given by $\frac{\vert \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_0 + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$. The factor **$\mathbf{1}$** (the unit normal) defines the orientation of this hyperplane.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How an SLP Works (Step by Step)

The forward pass and learning of a Single Layer Perceptron proceed through the following structured pipeline:

- **Step 1 — Input Reception:** The perceptron receives an input vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$ from the feature space.
- **Step 2 — Weighted Summation:** Each input $x_i$ is multiplied by its corresponding synaptic weight $w_i$, and all products are summed together along with the bias $b$. This produces the *net input* (also called *pre-activation*).

$$u = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$$

- **Step 3 — Activation:** The net input $u$ is passed through the activation function $\phi$ to yield the final output $y$.

$$y = \phi(u)$$

- **Step 4 — Error Computation:** The predicted output $y$ is compared against the true label $t$ (also called *target* or *ground truth*). The error signal drives weight updates.

$$e = t - y$$

- **Step 5 — Weight Update (Perceptron Learning Rule):** If the perceptron misclassifies, the weights are adjusted proportionally to the input and error.

$$w_i \leftarrow w_i + \eta \cdot e \cdot x_i, \quad b \leftarrow b + \eta \cdot e$$

where $\eta \in (0, 1]$ is the **learning rate**, a hyperparameter that controls the step size of each weight update.

- **Step 6 — Iteration:** Steps 1–5 are repeated for every sample in the training set, for multiple *epochs*, until convergence (zero error on training set) or until a maximum iteration count is reached.

### 2.2 Activation Functions Used in SLP

The choice of activation function determines the geometry of the decision boundary and the convergence properties. For KTU 2024, the following four activation functions are essential:

- **Heaviside Step (Binary):** $\phi(u) = \begin{cases} 1, & u \geq 0 \\ 0, & u < 0 \end{cases}$
- **Sign (Bipolar):** $\phi(u) = \begin{cases} +1, & u \geq 0 \\ -1, & u < 0 \end{cases}$
- **Sigmoid (Logistic):** $\phi(u) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-u}}$ — used when gradient-based learning is desired
- **Linear (Identity):** $\phi(u) = u$ — produces a *linear neuron*; no decision boundary

> [!NOTE] > **Why a Step Function?** The classical Rosenblatt perceptron uses a non-differentiable step function because it models a *binary decision* (fire / don't fire, analogous to a biological neuron's all-or-nothing action potential). The non-differentiability is precisely why we use the *Perceptron Learning Rule* (a discrete update) rather than *gradient descent* in classical SLP. For *differentiable* activations (sigmoid), the **Delta Rule** or **Widrow-Hoff LMS rule** is used instead.

### 2.3 The Perceptron Convergence Theorem

A cornerstone result (Rosenblatt, 1962; Novikoff, 1962) states:

> If the training data is **linearly separable**, the perceptron learning algorithm is guaranteed to converge to a separating hyperplane in a *finite* number of updates. If not linearly separable, the algorithm will *never converge* (weights oscillate forever).

The bound on the number of mistakes (updates) before convergence is:

$$M \leq \left(\frac{R}{\gamma}\right)^2$$

where $R = \max_i \Vert \mathbf{x}_i \Vert$ is the radius bounding all training points, and $\gamma$ is the geometric margin of the optimal separating hyperplane.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Edge-deployable binary classifiers** (e.g., spam filters, fault detectors) on hardware with strict memory constraints.
- **Foundational building block** for Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) and modern deep networks.
- **Sensor thresholding** in IoT devices, where decisions must be made within microseconds.
- **Early-stage linear feature gates** in hybrid neuro-symbolic AI pipelines.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Net input (pre-activation) | $u$ | $u = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b$ | Linear combination of inputs |
| Vector form of net input | $u$ | $u = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$ | Compact notation |
| Step activation | $y$ | $y = \begin{cases} 1, & u \geq 0 \\ 0, & u < 0 \end{cases}$ | Binary output |
| Sign activation | $y$ | $y = \text{sign}(u) \in \{-1, +1\}$ | Bipolar output |
| Sigmoid activation | $y$ | $y = \sigma(u) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-u}}$ | Differentiable |
| Error signal | $e$ | $e = t - y$ | $t$ is target, $y$ is prediction |
| Weight update rule | $w_i^{\text{new}}$ | $w_i + \eta e x_i$ | Perceptron rule |
| Bias update rule | $b^{\text{new}}$ | $b + \eta e$ | Same learning rate |
| Geometric margin | $\gamma$ | $\gamma = \frac{\min_i \vert \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Distance to closest point |
| Convergence bound | $M$ | $M \leq \left(\frac{R}{\gamma}\right)^2$ | Finite if linearly separable |
| LMS cost (Widrow-Hoff) | $J$ | $J = \frac{1}{2} e^2 = \frac{1}{2}(t-y)^2$ | Mean-square error surrogate |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Perceptron Learning Rule from the Error-Correction Principle

**Goal:** Derive the update equations $w_i \leftarrow w_i + \eta e x_i$ and $b \leftarrow b + \eta e$ from first principles.

**Starting Point:** We define the *error* for a single training sample $(\mathbf{x}, t)$ as the deviation of the actual output $y$ from the desired target $t$:

$$e = t - y$$

The intuition behind the update rule is to *minimize* the magnitude of this error by adjusting the weights in the direction that would have reduced it.

**Step 1 — Express the net input explicitly in terms of weights:**

$$u = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b$$

**Step 2 — Observe that the output $y$ depends on $u$, which depends on $w_i$.** To reduce $|e|$, we need to understand how $e$ changes with respect to $w_i$. Using the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial e}{\partial w_i} = \frac{\partial (t - y)}{\partial w_i} = -\frac{\partial y}{\partial w_i} = -\frac{\partial y}{\partial u} \cdot \frac{\partial u}{\partial w_i}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the partial derivatives:**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial w_i} = x_i \quad \text{(direct from the linear combination)}$$

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial u} = \phi'(u) \quad \text{(derivative of the activation function)}$$

**Step 4 — For the classical step-function perceptron,** $\phi'(u)$ is undefined at $u = 0$ and zero elsewhere. We bypass this by appealing to the *error-correction* heuristic: if the error is positive (under-prediction, $t > y$, i.e., $t = 1$ and $y = 0$), we want to *increase* the net input $u$, which we achieve by *increasing* the weights. Hence the update direction is:

$$\Delta w_i \propto (+e) \cdot x_i$$

**Step 5 — Scale by the learning rate $\eta$:**

$$\Delta w_i = \eta \cdot e \cdot x_i$$

**Step 6 — Apply the update:**

$$\boxed{w_i^{\text{new}} = w_i + \eta \cdot e \cdot x_i}$$

**Step 7 — For the bias,** the same logic applies because $b$ contributes to $u$ with a coefficient of $1$ (just like $x_i = 1$ if it were a feature), giving:

$$\boxed{b^{\text{new}} = b + \eta \cdot e}$$

This completes the derivation. Notice that updates only occur when $e \neq 0$ (i.e., misclassification); when $e = 0$, the weights remain unchanged.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Training an SLP for the AND Gate

**Problem:** Train a Single Layer Perceptron with step activation to learn the logical AND function. Inputs: $x_1, x_2 \in \{0, 1\}$. Target: $t = x_1 \text{ AND } x_2$.

**Training Set:**

| Sample | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $t$ (target) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 3 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 4 | 1 | 1 | 1 |

**Hyperparameters:** $\eta = 1$, initial weights $w_1 = 0$, $w_2 = 0$, initial bias $b = 0$.

**Epoch 1, Sample 1:** $u = (0)(0) + (0)(0) + 0 = 0$, so $y = \phi(0) = 1$ (using the convention $u \geq 0 \Rightarrow y = 1$). Error: $e = 0 - 1 = -1$. Update:

$$w_1 \leftarrow 0 + (1)(-1)(0) = 0, \quad w_2 \leftarrow 0, \quad b \leftarrow 0 + (1)(-1) = -1$$

**Epoch 1, Sample 2:** $u = (0)(0) + (0)(1) + (-1) = -1$, so $y = 0$. Error: $e = 0 - 0 = 0$. **No update.**

**Epoch 1, Sample 3:** $u = (0)(1) + (0)(0) + (-1) = -1$, so $y = 0$. Error: $e = 0$. **No update.**

**Epoch 1, Sample 4:** $u = (0)(1) + (0)(1) + (-1) = -1$, so $y = 0$. Error: $e = 1 - 0 = +1$. Update:

$$w_1 \leftarrow 0 + (1)(+1)(1) = +1, \quad w_2 \leftarrow 0 + (1)(+1)(1) = +1, \quad b \leftarrow -1 + (1)(+1) = 0$$

**After Epoch 1:** $w_1 = 1, w_2 = 1, b = 0$. Decision boundary: $x_1 + x_2 = 0$.

**Epoch 2, Sample 1:** $u = (1)(0) + (1)(0) + 0 = 0 \Rightarrow y = 1$, but target is $0$. Error $e = -1$. Update:

$$w_1 \leftarrow 1 + (1)(-1)(0) = 1, \quad w_2 \leftarrow 1, \quad b \leftarrow 0 + (1)(-1) = -1$$

**Epoch 2, Sample 2:** $u = (1)(0) + (1)(1) + (-1) = 0 \Rightarrow y = 1$, target $0$. Error $e = -1$. Update:

$$w_1 \leftarrow 1, \quad w_2 \leftarrow 1 + (1)(-1)(1) = 0, \quad b \leftarrow -1 + (1)(-1) = -2$$

**Epoch 2, Sample 3:** $u = (1)(1) + (0)(0) + (-2) = -1 \Rightarrow y = 0$, target $0$. **Correct, no update.**

**Epoch 2, Sample 4:** $u = (1)(1) + (0)(1) + (-2) = -1 \Rightarrow y = 0$, target $1$. Error $e = +1$. Update:

$$w_1 \leftarrow 1 + (1)(+1)(1) = 2, \quad w_2 \leftarrow 0 + (1)(+1)(1) = 1, \quad b \leftarrow -2 + 1 = -1$$

**After Epoch 2:** $w_1 = 2, w_2 = 1, b = -1$. Decision boundary: $2x_1 + x_2 - 1 = 0$, i.e., $x_2 = 1 - 2x_1$.

**Verification at convergence:** This process continues for several epochs. The final converged weights are typically $w_1 = 1, w_2 = 1, b = -1.5$ (or any positive scalar multiple thereof), yielding the decision boundary $x_1 + x_2 = 1.5$. This correctly classifies all four AND-gate samples:
- $(0, 0) \Rightarrow u = -1.5 \Rightarrow y = 0$ ✓
- $(0, 1) \Rightarrow u = -0.5 \Rightarrow y = 0$ ✓
- $(1, 0) \Rightarrow u = -0.5 \Rightarrow y = 0$ ✓
- $(1, 1) \Rightarrow u = +0.5 \Rightarrow y = 1$ ✓

> [!NOTE] > **Why AND is learnable but XOR is not:** The AND function is linearly separable — there exists a line $x_1 + x_2 = 1.5$ that cleanly separates the $(0,0)$, $(0,1)$, $(1,0)$ points (label 0) from the $(1,1)$ point (label 1). The XOR function, however, has positive samples at $(0,1)$ and $(1,0)$ that are *diagonally opposite*, so no single straight line can separate them. This is the famous *XOR limitation* of Single Layer Perceptrons, which motivated the development of Multi-Layer Perceptrons with hidden layers.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Boundary Geometry

| **Property** | **Expression** | **Engineering Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|
| Hyperplane equation | $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ | Set of all points with zero net input |
| Class 1 region | $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b > 0$ | Points where perceptron fires |
| Class 0 region | $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b < 0$ | Points where perceptron stays silent |
| Normal vector | $\mathbf{w}$ | Perpendicular to the hyperplane |
| Offset from origin | $b / \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$ | How far the boundary is pushed from $\mathbf{0}$ |
| Perpendicular distance of a point | $\frac{\vert \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Geometric margin of point $i$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture of a Single Layer Perceptron (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input1["INPUT X1"] --> mul1["WEIGHT W1"]
    input2["INPUT X2"] --> mul2["WEIGHT W2"]
    inputN["INPUT XN"] --> mulN["WEIGHT WN"]
    biasIn["BIAS B"] --> add1["SUMMATION UNIT"]
    mul1 --> add1
    mul2 --> add1
    mulN --> add1
    add1 --> netInput["NET INPUT U"]
    netInput --> actFn["ACTIVATION PHI"]
    actFn --> output["OUTPUT Y"]
    targetIn["TARGET T"] --> errComp["ERROR E = T MINUS Y"]
    output --> errComp
    errComp -->|"UPDATE SIGNAL"| mul1
    errComp -->|"UPDATE SIGNAL"| mul2
    errComp -->|"UPDATE SIGNAL"| mulN
    errComp -->|"UPDATE SIGNAL"| biasIn
```

### 4.2 SLP Training Loop (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA(["START"]) --> initA["INITIALIZE WEIGHTS W AND BIAS B TO ZERO"]
    initA --> epochA["SET EPOCH COUNTER TO 1"]
    epochA --> sampleA["PICK NEXT TRAINING SAMPLE X T"]
    sampleA --> forwardA["COMPUTE U = W TRANSPOSE X PLUS B"]
    forwardA --> activateA["COMPUTE Y = PHI OF U"]
    activateA --> compareA["COMPUTE ERROR E = T MINUS Y"]
    compareA --> decisionA{"IS E EQUAL TO ZERO"}
    decisionA -->|"YES"| nextA["MOVE TO NEXT SAMPLE"]
    decisionA -->|"NO"| updateA["UPDATE W AND B USING ETA E X"]
    updateA --> nextA
    nextA --> checkA{"ALL SAMPLES DONE"}
    checkA -->|"NO"| sampleA
    checkA -->|"YES"| convergeA{"CONVERGED OR MAX EPOCHS"}
    convergeA -->|"NO"| epochA
    convergeA -->|"YES"| endA(["RETURN FINAL W AND B"])
```

### 4.3 Geometric View — Decision Boundary in 2D Feature Space

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    plane["TWO DIMENSIONAL FEATURE SPACE X1 X2"]
    plane --> class1["REGION CLASS 1: W1 X1 + W2 X2 + B GREATER THAN ZERO"]
    plane --> class0["REGION CLASS 0: W1 X1 + W2 X2 + B LESS THAN ZERO"]
    plane --> line["DECISION HYPERPLANE: W1 X1 + W2 X2 + B EQUALS ZERO"]
    line --> wVec["NORMAL VECTOR W POINTS INTO CLASS 1"]
    line --> margin["MARGIN GAMMA = DISTANCE FROM NEAREST SAMPLE TO LINE"]
```

### 4.4 Comparison Block — Classical Perceptron vs. Linear Neuron

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    perc["CLASSICAL PERCEPTRON"] --> percAct["ACTIVATION: HEAVISIDE STEP"]
    percAct --> percUpdate["UPDATE: PERCEPTRON RULE"]
    lin["LINEAR NEURON WIDROW HOFF"] --> linAct["ACTIVATION: IDENTITY"]
    linAct --> linUpdate["UPDATE: LMS DELTA RULE USING GRADIENT"]
    percUpdate --> lim1["CANNOT LEARN NON LINEAR PROBLEMS"]
    linUpdate --> lim2["MINIMIZES MEAN SQUARE ERROR CONTINUOUSLY"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
