# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MLP Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

The following diagram maps the **functional flow** of information through an MLP, including forward and backward propagation channels, parameter updates, and the loss feedback loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Vector X] --> B[Input Layer Buffer]
    B --> C[Hidden Layer 1 Linear Transform]
    C --> D[Hidden Layer 1 Activation f1]
    D --> E[Hidden Layer 2 Linear Transform]
    E --> F[Hidden Layer 2 Activation f2]
    F --> G[Output Layer Linear Transform]
    G --> H[Output Layer Activation fL]
    H --> I[Prediction Y Hat]
    I --> J[Loss Function Computation L]
    J --> K[Output Error Delta L]
    K --> L[Hidden Layer 2 Error Delta 2]
    L --> M[Hidden Layer 1 Error Delta 1]
    M --> N[Weight and Bias Gradient Computation]
    N --> O[Gradient Descent Parameter Update]
    O --> C
    O --> E
    O --> G
```

## 4.2 Layered Neuron Connectivity Schematic

This diagram illustrates a **fully-connected (dense)** topology where every neuron in one layer connects to every neuron in the next.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp1((X1)) --> h1((H1))
    inp1((X1)) --> h2((H2))
    inp1((X1)) --> h3((H3))
    inp2((X2)) --> h1((H1))
    inp2((X2)) --> h2((H2))
    inp2((X2)) --> h3((H3))
    inp3((X3)) --> h1((H1))
    inp3((X3)) --> h2((H2))
    inp3((X3)) --> h3((H3))
    h1((H1)) --> o1((Y1))
    h1((H1)) --> o2((Y2))
    h2((H2)) --> o1((Y1))
    h2((H2)) --> o2((Y2))
    h3((H3)) --> o1((Y1))
    h3((H3)) --> o2((Y2))
```

## 4.3 Training Loop — Forward / Backward Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Initialize Weights and Biases] --> s2[Forward Pass Compute Predictions]
    s2 --> s3[Compute Loss L Yhat Y]
    s3 --> s4{Did Loss Converge}
    s4 -- No --> s5[Backward Pass Compute Gradients]
    s5 --> s6[Update Weights using Gradient Descent]
    s6 --> s2
    s4 -- Yes --> s7[Final Trained MLP Model]
```

## 4.4 Activation Function Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    actRoot[Activation Function Selector] --> sig[Sigmoid Output Range 0 to 1]
    actRoot --> tanhB[Tanh Output Range Minus 1 to 1]
    actRoot --> reluB[ReLU Output Max of 0 and X]
    actRoot --> soft[Softmax Multi Class Probability Vector]
    sig --> useC[Use Case Binary Classification Output]
    tanhB --> useH[Use Case Hidden Layers Zero Centred]
    reluB --> useR[Use Case Default Hidden Layer Fast Training]
    soft --> useS[Use Case Multi Class Output Layer]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are modeled exactly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern: **Part A (3 marks each)** and **Part B (14 marks with internal choice)**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Define Multilayer Perceptron. List any two activation functions used in MLPs.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)** is a class of feedforward artificial neural network consisting of an input layer, one or more hidden layers, and an output layer, where every node (except input nodes) applies a **non-linear activation function** to its weighted sum. MLPs are trained using the **backpropagation algorithm** combined with **gradient descent**.

Two commonly used activation functions:

1. **Sigmoid:** $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$, squashes output to $(0,1)$. Used in binary classification output layers.
2. **ReLU:** $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$, the default choice for hidden layers because it mitigates the vanishing gradient problem and enables faster training.

*(1 mark for correct MLP definition, 1 mark each for the two activation functions with their formulas.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Explain the role of the backpropagation algorithm in training an MLP.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Backpropagation** is the algorithm used to efficiently compute the gradient of the loss function with respect to every weight and bias in the network. It works in two phases:
>
> 1. **Forward Pass:** Inputs propagate through the network to compute the prediction and the loss $\mathcal{L}$.
> 2. **Backward Pass:** Using the **chain rule of calculus**, the gradient $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}$ is computed layer-by-layer, starting from the output and moving toward the input. These gradients are then used to update the parameters via gradient descent.
>
> Without backpropagation, training deep networks would be computationally intractable, since naively computing each gradient would require a separate forward pass per parameter.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Module 1, Choice 1]`

**(a)** Derive the equations for forward propagation through a 2-layer MLP with sigmoid activations in the hidden layer. Clearly show the pre-activation and activation computations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the backpropagation algorithm in detail, including the derivation of the output error term, hidden layer error term, and weight gradient updates using the chain rule. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

Consider an MLP with input $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_0}$, hidden layer with $H$ neurons, and a single output neuron.

**Step 1 — Input Reception:**
$$
\mathbf{a}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x}
$$

**Step 2 — Hidden Layer Pre-activation:**
$$
\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{a}^{(0)} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)} \quad \in \mathbb{R}^{H}
$$
where $\mathbf{W}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times n_0}$ and $\mathbf{b}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{H}$.

**Step 3 — Hidden Layer Activation (Sigmoid):**
$$
\mathbf{a}^{(1)} = \sigma(\mathbf{z}^{(1)}) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-\mathbf{z}^{(1)}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Output Layer Pre-activation:**
$$
z^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{a}^{(1)} + b^{(2)} \quad \in \mathbb{R}
$$

**Step 5 — Output Activation:**
$$
\hat{y} = \sigma(z^{(2)}) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z^{(2)}}}
$$

**Step 6 — Loss (Binary Cross-Entropy):**
$$
\mathcal{L} = -\left[ y \log(\hat{y}) + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y}) \right]
$$

*(Marks distribution: 2 marks for hidden layer equations, 2 marks for output equations, 1 mark for activation formulas, 2 marks for loss expression and explanation.)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Output Layer Error Term:**
$$
\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(2)} = \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(2)}} = \hat{y} - y
$$
This elegant result holds because the derivative of the sigmoid cancels with the inverse log in cross-entropy.

**Step 2 — Hidden Layer Error Term (Chain Rule):**
$$
\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(1)} = \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(1)}} = \left(\mathbf{W}^{(2)\top} \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(2)}\right) \odot \sigma'\!\left(\mathbf{z}^{(1)}\right)
$$
where $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$.

**Step 3 — Weight Gradient for Output Layer:**
$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{W}^{(2)}} \mathcal{L} = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(2)} \mathbf{a}^{(1)\top}
$$

**Step 4 — Bias Gradient for Output Layer:**
$$
\nabla_{b^{(2)}} \mathcal{L} = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(2)}
$$

**Step 5 — Weight Gradient for Hidden Layer:**
$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{W}^{(1)}} \mathcal{L} = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(1)} \mathbf{a}^{(0)\top}
$$

**Step 6 — Bias Gradient for Hidden Layer:**
$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{b}^{(1)}} \mathcal{L} = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(1)}
$$

**Step 7 — Parameter Update via Gradient Descent:**
$$
\mathbf{W}^{(l)} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{(l)} - \eta \nabla_{\mathbf{W}^{(l)}} \mathcal{L}
$$
$$
\mathbf{b}^{(l)} \leftarrow \mathbf{b}^{(l)} - \eta \nabla_{\mathbf{b}^{(l)}} \mathcal{L}
$$

**Justification:** Backpropagation efficiently reuses intermediate computations by the chain rule, making gradient computation scale as $O(\text{Parameters})$ rather than $O(\text{Parameters}^{2})$.

*(Marks distribution: 2 marks for output error, 2 marks for hidden error, 2 marks for gradient expressions, 1 mark for the update rule.)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Module 1, Choice 2]`

**(a)** Discuss the vanishing and exploding gradient problems in MLPs. Explain how activation function choice and weight initialization mitigate these issues. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider an MLP with one hidden layer of 2 neurons, sigmoid activation, and a single linear output. Input $\mathbf{x} = [1, 1]^{\top}$, target $y = 1$. Initial weights $w^{(1)}_{11} = 0.4, w^{(1)}_{12} = 0.3, w^{(1)}_{21} = 0.2, w^{(1)}_{22} = 0.1$, $b^{(1)}_1 = 0.5, b^{(1)}_2 = 0.6, w^{(2)}_1 = 0.7, w^{(2)}_2 = 0.8, b^{(2)} = 0.5$. Learning rate $\eta = 0.1$. Perform **one iteration** of forward and backward pass using MSE loss. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Vanishing Gradient Problem:**
In deep MLPs with sigmoid/tanh activations, repeated multiplication of small derivatives (sigmoid derivative peaks at $0.25$, tanh at $\approx 0.42$) across layers causes the gradient to shrink exponentially. As a result, weights in early layers update extremely slowly, and the network fails to converge.

**Exploding Gradient Problem:**
Conversely, when weight magnitudes are large, repeated multiplication of derivatives and weights across layers causes the gradient to grow exponentially, leading to unstable weight updates and numerical overflow (NaN losses).

**Mitigations:**

| Issue | Mitigation Strategy |
|-------|---------------------|
| Vanishing gradient | Use **ReLU** activation (derivative = 1 for $z > 0$) |
| Vanishing gradient | **Batch Normalization** to stabilize layer inputs |
| Exploding gradient | **Gradient Clipping** by norm: $\mathbf{g} \leftarrow \mathbf{g} \cdot \min\left(1, \dfrac{\theta}{\vert\vert\mathbf{g}\vert\vert_2}\right)$ |
| Both | **Proper Initialization:** **He init** for ReLU: $\text{Var}(W) = 2/n_{\text{in}}$; **Xavier init** for sigmoid/tanh: $\text{Var}(W) = 2/(n_{\text{in}} + n_{\text{out}})$ |
| Both | Use **Residual connections** (skip connections) |

*(Marks distribution: 2 marks vanishing, 2 marks exploding, 3 marks mitigation strategies.)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Forward Pass:**

**Hidden neuron 1:**
$$
z^{(1)}_1 = (0.4)(1) + (0.3)(1) + 0.5 = 1.2
$$
$$
a^{(1)}_1 = \sigma(1.2) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-1.2}} = 0.7685
$$

**Hidden neuron 2:**
$$
z^{(1)}_2 = (0.2)(1) + (0.1)(1) + 0.6 = 0.9
$$
$$
a^{(1)}_2 = \sigma(0.9) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-0.9}} = 0.7109
$$

**Output:**
$$
z^{(2)} = (0.7)(0.7685) + (0.8)(0.7109) + 0.5 = 0.5380 + 0.5687 + 0.5 = 1.6067
$$
$$
\hat{y} = \sigma(1.6067) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-1.6067}} = 0.8330
$$

**Loss (MSE):**
$$
\mathcal{L} = (\hat{y} - y)^{2} = (0.8330 - 1)^{2} = (-0.1670)^{2} = 0.0279
$$

**Backward Pass:**

**Output gradient (sigmoid + MSE combined):**
$$
\delta^{(2)} = 2(\hat{y} - y) \cdot \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y}) = 2(-0.1670)(0.8330)(0.1670) = -0.0465
$$

**Hidden gradients:**
$$
\delta^{(1)}_1 = \delta^{(2)} \cdot w^{(2)}_1 \cdot a^{(1)}_1 (1 - a^{(1)}_1) = (-0.0465)(0.7)(0.7685)(0.2315) = -0.00579
$$
$$
\delta^{(1)}_2 = \delta^{(2)} \cdot w^{(2)}_2 \cdot a^{(1)}_2 (1 - a^{(1)}_2) = (-0.0465)(0.8)(0.7109)(0.2891) = -0.00764
$$

**Parameter Gradients:**
$$
\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w^{(2)}_1} = \delta^{(2)} a^{(1)}_1 = (-0.0465)(0.7685) = -0.0357
$$
$$
\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w^{(2)}_2} = \delta^{(2)} a^{(1)}_2 = (-0.0465)(0.7109) = -0.0331
$$
$$
\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(2)}} = \delta^{(2)} = -0.0465
$$
$$
\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w^{(1)}_{11}} = \delta^{(1)}_1 \cdot x_1 = -0.00579
$$
$$
\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w^{(1)}_{12}} = \delta^{(1)}_1 \cdot x_2 = -0.00579
$$
$$
\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w^{(1)}_{21}} = \delta^{(1)}_2 \cdot x_1 = -0.00764
$$
$$
\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w^{(1)}_{22}} = \delta^{(1)}_2 \cdot x_2 = -0.00764
$$
$$
\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(1)}_1} = -0.00579, \quad \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(1)}_2} = -0.00764
$$

**Weight Updates ($\eta = 0.1$):**
$$
w^{(2)}_1 \leftarrow 0.7 - 0.1(-0.0357) = 0.7036
$$
$$
w^{(2)}_2 \leftarrow 0.8 - 0.1(-0.0331) = 0.8033
$$
$$
b^{(2)} \leftarrow 0.5 - 0.1(-0.0465) = 0.5047
$$
$$
w^{(1)}_{11} \leftarrow 0.4 - 0.1(-0.00579) = 0.4006
$$
$$
w^{(1)}_{12} \leftarrow 0.3 - 0.1(-0.00579) = 0.3006
$$
$$
w^{(1)}_{21} \leftarrow 0.2 - 0.1(-0.00764) = 0.2008
$$
$$
w^{(1)}_{22} \leftarrow 0.1 - 0.1(-0.00764) = 0.1008
$$
$$
b^{(1)}_1 \leftarrow 0.5 - 0.1(-0.00579) = 0.5006
$$
$$
b^{(1)}_2 \leftarrow 0.6 - 0.1(-0.00764) = 0.6008
$$

*(Marks distribution: 2 marks forward pass, 2 marks output backward, 2 marks hidden backward, 1 mark final updates.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the bias gradient is just the error term:** Many students incorrectly write $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial b = \delta \cdot a$. The correct answer is $\delta$.
> 2. **Confusing pre-activation $\mathbf{z}$ with activation $\mathbf{a}$:** Activation applies the non-linearity. Examiners specifically test whether you call $z^{(l)} = W a^{(l-1)} + b$ and $a^{(l)} = f(z^{(l)})$ separately.
> 3. **Wrong sigmoid derivative:** Writing $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z) - 1$ instead of $\sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$.
> 4. **Ignoring the chain rule:** Simply writing $\nabla \mathcal{L} = \hat{y} - y$ without showing the intermediate steps $\nabla_{\hat y} \mathcal{L} \odot f'(z^{(L)})$ loses partial credit.
> 5. **Not specifying element-wise multiplication ($\odot$):** When deriving hidden error, always write $\odot$ explicitly to indicate Hadamard product.
> 6. **Numerical computation errors:** In numerical problems, rounding sigmoid outputs to 2 decimals loses 0.5–1 mark. Maintain at least 4 decimal precision.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Multilayer Perceptron:**

- **Definition:** A feedforward neural network with $\geq 3$ layers using non-linear activations.
- **Architecture:** Input → Hidden Layer(s) → Output, with full connectivity between adjacent layers.
- **Key Building Blocks:** Weighted sum $\rightarrow$ Activation function $\rightarrow$ Non-linearity.
- **Forward Propagation:** Computes $z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}$ then $a^{(l)} = f(z^{(l)})$.
- **Loss Functions:** MSE for regression, Cross-Entropy for classification.
- **Backpropagation:** Applies the chain rule to compute gradients layer-by-layer in reverse order.
- **Output Error:** $\delta^{(L)} = \nabla_{\hat y} \mathcal{L} \odot f'(z^{(L)})$; for softmax+CE: $\delta^{(L)} = \hat y - y$.
- **Hidden Error:** $\delta^{(l)} = (W^{(l+1)\top} \delta^{(l+1)}) \odot f'(z^{(l)})$.
- **Weight Gradient:** $\nabla_{W^{(l)}} \mathcal{L} = \delta^{(l)} a^{(l-1)\top}$ (outer product).
- **Bias Gradient:** $\nabla_{b^{(l)}} \mathcal{L} = \delta^{(l)}$.
- **Update Rule:** $W \leftarrow W - \eta \nabla_W \mathcal{L}$.
- **Activations:** Sigmoid (binary output), Softmax (multi-class), ReLU (hidden default), Tanh (zero-centred hidden).
- **Universal Approximation Theorem:** One hidden layer of finite width can approximate any continuous function.
- **Vanishing/Exploding Gradients:** Mitigate with **ReLU**, **He/Xavier initialization**, **BatchNorm**, **gradient clipping**.
- **Learning Rate $\eta$:** Critical hyperparameter; too large → divergence, too small → slow convergence.
- **Training Loop:** Initialize → Forward Pass → Loss → Backward Pass → Update → Repeat for epochs.
- **Common Mistake:** Treating MLP as a linear model — always include the non-linear activation!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Back-Propagation Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT503 - DEEP LEARNING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Back-Propagation Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Back-Propagation (Backprop) Algorithm** is a supervised learning algorithm used to train **Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs)** and **Multilayer Perceptrons (MLPs)**. It efficiently computes the **gradient of the loss function** with respect to each weight in the network by applying the **chain rule of calculus** through a two-phase process: a **Forward Pass** (to compute activations and the loss) and a **Backward Pass** (to propagate the error gradients from the output layer back to the input layer).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned, Outcome-Based Education), Back-Propagation is positioned as the **computational workhorse** of deep learning, enabling gradient-based optimization (SGD, Adam, RMSProp) over networks with millions of parameters.

### 1.2 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Formal Definition | Role in Backprop |
|------|------------------|------------------|
| **Forward Pass** | Propagation of inputs through the network to produce outputs | Computes $a^{(l)} = f(z^{(l)})$ for each layer $l$ |
| **Backward Pass** | Reverse traversal to compute gradients of loss w.r.t. weights | Computes $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(l)}}$ and $\frac{\partial L}{\partial b^{(l)}}$ |
| **Loss Function** | Quantifies the error between predicted and true outputs | Standard: MSE for regression, Cross-Entropy for classification |
| **Activation Function** | Non-linear transformation applied at each neuron | Sigmoid, Tanh, ReLU, Softmax |
| **Learning Rate ($\eta$)** | Step size hyperparameter for weight updates | Typically $\eta \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-1}]$ |
| **Epoch** | One complete pass through the entire training dataset | Repeats until convergence |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Back-Propagation is a *core* topic in Module 1 because it bridges perceptron theory (Module 1, earlier sections) with practical deep learning training. Expect derivations and numerical examples in ESE (End Semester Examination).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blame Game" 🎯

Imagine a football team (your neural network) that loses a match (high loss). The coach (backprop) doesn't just say "we lost" — instead, he walks **backward** through the team:

1. **Identify the goalie's mistake** (output layer error).
2. **Trace which defenders failed to clear the ball** (previous layer).
3. **Find which midfielders made the wrong pass** (earlier layer).
4. **Finally, recognize which forwards made the initial bad shot** (input layer).

Each player (weight) is then assigned a **proportion of the blame** (gradient) and adjusts their training (weight update) accordingly. This is exactly what backpropagation does — it distributes the responsibility for error across all weights proportionally to their contribution.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Summary:** Backprop = "Figure out who messed up, by how much, going backward from the answer."

### 1.4 Historical Context

- **1970s:** Linnainmaa first published the reverse-mode automatic differentiation idea.
- **1986:** Rumelhart, Hinton & Williams popularized backpropagation for neural networks in their seminal *Nature* paper.
- **2006 onwards:** Re-emerged as the engine of "Deep Learning" with Hinton's deep belief networks, eventually leading to AlexNet (2012).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Setup & Notation

Consider a feedforward neural network with $L$ layers. For layer $l$ (where $l = 1, 2, \ldots, L$):

- $n^{[l]}$: Number of neurons in layer $l$
- $W^{[l]}$: Weight matrix of shape $(n^{[l]}, n^{[l-1]})$
- $b^{[l]}$: Bias vector of shape $(n^{[l]}, 1)$
- $z^{[l]}$: Pre-activation (linear combination) — $z^{[l]} = W^{[l]} a^{[l-1]} + b^{[l]}$
- $a^{[l]}$: Post-activation output — $a^{[l]} = g^{[l]}(z^{[l]})$
- $g^{[l]}$: Activation function at layer $l$
- $y$: Ground truth label
- $\hat{y} = a^{[L]}$: Network prediction
- $L(y, \hat{y})$: Loss function

### 2.2 The Two-Phase Process — Operational Logic

#### Phase 1: Forward Pass (Left → Right)

The forward pass computes activations layer by layer, caching intermediate values for use in the backward pass.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Initialize** input: $a^{[0]} = X$ (input matrix).
2. **Linear transformation** at layer $l$:
   $$z^{[l]} = W^{[l]} a^{[l-1]} + b^{[l]}$$
3. **Non-linear activation** at layer $l$:
   $$a^{[l]} = g^{[l]}(z^{[l]})$$
4. **Compute loss** at the output layer:
   $$L = \mathcal{L}(a^{[L]}, y)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why cache?** We must store $z^{[l]}$ and $a^{[l-1]}$ because the backward pass needs them to compute derivatives via the chain rule.

#### Phase 2: Backward Pass (Right → Left)

The backward pass propagates the error gradient backward, layer by layer.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Initialize** output error (Layer $L$):
   $$d a^{[L]} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{[L]}}$$
2. **Compute output layer gradient**:
   $$d z^{[L]} = d a^{[L]} \odot g^{[L]'}(z^{[L]})$$
3. **Compute weight gradient** at layer $l$:
   $$d W^{[l]} = \frac{1}{m} d z^{[l]} (a^{[l-1]})^T$$
4. **Compute bias gradient** at layer $l$:
   $$d b^{[l]} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} d z^{[l](i)}$$
5. **Propagate to previous layer**:
   $$d a^{[l-1]} = (W^{[l]})^T d z^{[l]}$$
6. **Loop back to step 2** for layer $l-1$.

### 2.3 The "Four Key Equations" of Backpropagation

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Favorite:** Memorize these four equations — they appear in nearly every ESE question on backprop.

For layer $l$ (final layer $L$ treated specially):

| # | Equation | Name | Purpose |
|---|----------|------|---------|
| 1 | $dz^{[L]} = \hat{y} - y$ | Output error (cross-entropy + softmax) | Starts the backward pass |
| 2 | $dW^{[l]} = \frac{1}{m} dz^{[l]} (a^{[l-1]})^T$ | Weight gradient | Direction to update $W$ |
| 3 | $db^{[l]} = \frac{1}{m} \text{np.sum}(dz^{[l]}, \text{axis}=1, \text{keepdims=True})$ | Bias gradient | Direction to update $b$ |
| 4 | $da^{[l-1]} = (W^{[l]})^T dz^{[l]}$ | Previous layer activation error | Propagates error backward |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Definition |
|--------|---------|---------------------|
| $z^{[l]}$ | Pre-activation at layer $l$ | $z^{[l]} = W^{[l]} a^{[l-1]} + b^{[l]}$ |
| $a^{[l]}$ | Post-activation at layer $l$ | $a^{[l]} = g^{[l]}(z^{[l]})$ |
| $\sigma'(z)$ | Sigmoid derivative | $\sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ |
| $\tanh'(z)$ | Tanh derivative | $1 - \tanh^2(z)$ |
| $\text{ReLU}'(z)$ | ReLU derivative | $1$ if $z > 0$, else $0$ |
| $d z^{[l]}$ | Error at layer $l$ | $d a^{[l]} \odot g^{[l]'}(z^{[l]})$ |
| $d W^{[l]}$ | Weight gradient | $\frac{1}{m} d z^{[l]} (a^{[l-1]})^T$ |
| $d b^{[l]}$ | Bias gradient | $\frac{1}{m} \sum_i d z^{[l](i)}$ |
| $d a^{[l-1]}$ | Previous activation gradient | $(W^{[l]})^T d z^{[l]}$ |
| $W^{[l]} := W^{[l]} - \eta d W^{[l]}$ | Weight update rule | Gradient descent |
| $b^{[l]} := b^{[l]} - \eta d b^{[l]}$ | Bias update rule | Gradient descent |
| $\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}}$ | Mean Squared Error | $\frac{1}{m} \sum_i (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ |
| $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}$ | Cross-Entropy Loss | $-\frac{1}{m} \sum_i y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ |
| $\eta$ | Learning rate | $\eta \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-1}]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions:** For sigmoid output + MSE loss: $dz^{[L]} = (\hat{y} - y) \odot \sigma'(z^{[L]})$. For softmax + cross-entropy: $dz^{[L]} = \hat{y} - y$ (cleaner form).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Computer Vision:** Training CNNs for object detection (YOLO, ResNet)
- **Natural Language Processing:** Training Transformers (BERT, GPT) for translation
- **Speech Recognition:** Acoustic modeling in systems like DeepSpeech
- **Medical Diagnosis:** Predicting diseases from X-ray / MRI data
- **Autonomous Driving:** End-to-end policy networks in self-driving cars
- **Financial Forecasting:** Stock price and credit risk prediction

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Chain Rule Foundation

**Goal:** Show that backpropagation is mathematically equivalent to repeated application of the multivariable chain rule.

Consider a 2-layer network. Loss depends on $a^{[2]}$, which depends on $z^{[2]}$, which depends on $W^{[2]}, b^{[2]}, a^{[1]}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L} &= \mathcal{L}(a^{[2]}, y) \\
a^{[2]} &= g^{[2]}(z^{[2]}) \\
z^{[2]} &= W^{[2]} a^{[1]} + b^{[2]} \\
a^{[1]} &= g^{[1]}(z^{[1]}) \\
z^{[1]} &= W^{[1]} X + b^{[1]}
\end{aligned}
$$

We want $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{[2]}}$. By the chain rule:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{[2]}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{[2]}} \cdot \frac{\partial a^{[2]}}{\partial z^{[2]}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{[2]}}{\partial W^{[2]}}
$$

Now compute each piece:

- $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{[2]}} = \frac{\partial}{\partial a^{[2]}} \left[ -(y \log a^{[2]} + (1-y)\log(1-a^{[2]})) \right] = -\frac{y}{a^{[2]}} + \frac{1-y}{1-a^{[2]}}$

- $\frac{\partial a^{[2]}}{\partial z^{[2]}} = g^{[2]'}(z^{[2]}) = a^{[2]}(1 - a^{[2]})$ (sigmoid)

Multiplying the first two:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{[2]}} = -\frac{y}{a^{[2]}} \cdot a^{[2]}(1 - a^{[2]}) + \frac{1-y}{1-a^{[2]}} \cdot a^{[2]}(1 - a^{[2]})
$$

$$
= -y(1 - a^{[2]}) + (1-y)a^{[2]} = -y + y a^{[2]} + a^{[2]} - y a^{[2]} = a^{[2]} - y
$$

Therefore:

$$
dz^{[2]} = a^{[2]} - y
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** The famous "$\hat{y} - y$" formula for the output layer emerges naturally from the chain rule when using sigmoid + cross-entropy. This is a common 14-mark KTU question.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: General Weight Gradient Formula

For a generic layer $l$, the weight matrix $W^{[l]}$ has entries $w_{ij}^{[l]}$ (weight from neuron $j$ in layer $l-1$ to neuron $i$ in layer $l$).

**Forward:** $z_i^{[l]} = \sum_j w_{ij}^{[l]} a_j^{[l-1]} + b_i^{[l]}$

**Gradient:**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_{ij}^{[l]}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i^{[l]}} \cdot \frac{\partial z_i^{[l]}}{\partial w_{ij}^{[l]}} = dz_i^{[l]} \cdot a_j^{[l-1]}
$$

In matrix form, for $m$ training examples (vectorized):

$$
dW^{[l]} = \frac{1}{m} dZ^{[l]} (A^{[l-1]})^T
$$

where $dZ^{[l]}$ is the matrix of $dz^{[l](i)}$ stacked column-wise for all $m$ examples, and $A^{[l-1]}$ is the activation matrix.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Bias Gradient

Since $\frac{\partial z_i^{[l]}}{\partial b_i^{[l]}} = 1$, we get $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b_i^{[l]}} = dz_i^{[l]}$.

Vectorized with $m$ examples:

$$
db^{[l]} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} dz^{[l](i)} = \frac{1}{m} \text{np.sum}(dZ^{[l]}, \text{axis}=1, \text{keepdims=True})
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Backpropagating to Previous Layer

We need $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a_j^{[l-1]}}$ to continue the backward pass:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a_j^{[l-1]}} = \sum_i \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i^{[l]}} \cdot \frac{\partial z_i^{[l]}}{\partial a_j^{[l-1]}} = \sum_i dz_i^{[l]} \cdot w_{ij}^{[l]}
$$

In matrix form:

$$
da^{[l-1]} = (W^{[l]})^T dz^{[l]}
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Forward and Backward Computations

| Layer | Forward Equations | Backward Equations |
|-------|-------------------|--------------------|
| Layer 1 | $Z^{[1]} = W^{[1]} X + b^{[1]}$ | $dW^{[1]} = \frac{1}{m} dZ^{[1]} X^T$ |
|       | $A^{[1]} = g^{[1]}(Z^{[1]})$ | $db^{[1]} = \frac{1}{m} \text{sum}(dZ^{[1]})$ |
|       |                   | $dA^{[0]} = (W^{[1]})^T dZ^{[1]}$ |
| Layer 2 | $Z^{[2]} = W^{[2]} A^{[1]} + b^{[2]}$ | $dW^{[2]} = \frac{1}{m} dZ^{[2]} (A^{[1]})^T$ |
|       | $A^{[2]} = g^{[2]}(Z^{[2]})$ | $db^{[2]} = \frac{1}{m} \text{sum}(dZ^{[2]})$ |
|       |                   | $dA^{[1]} = (W^{[2]})^T dZ^{[2]}$ |
| Output (L) | Compute $\mathcal{L}(A^{[L]}, Y)$ | $dZ^{[L]} = A^{[L]} - Y$ (with sigmoid + BCE) |
|       |                   | $dW^{[L]} = \frac{1}{m} dZ^{[L]} (A^{[L-1]})^T$ |
|       |                   | $db^{[L]} = \frac{1}{m} \text{sum}(dZ^{[L]})$ |

### 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** A 2-2-1 network (2 inputs, 2 hidden, 1 output) with sigmoid activation, MSE loss, and a single training example $(x_1, x_2) = (0.5, 1.0)$, $y = 1$.

**Given (random initialization):**
- $W^{[1]} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0.2 \\ 0.3 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix}$, $b^{[1]} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$
- $W^{[2]} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$, $b^{[2]} = [0.3]$
- Learning rate $\eta = 0.5$

#### Step 1: Forward Pass

$$
z_1^{[1]} = 0.1(0.5) + 0.2(1.0) + 0.1 = 0.05 + 0.2 + 0.1 = 0.35
$$

$$
z_2^{[1]} = 0.3(0.5) + 0.4(1.0) + 0.2 = 0.15 + 0.4 + 0.2 = 0.75
$$

Apply sigmoid $g(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$:

$$
a_1^{[1]} = \sigma(0.35) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.35}} = \frac{1}{1.7047} \approx 0.5866
$$

$$
a_2^{[1]} = \sigma(0.75) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.75}} = \frac{1}{2.1170} \approx 0.4724
$$

Output layer:

$$
z_1^{[2]} = 0.5(0.5866) + 0.6(0.4724) + 0.3 = 0.2933 + 0.2834 + 0.3 = 0.8767
$$

$$
a_1^{[2]} = \hat{y} = \sigma(0.8767) \approx 0.7061
$$

Loss (MSE for single example):

$$
\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2 = \frac{1}{2}(1 - 0.7061)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.2939)^2 \approx 0.0432
$$

#### Step 2: Backward Pass

**Output layer gradients:**

$$
dz_1^{[2]} = \hat{y} - y = 0.7061 - 1 = -0.2939
$$

$$
dW^{[2]} = dz_1^{[2]} \cdot (a^{[1]})^T = -0.2939 \begin{bmatrix} 0.5866 & 0.4724 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.1724 & -0.1388 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
db^{[2]} = -0.2939
$$

**Hidden layer gradients:**

$$
da^{[1]} = (W^{[2]})^T dz^{[2]} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.6 \end{bmatrix} (-0.2939) = \begin{bmatrix} -0.1470 \\ -0.1763 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Sigmoid derivative: $g'(z) = a(1 - a)$:

$$
g'(z_1^{[1]}) = 0.5866(1 - 0.5866) = 0.2425
$$

$$
g'(z_2^{[1]}) = 0.4724(1 - 0.4724) = 0.2493
$$

$$
dz^{[1]} = da^{[1]} \odot g'(z^{[1]}) = \begin{bmatrix} -0.1470 \times 0.2425 \\ -0.1763 \times 0.2493 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.0356 \\ -0.0440 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
dW^{[1]} = dz^{[1]} (a^{[0]})^T = \begin{bmatrix} -0.0356 \\ -0.0440 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 1.0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.0178 & -0.0356 \\ -0.0220 & -0.0440 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
db^{[1]} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.0356 \\ -0.0440 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Step 3: Weight Update (Gradient Descent)

$$
W^{[2]}_{new} = W^{[2]} - \eta \cdot dW^{[2]} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix} - 0.5 \begin{bmatrix} -0.1724 & -0.1388 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5862 & 0.6694 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
b^{[2]}_{new} = 0.3 - 0.5(-0.2939) = 0.3 + 0.1470 = 0.4470
$$

$$
W^{[1]}_{new} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0.2 \\ 0.3 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix} - 0.5 \begin{bmatrix} -0.0178 & -0.0356 \\ -0.0220 & -0.0440 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1089 & 0.2178 \\ 0.3110 & 0.4220 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
b^{[1]}_{new} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix} - 0.5 \begin{bmatrix} -0.0356 \\ -0.0440 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1178 \\ 0.2220 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification:** After this update, repeating the forward pass would yield a smaller loss, confirming the gradient direction is correct. This is the essence of one iteration of backprop training.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Back-Propagation Algorithm (High-Level Control Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize Weights W, Biases b] --> B[Set Learning Rate eta]
    B --> C[For each epoch]
    C --> D[Shuffle Training Data]
    D --> E[For each mini-batch]
    E --> F[FORWARD PASS: Input X = a0]
    F --> G[For each layer l: Compute z = W a + b]
    G --> H[Compute a = g of z]
    H --> I[At output layer: Compute Loss L]
    I --> J[BACKWARD PASS: Initialize dZ at output]
    J --> K[For each layer l from L to 1]
    K --> L[Compute dW = dZ times a_prev Transpose]
    L --> M[Compute db = sum of dZ]
    M --> N[Compute da_prev = W Transpose times dZ]
    N --> O[Compute dZ = da_prev element-wise g prime of z]
    O --> P{More layers?}
    P -- Yes --> K
    P -- No --> Q[Update W = W minus eta times dW]
    Q --> R[Update b = b minus eta times db]
    R --> S{Convergence reached?}
    S -- No --> C
    S -- Yes --> T[End: Trained Network]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Forward and Backward Pass

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        A0[x1]
        A1[x2]
    end

    subgraph HIDDEN[Hidden Layer]
        W1[W1 Matrix]
        B1[B1 Bias]
        Z1[Z1 Linear Sum]
        G1[g Activation]
        A1H[a1 Hidden Output]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        W2[W2 Matrix]
        B2[B2 Bias]
        Z2[Z2 Linear Sum]
        G2[g Activation]
        A2H[a2 Prediction]
    end

    subgraph LOSS[Loss Computation]
        YC[True Label y]
        LFN[Loss Function L]
    end

    subgraph BACKWARD[Backward Pass]
        DZ2[Error at Output dZ2]
        DZ1[Error at Hidden dZ1]
        DW2[Gradient dW2]
        DW1[Gradient dW1]
        DB2[Gradient dB2]
        DB1[Gradient dB1]
        UPDATE[Weight Update Rule]
    end

    A0 --> W1
    A1 --> W1
    W1 --> Z1
    B1 --> Z1
    Z1 --> G1
    G1 --> A1H
    A1H --> W2
    W2 --> Z2
    B2 --> Z2
    Z2 --> G2
    G2 --> A2H
    A2H --> LFN
    YC --> LFN
    LFN --> DZ2
    DZ2 --> DW2
    DZ2 --> DB2
    DZ2 --> DZ1
    DZ1 --> DW1
    DZ1 --> DB1
    DW2 --> UPDATE
    DW1 --> UPDATE
    DB2 --> UPDATE
    DB1 --> UPDATE
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Information Flow Direction (Forward vs Backward)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FPASS[Forward Pass Direction Left to Right]
        F1[Input] --> F2[Linear]
        F2 --> F3[Activation]
        F3 --> F4[Linear]
        F4 --> F5[Activation Output]
        F5 --> F6[Loss]
    end

    subgraph BPASS[Backward Pass Direction Right to Left]
        B6[Loss Gradient] --> B5[Output Error]
        B5 --> B4[Hidden Error]
        B4 --> B3[Input Gradient]
        B3 --> B2[Weight Updates]
        B2 --> B1[Bias Updates]
    end

    F6 -.triggers.-> B6
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Block — Computational Graph View

A computation graph unrolls the network operations into a DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph), where:
- **Nodes** are operations (linear, activation, loss).
- **Edges** are tensors flowing between operations.

The **forward pass** traverses the graph top-down, while the **backward pass** traverses bottom-up, computing local gradients at each node using the chain rule.

> [!TIP]
> **PyTorch / TensorFlow Connection:** Modern deep learning frameworks implement backprop as **reverse-mode automatic differentiation** on these computation graphs. The "autograd" engine in PyTorch and the "GradientTape" in TensorFlow do exactly what we derived above — automatically.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the Back-Propagation Algorithm. List its two main phases.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Back-Propagation Algorithm** is a supervised learning algorithm used to train multi-layer feedforward neural networks. It computes the gradient of the loss function with respect to each weight using the chain rule of calculus.
>
> **Two Main Phases:**
> 1. **Forward Pass:** Inputs are propagated layer-by-layer to compute the network output and the loss $\mathcal{L}$.
> 2. **Backward Pass:** The gradient of the loss is propagated from the output layer back to the input layer, computing $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{[l]}}$ and $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{[l]}}$ for every layer.
>
> Finally, weights are updated using: $W^{[l]} := W^{[l]} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{[l]}}$

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Why is the sigmoid derivative expressed as $g'(z) = g(z)(1 - g(z))$ important in backpropagation?

**Model Answer:**

> The expression $g'(z) = g(z)(1 - g(z))$ for the sigmoid function is important because it allows the gradient computation in the backward pass to be simplified to use only the already-computed activation $a = g(z)$, without needing to recompute the sigmoid from scratch. This optimization reduces computational cost. Furthermore, the derivative is **bounded in $(0, 0.25]$**, which means in deep networks gradients get multiplied repeatedly by values $\le 0.25$, causing the **vanishing gradient problem** — a key reason modern networks prefer ReLU.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Derive the four fundamental equations of the Back-Propagation Algorithm for a feedforward neural network. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the role of the learning rate $\eta$ and discuss what happens when $\eta$ is too small or too large. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation of the Four Fundamental Equations:**

Assume a network with $L$ layers, using gradient descent on loss $\mathcal{L}$.

**Equation 1 — Output layer error (assuming sigmoid + cross-entropy):**

Starting from the chain rule for the last layer:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i^{[L]}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a_i^{[L]}} \cdot \frac{\partial a_i^{[L]}}{\partial z_i^{[L]}}
$$

For cross-entropy loss: $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_i [y_i \log a_i^{[L]}]$, so $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a_i^{[L]}} = -\frac{y_i}{a_i^{[L]}}$.

For sigmoid: $\frac{\partial a_i^{[L]}}{\partial z_i^{[L]}} = a_i^{[L]}(1 - a_i^{[L]})$.

Multiplying:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i^{[L]}} = -\frac{y_i}{a_i^{[L]}} \cdot a_i^{[L]}(1 - a_i^{[L]}) = a_i^{[L]} - y_i
$$

Therefore: $\boxed{dZ^{[L]} = A^{[L]} - Y}$

**Equation 2 — Weight gradient at layer $l$:**

$$
dW^{[l]} = \frac{1}{m} dZ^{[l]} (A^{[l-1]})^T
$$

This comes from $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_{ij}^{[l]}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i^{[l]}} \cdot a_j^{[l-1]}$, summed over $m$ examples.

**Equation 3 — Bias gradient at layer $l$:**

$$
db^{[l]} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} dz^{[l](i)} = \frac{1}{m} \text{np.sum}(dZ^{[l]}, \text{axis}=1, \text{keepdims=True})
$$

**Equation 4 — Propagating error to previous layer:**

$$
dA^{[l-1]} = (W^{[l]})^T dZ^{[l]}
$$

These four equations, applied iteratively from $l = L$ down to $l = 1$, constitute backpropagation.

**(b) Role of Learning Rate $\eta$:**

The learning rate $\eta$ is a hyperparameter that controls the **step size** of weight updates:

$$
W_{new} = W - \eta \cdot dW
$$

- **If $\eta$ is too small:** Convergence is extremely slow, requiring many epochs. Training may get stuck in local minima or plateau regions. Computational cost is high.
- **If $\eta$ is too large:** The optimizer may **overshoot** minima, causing the loss to oscillate or diverge. The network may fail to converge at all (loss → $\infty$).

**Optimal Strategy:** Use learning rate schedules (decay, warmup), adaptive optimizers (Adam, RMSProp), or learning rate finder tools. Typical KTU-approved values: $\eta = 0.1$ for shallow networks, $\eta = 0.001$ for deep networks.

---

> **Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Consider a 2-2-1 neural network with sigmoid activation and MSE loss. Given input $(0.5, 1.0)$, target $y = 1$, and weights:
> $W^{[1]} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0.2 \\ 0.3 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix}$, $b^{[1]} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$, $W^{[2]} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$, $b^{[2]} = [0.3]$, with $\eta = 0.5$.
> Perform **one complete iteration of forward and backward propagation** and update the weights. **(8 marks)**
>
> **(b)** What is the **vanishing gradient problem**? How does ReLU activation help mitigate it? **(6 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Numerical Computation:**

**Forward Pass:**

Step 1: Hidden layer pre-activations:

$$
z_1^{[1]} = 0.1(0.5) + 0.2(1.0) + 0.1 = 0.35
$$

$$
z_2^{[1]} = 0.3(0.5) + 0.4(1.0) + 0.2 = 0.75
$$

Step 2: Hidden layer activations (sigmoid):

$$
a_1^{[1]} = \sigma(0.35) \approx 0.5866, \quad a_2^{[1]} = \sigma(0.75) \approx 0.4724
$$

Step 3: Output pre-activation:

$$
z_1^{[2]} = 0.5(0.5866) + 0.6(0.4724) + 0.3 = 0.8767
$$

Step 4: Output activation (prediction):

$$
\hat{y} = a_1^{[2]} = \sigma(0.8767) \approx 0.7061
$$

Step 5: Loss:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(1 - 0.7061)^2 \approx 0.0432
$$

**Backward Pass:**

Output error:

$$
dz_1^{[2]} = \hat{y} - y = -0.2939
$$

Weight & bias gradients at output:

$$
dW^{[2]} = -0.2939 \begin{bmatrix} 0.5866 & 0.4724 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.1724 & -0.1388 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
db^{[2]} = -0.2939
$$

Hidden layer error:

$$
da^{[1]} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.6 \end{bmatrix} (-0.2939) = \begin{bmatrix} -0.1470 \\ -0.1763 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
g'(z_1^{[1]}) = 0.5866 \times 0.4134 = 0.2425
$$

$$
g'(z_2^{[1]}) = 0.4724 \times 0.5276 = 0.2493
$$

$$
dz^{[1]} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.0356 \\ -0.0440 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Hidden weight & bias gradients:

$$
dW^{[1]} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.0178 & -0.0356 \\ -0.0220 & -0.0440 \end{bmatrix}, \quad db^{[1]} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.0356 \\ -0.0440 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Weight Updates (with $\eta = 0.5$):**

$$
W^{[2]}_{new} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5862 & 0.6694 \end{bmatrix}, \quad b^{[2]}_{new} = 0.4470
$$

$$
W^{[1]}_{new} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1089 & 0.2178 \\ 0.3110 & 0.4220 \end{bmatrix}, \quad b^{[1]}_{new} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1178 \\ 0.2220 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**(b) Vanishing Gradient Problem:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **vanishing gradient problem** occurs when gradients in deep networks become exponentially small as they propagate backward through layers, causing early layers to learn extremely slowly (or not at all).

**Root Cause:** With sigmoid or tanh activations, derivatives are bounded: $\sigma'(z) \in (0, 0.25]$ and $\tanh'(z) \in (0, 1]$. As gradients are multiplied by these small derivatives through $L$ layers, the gradient at early layers shrinks by factors of $\le 0.25$ per layer, becoming negligible.

**How ReLU Helps:**
- $\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$, so $\text{ReLU}'(z) = 1$ for $z > 0$ and $0$ for $z \le 0$.
- The derivative is **not bounded away from 1** for active neurons, allowing gradients to flow backward without attenuation.
- For positive activations, the gradient "passes through" unchanged.
- This is why ReLU is the **default activation** in modern deep networks.

**Trade-off:** ReLU can suffer from the "dying ReLU" problem (neurons stuck at 0), which is mitigated by variants like **Leaky ReLU** and **PReLU**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $\frac{1}{m}$** in the gradient formulas — KTU examiners explicitly check this. Always average gradients over the batch.
> 2. **Using $dW^{[l]} = dZ^{[l]} \cdot A^{[l-1]}$** without transposing. The correct form is $dW^{[l]} = dZ^{[l]} (A^{[l-1]})^T$ for the matrix dimensions to match $(n^{[l]}, n^{[l-1]})$.
> 3. **Confusing the order of operations** in the backward pass — gradients must propagate from output to input, not the reverse.
> 4. **Mixing up $da^{[l]}$ and $dA^{[l]}$** — lowercase denotes a single example; uppercase denotes a stacked matrix of $m$ examples.
> 5. **Ignoring the bias gradient $db$** entirely — many students only update weights and forget biases, leading to suboptimal performance.
> 6. **Not specifying the activation function** used. The KTU rubric mandates that students state the activation function and loss function in derivations.
> 7. **Skipping intermediate steps in the chain rule derivation** — show every $\frac{\partial}{\partial}$ step explicitly. Examiners award partial credit for each correct intermediate result.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Back-Propagation Algorithm**

- ✅ **Backprop** = supervised learning algorithm to train MLPs/ANNs using the chain rule of calculus.
- ✅ **Two phases:** Forward Pass (compute activations & loss) + Backward Pass (compute gradients).
- ✅ **Forward equations:** $Z^{[l]} = W^{[l]} A^{[l-1]} + b^{[l]}$; $A^{[l]} = g^{[l]}(Z^{[l]})$.
- ✅ **Backward equations (4 key):** $dZ^{[L]} = A^{[L]} - Y$; $dW^{[l]} = \frac{1}{m} dZ^{[l]} (A^{[l-1]})^T$; $db^{[l]} = \frac{1}{m} \text{sum}(dZ^{[l]})$; $dA^{[l-1]} = (W^{[l]})^T dZ^{[l]}$.
- ✅ **Output error (sigmoid + cross-entropy):** $dz^{[L]} = \hat{y} - y$.
- ✅ **Output error (sigmoid + MSE):** $dz^{[L]} = (\hat{y} - y) \cdot \sigma'(z^{[L]})$.
- ✅ **Weight update:** $W := W - \eta \cdot dW$; $b := b - \eta \cdot db$.
- ✅ **Sigmoid derivative:** $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$.
- ✅ **Tanh derivative:** $\tanh'(z) = 1 - \tanh^2(z)$.
- ✅ **ReLU derivative:** $1$ if $z > 0$, else $0$.
- ✅ **Learning rate:** $\eta \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-1}]$; too small → slow; too large → divergence.
- ✅ **Vanishing gradient:** Caused by sigmoid/tanh derivatives $< 1$ compounded across layers; mitigated by ReLU.
- ✅ **Exploding gradient:** Caused by large weights; mitigated by gradient clipping, batch normalization.
- ✅ **Modern framework implementation:** PyTorch autograd / TensorFlow GradientTape perform reverse-mode autodiff on computational graphs.
- ✅ **Computational efficiency:** Backprop is $O(W)$ in time and memory, where $W$ is the total number of weights — same order as forward pass.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 1 Notes — Back-Propagation Algorithm**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020), PCCMT503 — Deep Learning Concepts*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) - Architectural Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT503 - DEEP LEARNING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) - Architectural Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** is a specialized class of deep feed-forward neural networks designed to process data that has a known grid-like topology, most commonly images (2-D pixel grids) and audio signals (1-D time-series). It employs a mathematical operation called **convolution** (a specialized kind of linear operation) in place of general matrix multiplication in at least one of its layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (High-Weight):** A CNN is a deep learning architecture that uses three core building blocks — **(i) Convolution Layer, (ii) Pooling Layer, and (iii) Fully Connected (FC) Layer** — to automatically and hierarchically learn spatial feature representations from input images with minimal pre-processing compared to classical hand-crafted feature methods.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Magnifying Glass Detective"

Imagine you are a detective trying to find a specific face in a crowded stadium photograph.

| Detective Action | CNN Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Scanning the photo with a small **magnifying glass** that moves left-to-right, top-to-bottom | The **Convolution Kernel (Filter)** sliding across the input image |
| The magnifying glass only sees a small patch at a time, but reuses the same pattern recognition everywhere | **Parameter Sharing** — the same filter weights are used across all spatial positions |
| Recording *where* the pattern was found in a new "highlight map" | The **Feature Map (Activation Map)** produced by the filter |
| Downsizing the highlight map to keep only the strongest evidence | **Pooling Layer** (Max/Average Pooling) |
| After many such maps, the detective takes a final decision: "Yes/No, who is it?" | **Fully Connected Layer** for final classification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CNNs Beat Traditional ANNs on Images:** A fully connected network on a $224 \times 224 \times 3$ image would require $\approx 150{,}000$ input neurons per hidden unit. CNNs exploit **local connectivity**, **parameter sharing**, and **translation equivariance** to reduce this to a tractable size.

### 1.3 Key Architectural Components (at a glance)

- **Input Layer** — Raw image tensor of shape $(H, W, C)$.
- **Convolutional Layer (`CONV`)** — Applies learnable filters to extract local features.
- **Activation Layer** — Usually **ReLU** $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ for non-linearity.
- **Pooling Layer (`POOL`)** — Downsamples feature maps (typically $2 \times 2$ max-pool).
- **Fully Connected Layer (`FC`)** — Performs high-level reasoning and final classification.
- **Softmax Layer** — Converts logits into class probabilities summing to **1**.

> [!TIP]
> A standard CNN follows the **"CONV $\to$ ReLU $\to$ POOL"** repeating block (a *convolutional block*), followed by one or more **FC layers** at the head. This is the universal design pattern students must memorise for KTU boards.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of a Convolution Layer — Step by Step

1. **Filter Initialization:** A filter $F$ of size $k_h \times k_w \times C_{in}$ is initialized with small random weights (e.g., He or Xavier initialization).
2. **Sliding Window (Strided Convolution):** The filter slides over the input volume. The horizontal/vertical step size is called the **stride** $S$.
3. **Element-wise Dot Product + Bias:** At each spatial position $(i, j)$, compute a scalar $z = \sum F \odot X_{patch} + b$.
4. **ReLU Activation:** Apply $a = \max(0, z)$.
5. **Feature Map Output:** The collection of all such scalars across positions forms one 2-D **feature map**.
6. **Multiple Filters:** Stacking $K$ different filters produces $K$ feature maps, giving an output volume of depth $K = C_{out}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Parameter Sharing Insight:** Every spatial location of a single feature map uses the *same* filter weights. This makes CNNs dramatically parameter-efficient and translation-equivariant.

### 2.2 Padding — "Preserving" the Spatial Size

When stride $= 1$ and no padding, the output shrinks by $(k - 1)$ per spatial dimension. To preserve dimensions, we **pad** the input border with $P$ rows/columns of zeros (**Zero-Padding**).

**Valid vs Same Convolution:**
- **Valid** (no padding): output shrinks.
- **Same** (padding chosen to preserve size): $P = \frac{k - 1}{2}$ for stride $= 1$ and odd kernel size.

### 2.3 Pooling Layer — Dimensionality Reduction

Pooling operates on each feature map **independently** (no learnable parameters, no depth change). Common variants:
- **Max Pooling:** $y = \max(\text{region})$ — captures the *strongest activation*.
- **Average Pooling:** $y = \frac{1}{k^2}\sum \text{region}$ — captures the *average presence*.
- **Global Average Pooling (GAP):** Replaces FC layers; outputs one value per feature map (used in modern architectures like GoogLeNet, ResNet).

### 2.4 Receptive Field — "How Much Input a Neuron Sees"

The **receptive field** of a neuron in a deep layer is the region of the original input image that influences its activation. Stacking convolutional and pooling layers *grows* the receptive field exponentially.

$$ RF_{l} = RF_{l-1} + (k_l - 1) \cdot S_{1..l-1} $$

where $S_{1..l-1} = \prod_{i=1}^{l-1} s_i$ is the cumulative stride product up to layer $l-1$.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $H_{out}$ | Output height | $H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{H_{in} + 2P - k_h}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$ | Pixels |
| $W_{out}$ | Output width | $W_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{W_{in} + 2P - k_w}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$ | Pixels |
| $C_{out}$ | Output depth | Equals number of filters $K$ | Integer |
| $P_{conv}$ | Params in a CONV layer | $P_{conv} = (k_h \cdot k_w \cdot C_{in} + 1) \cdot C_{out}$ | Integers |
| $P_{pool}$ | Params in a POOL layer | $0$ (no learnable params) | Integers |
| $P_{fc}$ | Params in an FC layer | $(N_{in} + 1) \cdot N_{out}$ | Integers |
| $P_{total}$ | Total network params | $\sum (\text{CONV params} + \text{FC params})$ | Integers |
| $FLOPs$ | Multiply-Accumulates per layer | $FLOPs = H_{out} \cdot W_{out} \cdot C_{out} \cdot k_h \cdot k_w \cdot C_{in}$ | MACs |
| $RF$ | Receptive field | $RF_l = RF_{l-1} + (k_l - 1) \cdot S_{1..l-1}$ | Pixels |
| $f_{ReLU}$ | Activation | $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ | Unitless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in Formula Sheet:** Students often write the bias count as $C_{out}$ instead of $+1$ inside the bracket. The "+1" accounts for **one bias term per output feature map**.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

CNNs are the **backbone** of:
- **Medical Imaging** — tumor segmentation, X-ray classification.
- **Autonomous Vehicles** — Tesla/Hyundai lane and pedestrian detection.
- **Industrial Inspection** — PCB defect detection (Kerala-based industries).
- **Satellite Imaging** — Crop health monitoring (ISRO collaborations).
- **Smartphone Apps** — Face unlock, Google Lens OCR.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Output Dimension of a Convolution Layer

**Statement:** Given an input of size $H_{in} \times W_{in} \times C_{in}$, a filter of size $k_h \times k_w$, padding $P$, and stride $S$, derive $H_{out}$ and $W_{out}$.

**Step 1 — Conceptual Setup:**  
The filter must be fully contained inside the (padded) input at every position. Let the *effective input* dimension be $H_{in} + 2P$. The number of valid filter placements along the height is the count of starting positions such that the filter does not exceed the right/bottom boundary.

**Step 2 — Position Counting:**  
If we step by $S$ pixels, the number of integer positions for the top-left corner of the filter is:

$$ N = \frac{(H_{in} + 2P) - k_h}{S} + 1 $$

**Step 3 — Floor Operation:**  
Since $N$ must be a non-negative integer, we take the floor:

$$ H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{H_{in} + 2P - k_h}{S} \right\rfloor + 1 $$

**Step 4 — Symmetry Argument:**  
The same logic applies horizontally, so:

$$ W_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{W_{in} + 2P - k_w}{S} \right\rfloor + 1 $$

**Step 5 — Depth Argument:**  
Each filter produces one feature map. If we have $K = C_{out}$ filters, the output volume is $H_{out} \times W_{out} \times C_{out}$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — "Calculate the CNN Pipeline Dimensions"

**Problem (KTU Standard Style):**  
Input image: $32 \times 32 \times 3$. Apply the following sequence and find the output volume after each layer and the total number of trainable parameters.

| Layer | Configuration |
|---|---|
| $L_1$ | Conv, $k=5, P=0, S=1, C_{out}=6$ |
| $L_2$ | Max-Pool, $k=2, S=2$ |
| $L_3$ | Conv, $k=5, P=0, S=1, C_{out}=16$ |
| $L_4$ | Max-Pool, $k=2, S=2$ |
| $L_5$ | Flatten $\to$ FC $\to$ 120 |
| $L_6$ | FC $\to$ 84 |
| $L_7$ | Softmax $\to$ 10 classes |

---

**Step 1 — Compute $L_1$ output volume:**

$$ H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{32 + 0 - 5}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = 28 $$

$$ W_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{32 + 0 - 5}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = 28 $$

$$ C_{out} = 6 \quad (\text{number of filters}) $$

$\Rightarrow$ Output of $L_1$ = $\mathbf{28 \times 28 \times 6}$

---

**Step 2 — Compute parameters of $L_1$:**

$$ P_{L_1} = (k_h \cdot k_w \cdot C_{in} + 1) \cdot C_{out} = (5 \cdot 5 \cdot 3 + 1) \cdot 6 = (75 + 1) \cdot 6 = 456 $$

$\Rightarrow$ **$L_1$ has 456 parameters.**

---

**Step 3 — Compute $L_2$ (Max-Pool) output volume:**

$$ H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{28 + 0 - 2}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 14 $$

$$ W_{out} = 14, \quad C_{out} = 6 \text{ (pooling preserves depth)} $$

$\Rightarrow$ Output of $L_2$ = $\mathbf{14 \times 14 \times 6}$, **$P_{L_2} = 0$**

---

**Step 4 — Compute $L_3$ output volume:**

$$ H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{14 + 0 - 5}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = 10 $$

$$ W_{out} = 10, \quad C_{out} = 16 $$

$\Rightarrow$ Output of $L_3$ = $\mathbf{10 \times 10 \times 16}$

---

**Step 5 — Compute parameters of $L_3$:**

$$ P_{L_3} = (5 \cdot 5 \cdot 6 + 1) \cdot 16 = (150 + 1) \cdot 16 = 2416 $$

$\Rightarrow$ **$L_3$ has 2,416 parameters.**

---

**Step 6 — Compute $L_4$ output volume:**

$$ H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{10 - 2}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 5 $$

$\Rightarrow$ Output of $L_4$ = $\mathbf{5 \times 5 \times 16}$, **$P_{L_4} = 0$**

---

**Step 7 — Flatten and compute FC parameters:**

Flattened size $= 5 \times 5 \times 16 = 400$

$$ P_{L_5} = (400 + 1) \cdot 120 = 48{,}120 $$

$$ P_{L_6} = (120 + 1) \cdot 84 = 10{,}164 $$

$$ P_{L_7} = (84 + 1) \cdot 10 = 850 $$

---

**Step 8 — Total Parameter Count:**

$$ P_{total} = 456 + 0 + 2416 + 0 + 48{,}120 + 10{,}164 + 850 $$

$$ P_{total} = \mathbf{62{,}006 \text{ parameters}} $$

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** FC layers consume **$\approx 95\%$** of the total parameters ($59{,}134 / 62{,}006$). This is precisely why modern architectures (ResNet, MobileNet) replace FC layers with **Global Average Pooling (GAP)**.

---

### 3.3 Receptive Field Worked Example

For three consecutive $3 \times 3$ conv layers with stride $= 1$:

$$ RF_1 = 3 $$

$$ RF_2 = RF_1 + (3 - 1) \cdot S_{1..1} = 3 + 2 \cdot 1 = 5 $$

$$ RF_3 = RF_2 + (3 - 1) \cdot S_{1..2} = 5 + 2 \cdot 1 = 7 $$

This confirms the well-known result: **three stacked $3 \times 3$ convs give a receptive field of $7 \times 7$**, with fewer parameters and more non-linearity than a single $7 \times 7$ conv.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic CNN

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Input Image 32x32x3] --> B1[Conv Layer L1 k5 S1 P0 6 filters]
    B1 --> B2[ReLU Activation]
    B2 --> C1[Max Pool L2 k2 S2]
    C1 --> D1[Conv Layer L3 k5 S1 P0 16 filters]
    D1 --> D2[ReLU Activation]
    D2 --> E1[Max Pool L4 k2 S2]
    E1 --> F1[Flatten Layer 400 nodes]
    F1 --> G1[FC Layer L5 120 neurons]
    G1 --> G2[ReLU Activation]
    G2 --> H1[FC Layer L6 84 neurons]
    H1 --> H2[ReLU Activation]
    H2 --> I1[Softmax Layer 10 classes]
    I1 --> J1[Output Probabilities]

    style A1 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,color:#000
    style B1 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FF7F0E,color:#000
    style C1 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2CA02C,color:#000
    style D1 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FF7F0E,color:#000
    style E1 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2CA02C,color:#000
    style F1 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#9467BD,color:#000
    style G1 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#9467BD,color:#000
    style H1 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#9467BD,color:#000
    style I1 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#D62728,color:#000
    style J1 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#D62728,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Color Legend:** Blue = Input, Orange = Convolution, Green = Pooling, Purple = Fully Connected, Red = Output/Classifier.

---

### 4.2 Convolution Operation — Sliding Filter Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A2[Input Image 5x5]
        A2A[Pixel Block]
    end
    subgraph B2[Filter 3x3]
        B2A[Kernel Weights]
    end
    subgraph C2[Feature Map 3x3]
        C2A[Activation Value]
    end

    A2 -->|Element-wise Dot Product + Bias| D2[Convolution Engine]
    B2 --> D2
    D2 -->|Stride 1 sliding| C2

    D2 --> E2[Add Bias b]
    E2 --> F2[Apply ReLU]
    F2 --> C2

    style A2 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,color:#000
    style B2 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FF7F0E,color:#000
    style C2 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2CA02C,color:#000
    style D2 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#9467BD,color:#000
    style E2 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#D62728,color:#000
    style F2 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#D62728,color:#000
```

---

### 4.3 Pooling Operation Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Input 4x4 Feature Map] --> P2{Choose Pooling Type}
    P2 -->|Max Pooling 2x2 S2| P3[Output 2x2 - Strongest Activation]
    P2 -->|Average Pooling 2x2 S2| P4[Output 2x2 - Mean Activation]
    P2 -->|Global Average Pooling| P5[Output 1x1 - One Value per Map]

    style P1 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FF7F0E,color:#000
    style P3 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2CA02C,color:#000

```

---

### 4.4 Receptive Field Growth Across Layers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Layer 1 Conv 3x3 RF 3] -->|add 2| R2[Layer 2 Conv 3x3 RF 5]
    R2 -->|add 2| R3[Layer 3 Conv 3x3 RF 7]
    R3 -->|add 4 after Pool| R4[Layer 4 Conv 3x3 RF 16]
    R4 -->|add 4| R5[Layer 5 Conv 3x3 RF 20]

    style R1 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,color:#000

    style R3 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2CA02C,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
> **Define the term *receptive field* in the context of a Convolutional Neural Network. How is it affected by stride and pooling operations?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **receptive field** of a neuron in a CNN is the region of the original input image that influences its activation. It grows as we go deeper into the network. **Stride** greater than 1 causes the receptive field to grow faster, because each neuron "sees" a wider input region due to downsampling. **Pooling layers** with stride 2 double the effective receptive field growth rate. Formally, $RF_l = RF_{l-1} + (k_l - 1) \cdot S_{1..l-1}$ where $S_{1..l-1}$ is the product of strides of all preceding layers. **(3 marks)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
> **Compare Max Pooling and Average Pooling. When would you prefer one over the other?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Max Pooling | Average Pooling |
|---|---|---|
| Operation | Takes max value in window | Takes mean of values in window |
| Information preserved | Strongest activation | Average presence |
| Use case | Feature detection (edges, textures) | Smooth feature maps, GAP at the end |
| Gradient flow | Sharper, can vanish | Smoother gradients |

**Preference:** Use **Max Pooling** in early/middle layers for sharp feature selection (e.g., in LeNet, AlexNet). Use **Average Pooling / GAP** at the end of the network before the classifier (e.g., GoogLeNet, ResNet) to reduce parameters. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> ### **Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **(a)** Explain the **convolution operation** in detail, clearly defining **filter, stride, padding, and feature map** with suitable diagrams. Discuss the role of **parameter sharing** in CNNs. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For an input volume of size **$224 \times 224 \times 3$**, apply the following layers sequentially and compute the output volume and number of parameters after each layer:  
>     (i) Conv: $k=7, P=3, S=2, C_{out}=64$  
>     (ii) Max-Pool: $k=3, S=2, P=1$  
>     (iii) Conv: $k=3, P=1, S=1, C_{out}=128$  
>     (iv) FC $\to$ 1000 classes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to A(a):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A convolution operation slides a learnable filter (kernel) of size $k \times k \times C_{in}$ across the input and computes element-wise dot products at each spatial position, producing a 2-D **feature map**.

**Filter (1 mark):** A small matrix of learnable weights. Multiple filters $\Rightarrow$ multiple feature maps. For an RGB input, the filter has depth 3 to span all channels.

**Stride (1 mark):** The step size $S$ of the filter as it slides. $S = 1$ keeps the spatial size roughly the same (with padding); $S = 2$ halves the spatial size.

**Padding (1 mark):** Zeros added to the input border. **Same padding** ($P = (k-1)/2$) preserves dimensions; **Valid padding** ($P=0$) shrinks them.

**Feature Map (1 mark):** The 2-D output of one filter, indicating *where* and *how strongly* a particular pattern is detected.

**Parameter Sharing (2 marks):** A single filter uses the *same* weights at every spatial position. This drastically reduces the number of parameters (e.g., a $5 \times 5 \times 3$ filter has only $76$ params, regardless of input size) and provides **translation equivariance** — the same pattern detected anywhere in the image.

---

#### **Model Solution to A(b):**

**Step 1 — Conv Layer (i):** $H_{in}=224, W_{in}=224, C_{in}=3, k=7, P=3, S=2, C_{out}=64$

$$ H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{224 + 6 - 7}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{223}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 111 + 1 = 112 $$

$$ W_{out} = 112, \quad C_{out} = 64 $$

$\Rightarrow$ Output = $\mathbf{112 \times 112 \times 64}$  
$\Rightarrow$ Parameters $= (7 \cdot 7 \cdot 3 + 1) \cdot 64 = (147 + 1) \cdot 64 = \mathbf{9{,}472}$

---

**Step 2 — Max-Pool Layer (ii):** $H_{in}=112, k=3, P=1, S=2$

$$ H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{112 + 2 - 3}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{111}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 55 + 1 = 56 $$

$\Rightarrow$ Output = $\mathbf{56 \times 56 \times 64}$, Parameters $= \mathbf{0}$

---

**Step 3 — Conv Layer (iii):** $H_{in}=56, k=3, P=1, S=1, C_{out}=128$

$$ H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{56 + 2 - 3}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = 56 $$

$\Rightarrow$ Output = $\mathbf{56 \times 56 \times 128}$  
$\Rightarrow$ Parameters $= (3 \cdot 3 \cdot 64 + 1) \cdot 128 = (576 + 1) \cdot 128 = \mathbf{73{,}856}$

---

**Step 4 — FC Layer (iv):** Flatten $56 \times 56 \times 128 = 401{,}408$

$$ P_{FC} = (401{,}408 + 1) \cdot 1000 = \mathbf{401{,}409{,}000} $$

**Final Summary Table (1 mark):**

| Layer | Output Volume | Parameters |
|---|---|---|
| Conv1 | $112 \times 112 \times 64$ | 9,472 |
| Pool1 | $56 \times 56 \times 64$ | 0 |
| Conv2 | $56 \times 56 \times 128$ | 73,856 |
| FC | 1000 | 401,409,000 |
| **Total** | — | **401,492,328** |

---

> ### **Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **(a)** Describe the **complete architecture of LeNet-5** with a neat diagram. Explain the role of each layer. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **role of pooling layers** in CNNs. Discuss **max pooling, average pooling, and global average pooling** with their advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to B(a) — LeNet-5 Architecture (7 marks):**

LeNet-5, proposed by **Yann LeCun (1998)**, was the first successful CNN used for handwritten digit recognition (MNIST). It takes $32 \times 32 \times 1$ input images.

| Layer | Type | Output | Params | Activation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $C_1$ | Conv $5 \times 5, 6$ filters | $28 \times 28 \times 6$ | 156 | tanh |
| $S_2$ | Avg-Pool $2 \times 2, S=2$ | $14 \times 14 \times 6$ | 0 | — |
| $C_3$ | Conv $5 \times 5, 16$ filters | $10 \times 10 \times 16$ | 2,416 | tanh |
| $S_4$ | Avg-Pool $2 \times 2, S=2$ | $5 \times 5 \times 16$ | 0 | — |
| $C_5$ | Conv $5 \times 5, 120$ filters | $1 \times 1 \times 120$ | 48,120 | tanh |
| $F_6$ | FC $\to$ 84 | 84 | 10,164 | tanh |
| Output | RBF / Softmax $\to$ 10 | 10 | 850 | — |

**Role of each layer (4 marks):**
- **$C_1$:** Detects low-level features like edges and corners. **(1 mark)**
- **$S_2$:** Downsamples, provides translation invariance. **(0.5 mark)**
- **$C_3$:** Detects higher-level features like curves and loops. **(1 mark)**
- **$S_4$:** Further downsampling, increases receptive field. **(0.5 mark)**
- **$C_5, F_6$:** Combines features for high-level reasoning. **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Model Solution to B(b) — Pooling Layers (7 marks):**

**Role of Pooling (2 marks):**
1. **Spatial dimensionality reduction** — Reduces $H \times W$, lowering computation in deeper layers.
2. **Translation invariance** — Small shifts in input do not change the pooled output.
3. **Receptive field expansion** — Allows deeper neurons to "see" a larger input region.
4. **Overfitting reduction** — Acts as a form of structural regularization.

**Max Pooling (2 marks):**  
Takes the maximum value in each window.  
- *Advantages:* Retains strongest activations; works well for feature detection; standard choice in classic CNNs.  
- *Disadvantages:* Discards all non-max values; can be sensitive to noise (a single outlier dominates).

**Average Pooling (1.5 marks):**  
Takes the mean of each window.  
- *Advantages:* Smoother feature maps; gentler gradient flow.  
- *Disadvantages:* Dilutes strong activations; can blur the feature map.

**Global Average Pooling (1.5 marks):**  
Averages the *entire* feature map into a single scalar, producing a $1 \times 1 \times C$ vector.  
- *Advantages:* Eliminates FC parameters entirely; drastically reduces model size; resists overfitting.  
- *Disadvantages:* Loss of spatial information; may underperform on tasks requiring precise localization.  
- *Used in:* GoogLeNet, ResNet, MobileNet (modern architectures).

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
> 1. **Bias Mistake:** In parameter calculation, students often write $(k_h \cdot k_w \cdot C_{in}) \cdot C_{out}$ *forgetting the $+1$ bias term*. This costs **1 full mark** per occurrence.
> 2. **Padding Mismatch:** Using "Same" padding without verifying the formula — it only works cleanly for **odd kernels with stride 1**.
> 3. **Floor Function Ignored:** Output dimensions are integers; always apply $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ for non-integer results.
> 4. **Confusing Receptive Field with Kernel Size:** A $3 \times 3$ filter does NOT mean the receptive field is $3 \times 3$ — it grows with depth.
> 5. **Wrong Depth Argument:** Pooling does NOT change the number of channels; students often incorrectly reduce depth.
> 6. **Skipping Units/Justification:** KTU examiners award **0.5 mark** for mentioning the formula AND **0.5 mark** for the unit/intuition — always justify, don't just compute.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Cram Sheet)

- **CNN = Convolution + Pooling + FC** — Three pillars, always in that order.
- **Convolution output size:** $H_{out} = \lfloor (H_{in} + 2P - k)/S \rfloor + 1$
- **Conv parameter count:** $P = (k_h \cdot k_w \cdot C_{in} + 1) \cdot C_{out}$
- **Pool parameter count:** **Always zero.** No weights, no biases.
- **Padding types:** *Valid* ($P=0$, shrinks) vs *Same* ($P=(k-1)/2$, preserves).
- **Stride $> 1$** causes spatial downsampling **without** learnable parameters.
- **Receptive field** grows as $RF_l = RF_{l-1} + (k_l - 1) \cdot S_{1..l-1}$.
- **Three $3 \times 3$ convs** $\equiv$ one $7 \times 7$ conv (in receptive field) but **fewer parameters** and **more non-linearity**.
- **Max Pool** $\to$ sharp features, **Avg Pool** $\to$ smooth features, **GAP** $\to$ replaces FC.
- **ReLU** is the default activation: $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ — introduces non-linearity cheaply.
- **Softmax** at the output for multi-class classification: $P(y_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$.
- **FC layers dominate parameters** — typically $> 90\%$ of total weights. GAP solves this.
- **Key Architectures (in chronological order):** LeNet-5 (1998) $\to$ AlexNet (2012) $\to$ VGG-16 (2014) $\to$ GoogLeNet/Inception (2014) $\to$ ResNet (2015).
- **Translation Equivariance:** Same filter detects the same pattern anywhere in the image.
- **Local Connectivity:** Each neuron connects to only a *small region*, not the full image.
- **Hierarchical Features:** Early layers learn edges $\to$ textures $\to$ parts $\to$ objects (deeper = more abstract).
- **Memory Tip for Boards:** "**C-R-P-C-R-P-Flatten-FC-FC-Softmax**" = standard CNN backbone pattern.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
