# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the Encoder–Decoder Sequence-to-Sequence Architecture. What is the role of the context vector?**

**Model Answer:**

The Encoder–Decoder Seq2Seq architecture is a neural network framework that maps an input sequence of variable length $X = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T)$ to an output sequence of variable length $Y = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_{T'})$. It consists of two recurrent networks:

1. **Encoder:** Processes the source sequence token by token, updating its hidden state at each step: $h_t = f_{\text{enc}}(h_{t-1}, x_t)$.
2. **Decoder:** Generates the target sequence autoregressively, conditioned on the **context vector** $\mathbf{c} = h_T^{\text{enc}}$.

**Role of the context vector $\mathbf{c}$:** It is the **sole information bridge** between encoder and decoder — a fixed-dimensional vector (typically 256–1024 dimensions) that must encode the entire semantic content of the source. The decoder is initialized with $\mathbf{c}$ and uses it at every generation step to produce the output distribution. In the *vanilla* (no-attention) variant, $\mathbf{c}$ is the *only* signal from the source available to the decoder, which becomes the information bottleneck that motivates attention mechanisms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1.5) + Role of context vector (1) + Example application (0.5).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between Bahdanau Attention and Luong Attention mechanisms.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Bahdanau Attention | Luong Attention |
|---|---|---|
| **Type of score** | Additive (concat with tanh) | Multiplicative (dot or bilinear) |
| **Score formula** | $e_{t,i} = v_a^{\top}\tanh(W_a s_{t-1} + U_a h_i)$ | $e_{t,i} = s_t^{\top} h_i$ (dot) or $s_t^{\top} W_a h_i$ (general) |
| **Decoder state used** | Previous state $s_{t-1}$ | Current state $s_t$ |
| **Computational cost** | Higher (one extra linear + tanh) | Lower (single matrix product) |
| **Output combination** | Concatenation of $c_t$ and $s_t$ | $\tilde{s}_t = \tanh(W_c[c_t ; s_t])$ concat-add |

Bahdanau is preferred when accuracy is critical and model size is moderate; Luong is preferred in large-scale systems like GNMT where efficiency matters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** Two differences (1 mark each) + Comparison summary (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the Encoder–Decoder Sequence-to-Sequence architecture for Machine Translation. Discuss the information bottleneck problem of the fixed context vector and explain how the Bahdanau attention mechanism solves it. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Seq2Seq Architecture & Diagram [7 Marks]

**Solution:**

The Seq2Seq architecture (Sutskever et al., 2014) consists of two coupled RNNs (typically LSTMs):

**Step 1 — Encoder (4 marks):**

The encoder reads the source sentence (e.g., French) one token at a time:

$$h_t^{\text{enc}} = \text{LSTM}(h_{t-1}^{\text{enc}}, E x_t), \quad t = 1, \ldots, T$$

The final hidden state becomes the **context vector**:

$$\mathbf{c} = h_T^{\text{enc}}$$

**Step 2 — Decoder (3 marks):**

The decoder is initialized with $s_0 = \mathbf{c}$ and generates target tokens (e.g., English) autoregressively:

$$s_t = \text{LSTM}(s_{t-1}, y_{t-1}, \mathbf{c})$$

$$p(y_t \mid y_{<t}, X) = \text{softmax}(W_o s_t + b_o)$$

**Diagram (refer SECTION 4.1)** — the KTU answer must include a labeled diagram with at least: source tokens $x_i$, encoder hidden states $h_i$, the single context vector $\mathbf{c}$, decoder states $s_t$, and output tokens $y_t$ with feedback arrows.

---

#### Part (b) — Bottleneck Problem & Bahdanau Attention [7 Marks]

**Solution:**

**Information Bottleneck (3 marks):**

The fixed-size context vector $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_c}$ must encode *all* information of a variable-length source. For source length $T$ and embedding dimension $d_e$, the compression ratio is:

$$R = \frac{T \cdot d_e}{d_c}$$

For $T = 50$, $d_e = 256$, $d_c = 512$, we get $R = 25$. Empirical results show BLEU degrades sharply for $T > 30$. Long-range dependencies are lost due to:

- Gradient vanishing in the encoder.
- Limited representational capacity of the fixed $\mathbf{c}$.

**Bahdanau Attention Mechanism (4 marks):**

Bahdanau et al. (2014) proposed that the decoder should attend to **all** encoder states at every step, computing a dynamic context:

1. **Alignment scores:** $e_{t,i} = v_a^{\top}\tanh(W_a s_{t-1} + U_a h_i)$
2. **Attention weights:** $\alpha_{t,i} = \dfrac{\exp(e_{t,i})}{\sum_{j=1}^T \exp(e_{t,j})}$
3. **Dynamic context:** $c_t = \sum_{i=1}^{T} \alpha_{t,i} h_i$
4. **Decoder update:** $s_t = \text{LSTM}(s_{t-1}, y_{t-1}, c_t)$

**How it solves the bottleneck:** Each $c_t$ is a *weighted average* of all encoder states, with weights $\alpha_{t,i}$ adaptively choosing which source positions matter most for the *current* target word. This eliminates the need to cram all information into a single vector, allowing the model to scale to long sequences while preserving word-level alignment.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain Teacher Forcing and the Exposure Bias problem in Seq2Seq training. Describe Beam Search decoding and the BLEU evaluation metric with suitable formulas. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Teacher Forcing & Exposure Bias [7 Marks]

**Solution:**

**Teacher Forcing (4 marks):**

During training, the decoder receives the **ground-truth** previous token $y_{t-1}^*$ as input, *not* the model's own prediction $\hat{y}_{t-1}$. This is called **teacher forcing** because the "teacher" (training data) corrects the student at every step.

- **Advantage:** Parallelizable across time steps; stable gradients; fast convergence.
- **Loss function:** $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T'} \log p(y_t^* \mid y_{<t}^*, X)$

**Exposure Bias (3 marks):**

At inference time, the model must feed its *own predictions* $\hat{y}_{t-1}$ back, since no ground truth is available. This creates a **train-test mismatch**:

- Training: clean ground-truth history $\rightarrow$ low error.
- Inference: noisy self-generated history $\rightarrow$ errors compound.

For a sequence of length $T'$, an error at step $t$ propagates to all subsequent steps, causing **error avalanche**. Empirical studies show degradation of 2–5 BLEU points due to exposure bias.

**Mitigations:**
1. **Scheduled Sampling** (Bengio et al., 2015): With probability $\epsilon_k$, use model prediction; decay $\epsilon_k$ over training.
2. **Professor Forcing** (Lamb et al., 2016): Adversarial domain adaptation between train and inference modes.
3. **Minimum Risk Training** (Shen et al., 2016): Optimize expected loss over sampled sequences.

---

#### Part (b) — Beam Search Decoding & BLEU Metric [7 Marks]

**Solution:**

**Beam Search (4 marks):**

Greedy decoding ($\arg\max$ at every step) is suboptimal because locally best tokens may lead to globally poor sequences. Beam search keeps the top-$k$ partial hypotheses (beam width $k$, typically 5–10):

**Algorithm:**
1. Initialize beam with $\langle \text{BOS} \rangle$ token.
2. At each step, expand each beam by all $|V|$ vocabulary tokens.
3. Compute cumulative log-probability: $\ell(Y) = \sum_t \log p(y_t \mid y_{<t}, X)$.
4. Retain top-$k$ hypotheses (prune the rest).
5. Terminate when $k$ sequences emit $\langle \text{EOS} \rangle$.
6. Score final candidates using length normalization:

$$\text{score}(Y) = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T'} \log p(y_t)}{(5 + T')^{\alpha} / 6^{\alpha}}$$

with $\alpha \approx 0.6$.

**BLEU Metric (3 marks):**

The **Bilingual Evaluation Understudy** score measures $n$-gram overlap between candidate and reference translations, with a brevity penalty:

$$\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp\!\left( \sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n \right)$$

where $p_n$ is the *modified $n$-gram precision* (clipped to max count in any reference) and:

$$\text{BP} = \begin{cases} 1, & c > r \\ \exp\!\left(1 - \dfrac{r}{c}\right), & c \le r \end{cases}$$

Standard usage: $N = 4$ with uniform weights $w_n = 0.25$. BLEU ranges in $[0, 1]$; values $> 0.4$ typically indicate reasonable translation quality.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common 5-Mark Losses):**
>
> 1. **Omitting the diagram** in Part A — KTU specifically awards 2 marks for a *neat, labeled* diagram. Use boxes, arrows, and label $\mathbf{c}$ explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing context vector with hidden state** — many students write "$c = h_t$ for all $t$" which is wrong. The vanilla context is *only* the final state.
> 3. **Skipping the $\tanh$ in Bahdanau** — the formula $v_a^{\top}\tanh(W_a s + U_a h)$ requires the hyperbolic tangent; writing just $v_a^{\top}(W_a s + U_a h)$ loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the brevity penalty** in BLEU — without BP, a degenerate 1-word candidate that happens to match could score well. KTU examiners *always* deduct for missing BP.
> 5. **Confusing teacher forcing with curriculum learning** — these are distinct concepts. Teacher forcing is *input substitution*; curriculum learning is *training-data ordering*.
> 6. **Computing attention weights without softmax** — alignment scores are *unnormalized*; the softmax is what makes them a valid probability distribution $\sum_i \alpha_{t,i} = 1$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3, Seq2Seq Architectures**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] **Seq2Seq** = Encoder RNN + Decoder RNN, connected by a context vector $\mathbf{c}$.
- [ ] **Context vector** = final encoder hidden state $h_T^{\text{enc}}$ (vanilla) — fixed-dimensional bottleneck.
- [ ] **Decoder** is **autoregressive** — feeds previous output as next input.
- [ ] **Training loss** = cross-entropy summed over target tokens.
- [ ] **Teacher forcing** uses ground-truth $y_{t-1}^*$ at training; causes **exposure bias** at inference.
- [ ] **Bahdanau attention** = additive score $v_a^{\top}\tanh(W_a s + U_a h)$, then softmax, then weighted sum.
- [ ] **Luong attention** = multiplicative score $s^{\top}h$ (dot) or $s^{\top}W h$ (general).
- [ ] **Dynamic context** $c_t = \sum_i \alpha_{t,i} h_i$ replaces the static $c$ — breaks the bottleneck.
- [ ] **Greedy decoding** = argmax at each step, fast but suboptimal.
- [ ] **Beam search** keeps top-$k$ hypotheses, with length-normalized scoring $\ell / (5+T')^{\alpha}$.
- [ ] **BLEU score** = $\text{BP} \cdot \exp(\sum_n w_n \log p_n)$, with $N=4$ typical, range $[0,1]$.

### Key Formulas to Memorize
- [ ] Encoder: $h_t = \text{LSTM}(h_{t-1}, x_t)$
- [ ] Decoder: $s_t = \text{LSTM}(s_{t-1}, y_{t-1}, c_t)$
- [ ] Bahdanau score: $e_{t,i} = v_a^{\top}\tanh(W_a s_{t-1} + U_a h_i)$
- [ ] Attention weight: $\alpha_{t,i} = \dfrac{\exp(e_{t,i})}{\sum_j \exp(e_{t,j})}$
- [ ] Dynamic context: $c_t = \sum_i \alpha_{t,i} h_i$
- [ ] BLEU: $\text{BP} \cdot \exp(\sum_n w_n \log p_n)$
- [ ] Beam score: $\ell / (5+T')^{\alpha}$

### Engineering Applications to Cite
- [ ] Machine Translation (GNMT, Google Translate 2016)
- [ ] Image Captioning (Show and Tell, Vinyals 2015)
- [ ] Text Summarization (abstractive)
- [ ] Speech Recognition (acoustic encoder → text decoder)
- [ ] Chatbots and dialogue systems
- [ ] Code generation and completion

### Historical / Citation Facts
- [ ] **Sutskever, Vinyals, Le (NIPS 2014)** — original Seq2Seq with LSTMs.
- [ ] **Cho et al. (2014)** — GRU-based encoder-decoder, same NIPS workshop.
- [ ] **Bahdanau, Cho, Bengio (ICLR 2015)** — first attention mechanism.
- [ ] **Luong, Pham, Manning (EMNLP 2015)** — multiplicative attention variants.
- [ ] **Wu et al. (2016, GNMT)** — Google's production system, 8 layers, residual, attention.
- [ ] **Vaswani et al. (NeurIPS 2017)** — Transformer: attention *without* RNN.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] Do not write $c = h_t$ for *any* $t$ in the vanilla model — only $t = T$.
- [ ] Do not forget the softmax in attention — alignment scores are unnormalized.
- [ ] Do not confuse Bahdanau's $s_{t-1}$ with Luong's $s_t$ — they differ.
- [ ] Do not omit the brevity penalty in BLEU — it's a 1-mark deduction.
- [ ] Do not claim attention "solves" exposure bias — it does not.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** Always start any Seq2Seq answer with the diagram, then define $\mathbf{c}$, then describe the *problem* (bottleneck), then the *solution* (attention). This four-step structure is exactly what KTU examiners reward.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BPTT for Training RNN
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT503 - DEEP LEARNING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** BPTT for Training RNN

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 3 — BPTT for Training RNN

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)** is the standard supervised learning algorithm used to compute gradients of the loss function with respect to the parameters of a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) by "unrolling" the network across all time steps of an input sequence and then applying the standard backpropagation algorithm on this unrolled computational graph.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, BPTT is a **temporal extension of the classical backpropagation algorithm** where the chain rule of calculus is applied across two dimensions simultaneously:

1. **Spatial depth** (across network layers, like in a standard MLP).
2. **Temporal depth** (across consecutive time steps $t = 1, 2, \dots, T$).

Mathematically, given a loss function $\mathcal{L}^{(t)}$ at each time step, the total loss for a sequence of length $T$ is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}^{(t)}$$

The objective of BPTT is to compute:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}}{\partial W}, \quad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}}{\partial U}, \quad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}}{\partial b}$$

where $W$ is the recurrent weight matrix, $U$ is the input-to-hidden weight matrix, and $b$ is the bias.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Domino Chain in Reverse"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: The Domino Chain in Reverse**
> Imagine a long row of dominoes standing in a line, where each domino represents the hidden state $h^{(t)}$ at time $t$. When you push the first domino (input $x^{(1)}$), it falls and hits the second, which hits the third, and so on — this is the **forward pass**. Now, if a sensitive instrument at the end (the loss $\mathcal{L}^{(T)}$) measures how far the last domino landed from a target, you must communicate that error *backwards* through every single domino to figure out which ones were misaligned. **BPTT is exactly that reverse communication process**, propagating the error signal from the final time step all the way back to $t = 1$, adjusting each domino's "weight" (i.e., the RNN parameters) so that the next time you push, the chain falls more accurately.

### 1.3 Why Standard Backpropagation Fails for RNNs

A standard feedforward MLP has **no cycles** in its computational graph, so gradients can be computed in a single backward sweep. An RNN, however, has the recurrent weight $W$ reused at every time step — this creates a **directed cycle** in the unfolded graph. We cannot directly apply the static backpropagation without first **unrolling** the network in time.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Table

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Input at time $t$ | $x^{(t)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ | Input vector at time step $t$ |
| Hidden state at time $t$ | $h^{(t)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ | Memory cell output at time $t$ |
| Output at time $t$ | $o^{(t)} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ | Network output at time $t$ |
| Recurrent weight | $W \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ | Hidden-to-hidden weight matrix |
| Input weight | $U \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ | Input-to-hidden weight matrix |
| Output weight | $V \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ | Hidden-to-output weight matrix |
| Activation function | $\tanh$ or $\sigma$ | Non-linearity applied to hidden state |
| Sequence length | $T$ | Number of time steps unrolled |
| Learning rate | $\eta$ or $\alpha$ | Step size for gradient descent |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The RNN Forward Pass (Unrolled Equations)

The vanilla RNN at each time step $t$ performs the following computations:

**Hidden state update:**

$$h^{(t)} = \tanh\left( U \, x^{(t)} + W \, h^{(t-1)} + b_h \right)$$

**Output computation:**

$$o^{(t)} = V \, h^{(t)} + b_o$$

**Softmax prediction (for classification):**

$$\hat{y}^{(t)} = \text{softmax}\left( o^{(t)} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **same** parameters $U, W, V, b_h, b_o$ are reused at every time step. This is the **weight-sharing property** of RNNs, which is what makes unrolling possible.

## 2.2 The Loss Function

For a sequence-level loss (sequence-to-sequence tasks) or per-time-step loss (many-to-many tasks), the total loss is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}^{(t)}\left( y^{(t)}, \hat{y}^{(t)} \right)$$

For **Cross-Entropy Loss** at each time step:

$$\mathcal{L}^{(t)} = - \sum_{k} y_k^{(t)} \log \hat{y}_k^{(t)}$$

## 2.3 BPTT — The Backward Pass Logic

The chain rule in BPTT must traverse **two paths**:
1. **Output path:** Gradient flows from $\mathcal{L}^{(t)}$ through $o^{(t)}$ back to $h^{(t)}$.
2. **Recurrent path:** Gradient flows from $h^{(t+1)}$ back to $h^{(t)}$ through $W$.

The **total gradient at hidden state $h^{(t)}$** has contributions from two sources:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}}{\partial h^{(t)}} = \underbrace{\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(t)}}{\partial h^{(t)}}}_{\text{local output path}} + \underbrace{\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}}{\partial h^{(t+1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial h^{(t+1)}}{\partial h^{(t)}}}_{\text{future recurrent path}}$$

This recursive relation is the **heart of BPTT** — the gradient at $h^{(t)}$ depends on the gradient at $h^{(t+1)}$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $h^{(t)} = \tanh(U x^{(t)} + W h^{(t-1)} + b_h)$ | Hidden state forward equation |
| 2 | $o^{(t)} = V h^{(t)} + b_o$ | Output forward equation |
| 3 | $\hat{y}^{(t)} = \text{softmax}(o^{(t)})$ | Probability output |
| 4 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}^{(t)}$ | Total loss across time |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(t)}}{\partial o^{(t)}} = \hat{y}^{(t)} - y^{(t)}$ | Gradient at output (softmax + cross-entropy) |
| 6 | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(t)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} = V^\top (\hat{y}^{(t)} - y^{(t)})$ | Local gradient at hidden state |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\partial h^{(t+1)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} = \text{diag}\left(1 - (h^{(t+1)})^2\right) W$ | Jacobian of recurrent transition |
| 8 | $\delta^{(t)} = \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}}{\partial h^{(t)}}$ | Hidden state error term (delta) |
| 9 | $\delta^{(t)} = V^\top(\hat{y}^{(t)} - y^{(t)}) + (W^\top \delta^{(t+1)}) \odot (1 - (h^{(t)})^2)$ | BPTT recursive delta equation |
| 10 | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial V} = \sum_{t} (\hat{y}^{(t)} - y^{(t)}) (h^{(t)})^\top$ | Gradient w.r.t. output weight $V$ |
| 11 | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W} = \sum_{t} \delta^{(t)} (h^{(t-1)})^\top$ | Gradient w.r.t. recurrent weight $W$ |
| 12 | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial U} = \sum_{t} \delta^{(t)} (x^{(t)})^\top$ | Gradient w.r.t. input weight $U$ |
| 13 | $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta}$ | Parameter update (gradient descent) |
| 14 | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W} = \sum_{t} \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} (W^\top)^{k} \cdot \ldots$ | Vanishing gradient expression form |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **$\delta^{(t)}$ recursion (Formula 9)** is the most examinable equation in BPTT. It is the BPTT analogue of the standard $\delta$ in backpropagation, and you must derive it in the exam if asked.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Speech Recognition** (DeepSpeech, Whisper) — BPTT is the workhorse for training acoustic models.
- **Machine Translation** (GNMT, early seq2seq) — Encoder–decoder RNNs were trained using BPTT.
- **Stock Price Forecasting** — Time-series RNNs use BPTT to learn long-range temporal correlations.
- **Music Generation & ECG Anomaly Detection** — Sequence-to-sequence RNNs.

## 2.6 Vanishing and Exploding Gradient Problem

The Jacobian product in BPTT produces a chain of $T$ matrix multiplications:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h^{(1)}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \prod_{k=1}^{t-1} \frac{\partial h^{(k+1)}}{\partial h^{(k)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(t)}}{\partial h^{(t)}}$$

Since $\frac{\partial h^{(k+1)}}{\partial h^{(k)}} \approx W \cdot \text{diag}(\tanh'(\cdot))$, if the **largest singular value** of $W$ is:

- **$> 1$** → gradients **explode** (NaN training, requires **gradient clipping**).
- **$< 1$** → gradients **vanish** (RNN cannot learn long dependencies → birth of **LSTM/GRU**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Truncated BPTT (TBPTT)** is a practical remedy: instead of backpropagating through all $T$ steps, only the most recent $\tau$ steps are used in the backward pass, which limits the multiplicative depth.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the BPTT Recursive Delta Equation

We want to derive **Formula 9** of the cheat sheet:

$$\delta^{(t)} = V^\top(\hat{y}^{(t)} - y^{(t)}) + \left(W^\top \delta^{(t+1)}\right) \odot \left(1 - (h^{(t)})^2\right)$$

### Step 1 — Define the total loss and the delta

The total loss is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}^{(t)}$$

Define $\delta^{(t)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}}{\partial h^{(t)}}$ (an $n$-dimensional vector).

### Step 2 — Identify all paths that contribute to $\delta^{(t)}$

The hidden state $h^{(t)}$ influences:
- The **output** $o^{(t)}$ at the **current** time step (via $V$).
- The **next hidden state** $h^{(t+1)}$ (via $W$).

Therefore, by the multivariate chain rule:

$$\delta^{(t)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}}{\partial h^{(t)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(t)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} + \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}}{\partial h^{(t+1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial h^{(t+1)}}{\partial h^{(t)}}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the first term (output path)

We have $o^{(t)} = V h^{(t)} + b_o$, so $\frac{\partial o^{(t)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} = V^\top$.

Using softmax + cross-entropy, the standard result is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(t)}}{\partial o^{(t)}} = \hat{y}^{(t)} - y^{(t)}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(t)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(t)}}{\partial o^{(t)}} \cdot \frac{\partial o^{(t)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} = V^\top (\hat{y}^{(t)} - y^{(t)})$$

### Step 4 — Compute the second term (recurrent path)

We have $h^{(t+1)} = \tanh(U x^{(t+1)} + W h^{(t)} + b_h)$.

The Jacobian with respect to $h^{(t)}$ is:

$$\frac{\partial h^{(t+1)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} = \text{diag}\left(1 - (h^{(t+1)})^2\right) \cdot W$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}}{\partial h^{(t+1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial h^{(t+1)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} = \delta^{(t+1)} \cdot \left[\text{diag}\left(1 - (h^{(t+1)})^2\right) W\right]$$

Using the property that for vectors $a$ and a diagonal matrix $D$, $a \cdot D = a \odot \text{diag}(D)$, this becomes:

$$= \left(W^\top \delta^{(t+1)}\right) \odot \left(1 - (h^{(t+1)})^2\right)$$

### Step 5 — Combine

Adding the two terms:

$$\delta^{(t)} = V^\top (\hat{y}^{(t)} - y^{(t)}) + \left(W^\top \delta^{(t+1)}\right) \odot \left(1 - (h^{(t+1)})^2\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The recursion is initialized at the **final** time step $t = T$ where $\delta^{(T+1)} = 0$ (no future contribution), giving $\delta^{(T)} = V^\top(\hat{y}^{(T)} - y^{(T)})$.

## 3.2 Derivation of $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W}$

We start with the fact that $W$ appears in **every** time step's hidden state:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}}{\partial W} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}}{\partial h^{(t)}} \cdot \frac{\partial h^{(t)}}{\partial W}$$

From $h^{(t)} = \tanh(U x^{(t)} + W h^{(t-1)} + b_h)$:

$$\frac{\partial h^{(t)}}{\partial W} = \text{diag}\left(1 - (h^{(t)})^2\right) \cdot h^{(t-1)\top}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}}{\partial W} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta^{(t)} \odot \left(1 - (h^{(t)})^2\right) \cdot (h^{(t-1)})^\top$$

For compactness, this is often written as:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \tilde{\delta}^{(t)} (h^{(t-1)})^\top$$

where $\tilde{\delta}^{(t)} = \delta^{(t)} \odot (1 - (h^{(t)})^2)$.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — TBPTT on a 3-Step Toy Sequence

**Setup:** A scalar RNN with $W = 0.5$, $U = 0.3$, no bias, $h^{(0)} = 0$, target $y = [0, 1, 0]$, $\tanh$ activation, and **truncation window $\tau = 2$**.

### Step 1 — Forward Pass

$$
\begin{aligned}
h^{(1)} &= \tanh(0.3 \cdot x^{(1)} + 0.5 \cdot 0) \\
h^{(2)} &= \tanh(0.3 \cdot x^{(2)} + 0.5 \cdot h^{(1)}) \\
h^{(3)} &= \tanh(0.3 \cdot x^{(3)} + 0.5 \cdot h^{(2)})
\end{aligned}
$$

Assume $x^{(1)} = 1, x^{(2)} = 0, x^{(3)} = 1$, and the prediction is $\hat{y}^{(t)} = h^{(t)}$ (regression):

$$
\begin{aligned}
h^{(1)} &\approx \tanh(0.3) = 0.2913 \\
h^{(2)} &\approx \tanh(0.5 \cdot 0.2913) = \tanh(0.1456) = 0.1447 \\
h^{(3)} &\approx \tanh(0.3 + 0.5 \cdot 0.1447) = \tanh(0.3724) = 0.3570
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Loss (Mean Squared Error)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\left[(0 - 0.2913)^2 + (1 - 0.1447)^2 + (0 - 0.3570)^2\right] \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2}\left[0.0849 + 0.7316 + 0.1274\right] \\
&= 0.4720
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Backward Pass (TBPTT, $\tau = 2$)

We compute $\delta^{(3)}, \delta^{(2)}$ only (truncate before $t = 1$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta^{(3)} &= \hat{y}^{(3)} - y^{(3)} = 0.3570 - 0 = 0.3570 \\
\delta^{(2)} &= (\hat{y}^{(2)} - y^{(2)}) + W \cdot \delta^{(3)} \cdot (1 - (h^{(2)})^2) \\
&= (0.1447 - 1) + 0.5 \cdot 0.3570 \cdot (1 - 0.0209) \\
&= -0.8553 + 0.5 \cdot 0.3570 \cdot 0.9791 \\
&= -0.8553 + 0.1748 = -0.6805
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Update $W$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W} \approx \delta^{(3)} \cdot (1 - (h^{(3)})^2) \cdot h^{(2)} + \delta^{(2)} \cdot (1 - (h^{(2)})^2) \cdot h^{(1)}
$$

$$
= 0.3570 \cdot 0.8726 \cdot 0.1447 + (-0.6805) \cdot 0.9791 \cdot 0.2913 = -0.1490
$$

With $\eta = 0.1$:

$$W_{\text{new}} = 0.5 - 0.1 \cdot (-0.1490) = 0.5149$$

> [!NOTE]
> The update nudges $W$ upward, which is the direction that helps the network better remember past inputs.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Recommended BPTT Variant | Reason |
|----------|--------------------------|--------|
| Short sequences ($T < 50$) | Full BPTT | Gradients are still computable |
| Long sequences ($T > 200$) | Truncated BPTT ($\tau = 20$–$50$) | Memory and vanishing gradient relief |
| Online / streaming data | TBPTT with $\tau = 1$ (RTRL-like) | Real-time feasibility |
| Tasks requiring long context | LSTM/GRU + TBPTT | Architectural mitigation of vanishing gradients |
| Numerical instability observed | TBPTT + gradient clipping | Clips exploding gradient norm at threshold $\|\nabla\| \le c$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RNN Unrolled Across Time — Computational Graph

The following Mermaid diagram shows how the RNN is **unrolled** into a feedforward-like graph of $T = 4$ time steps, on which standard backpropagation can then operate in reverse.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef inputNode fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000
    classDef hiddenNode fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    classDef lossNode fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000

    x1["x_t1"]:::inputNode
    x2["x_t2"]:::inputNode
    x3["x_t3"]:::inputNode
    x4["x_t4"]:::inputNode

    h1["h_t1"]:::hiddenNode
    h2["h_t2"]:::hiddenNode
    h3["h_t3"]:::hiddenNode
    h4["h_t4"]:::hiddenNode

    o1["o_t1"]:::outputNode
    o2["o_t2"]:::outputNode
    o3["o_t3"]:::outputNode
    o4["o_t4"]:::outputNode

    L1["L_t1"]:::lossNode
    L2["L_t2"]:::lossNode
    L3["L_t3"]:::lossNode
    L4["L_t4"]:::lossNode

    h0(("h_t0 = 0"))

    h0 --> h1
    x1 --> h1
    h1 --> h2
    x2 --> h2
    h2 --> h3
    x3 --> h3
    h3 --> h4
    x4 --> h4

    h1 --> o1
    h2 --> o2
    h3 --> o3
    h4 --> o4

    o1 --> L1
    o2 --> L2
    o3 --> L3
    o4 --> L4
```

## 4.2 BPTT — Backward Flow of Gradients

This diagram illustrates the **reverse sweep** of BPTT. The red dashed arrows indicate gradient flow, which travels **from right to left** through the unrolled graph, and the same weight $W$ receives accumulated gradient contributions from every time step.

```mermaid
flowchart RL
    classDef gradNode fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef weightNode fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#4527A0,color:#000
    classDef dataNode fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000

    Lgrad["grad L_t4"]:::gradNode
    ograd["grad o_t4"]:::gradNode
    h4grad["grad h_t4"]:::gradNode
    h3grad["grad h_t3"]:::gradNode
    h2grad["grad h_t2"]:::gradNode
    h1grad["grad h_t1"]:::gradNode

    Wshared["W (shared recurrent weight)"]:::weightNode
    Ushared["U (input weight)"]:::weightNode
    Vshared["V (output weight)"]:::weightNode

    Lgrad --> ograd
    ograd --> Vshared
    ograd --> h4grad
    h4grad --> Wshared
    h4grad --> h3grad
    h3grad --> Wshared
    h3grad --> h2grad
    h2grad --> Wshared
    h2grad --> h1grad
    h1grad --> Wshared
    h3grad --> Ushared
    h2grad --> Ushared
    h1grad --> Ushared
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — BPTT in a Production Training Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef procBox fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef dataBox fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef controlBox fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000

    A["Input Sequence X = x1, x2, ..., xT"]:::dataBox
    B["Initialize h0 = 0"]:::dataBox
    C["Forward Pass Loop t = 1 to T"]:::procBox
    D["Compute h_t, o_t, yhat_t"]:::procBox
    E["Compute Loss L_t"]:::procBox
    F["Total Loss L = sum L_t"]:::procBox
    G["Backward Pass: compute delta_T to delta_1"]:::procBox
    H["Accumulate Gradients for U, W, V"]:::procBox
    I{"TBPTT or Full BPTT?"}:::controlBox
    J["Apply Gradient Clipping if norm grad is large"]:::procBox
    K["Update Parameters with SGD or Adam"]:::procBox
    L{"Convergence?"}:::controlBox
    M["End of Training"]:::dataBox

    A --> C
    B --> C
    C --> D --> E --> F
    F --> G --> H
    H --> I
    I -- "Long sequence" --> J
    I -- "Short sequence" --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L
    L -- "No" --> C
    L -- "Yes" --> M
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT). Why is it required for training RNNs?** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

BPTT is the gradient-based learning algorithm used to train Recurrent Neural Networks. It works by **unrolling the RNN across all time steps** of an input sequence to form a feedforward-style computational graph, and then applying the standard backpropagation algorithm on this unrolled graph to compute gradients of the loss with respect to the network parameters $U, W, V$.

It is required because:
1. RNNs have **cyclic connections** in their compact form — standard backpropagation cannot be directly applied to cyclic graphs.
2. The recurrent weight $W$ is **shared** across all time steps, so its gradient must accumulate contributions from every time step.
3. BPTT correctly handles this **weight-sharing constraint** by computing $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta^{(t)} (h^{(t-1)})^\top$.

---

### **Q2. What is the vanishing gradient problem in RNNs? How does truncated BPTT address it?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **vanishing gradient problem** occurs when training deep RNNs on long sequences: during BPTT, gradients are multiplied by the same recurrent Jacobian $W \cdot \text{diag}(\tanh'(\cdot))$ at every time step. If the largest singular value of this product is less than **1**, the gradient signal decays exponentially with time, making it impossible for the network to learn dependencies between distant time steps.

**Truncated BPTT (TBPTT)** limits the backward pass to a window of the most recent $\tau$ time steps (e.g., $\tau = 20$), thereby **bounding the depth of the gradient chain multiplication** and reducing the severity of vanishing gradients, at the cost of ignoring very long-range dependencies.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### 📌 Question A — 14 Marks

**`(a)` Derive the recursive delta equation used in BPTT. Show clearly how the gradient at hidden state $h^{(t)}$ depends on the gradient at $h^{(t+1)}$.` (7 marks)`** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`

**Model Solution:**

The total loss is $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}^{(t)}$.

Let $\delta^{(t)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}}{\partial h^{(t)}}$.

Since $h^{(t)}$ influences both $o^{(t)}$ (output path) and $h^{(t+1)}$ (recurrent path), we write:

$$
\delta^{(t)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(t)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} + \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}}{\partial h^{(t+1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial h^{(t+1)}}{\partial h^{(t)}}
$$

**Step 1 — Output path gradient:** With $o^{(t)} = V h^{(t)}$, and using softmax cross-entropy giving $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(t)}}{\partial o^{(t)}} = \hat{y}^{(t)} - y^{(t)}$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(t)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} = V^\top (\hat{y}^{(t)} - y^{(t)})
$$

**Step 2 — Recurrent path gradient:** With $h^{(t+1)} = \tanh(U x^{(t+1)} + W h^{(t)} + b_h)$:

$$
\frac{\partial h^{(t+1)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} = \text{diag}\left(1 - (h^{(t+1)})^2\right) W
$$

Hence:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}}{\partial h^{(t+1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial h^{(t+1)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} = \left(W^\top \delta^{(t+1)}\right) \odot \left(1 - (h^{(t+1)})^2\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Combine:**

$$
\boxed{\delta^{(t)} = V^\top(\hat{y}^{(t)} - y^{(t)}) + \left(W^\top \delta^{(t+1)}\right) \odot \left(1 - (h^{(t+1)})^2\right)}
$$

Boundary condition: $\delta^{(T+1)} = 0$, so $\delta^{(T)} = V^\top(\hat{y}^{(T)} - y^{(T)})$.

**`(b)` Explain the vanishing and exploding gradient problem in RNNs. Discuss TWO techniques to mitigate it.` (7 marks)`**

**Model Solution:**

During BPTT, the gradient $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h^{(1)}}$ involves a product of $T$ Jacobian matrices:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h^{(1)}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left[\prod_{k=1}^{t-1} \frac{\partial h^{(k+1)}}{\partial h^{(k)}}\right] \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(t)}}{\partial h^{(t)}}
$$

Each factor $\frac{\partial h^{(k+1)}}{\partial h^{(k)}} = \text{diag}(1 - (h^{(k+1)})^2) \cdot W$ has singular values bounded by the spectral radius $\rho(W)$.

- If $\rho(W) > 1$ and $\tanh'$ is not vanishing → **exploding gradient** (NaN loss, divergence).
- If $\rho(W) < 1$ → **vanishing gradient** (no learning of long-range patterns).

**Mitigation Techniques:**

1. **Gradient Clipping** — Rescale the gradient when its norm exceeds a threshold $c$:

$$
g \leftarrow \begin{cases} g & \text{if } \|g\| \le c \\ \dfrac{c}{\|g\|} \, g & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

This addresses **exploding** gradients.

2. **Gated Architectures (LSTM/GRU)** — Replace simple $\tanh$ units with gating mechanisms that allow gradients to flow through "cell state" highways with near-identity Jacobians, addressing **vanishing** gradients.

3. **Truncated BPTT** — Limit the temporal depth of the chain rule multiplication.

4. **Orthogonal weight initialization** — Initialize $W$ as a random orthogonal matrix, setting $\rho(W) \approx 1$.

---

### 📌 Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**`(a)` With a neat diagram, explain how an RNN is unrolled in time for training via BPTT. Distinguish between forward pass and backward pass computations.` (7 marks)`** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**Model Solution:**

**Unrolling:** A compact RNN with a self-loop is mathematically equivalent to a feedforward network of $T$ layers, where each "layer" corresponds to one time step and the same weight matrices $U, W, V$ are reused across all layers. This unrolling converts the cyclic dependency into an acyclic graph amenable to backpropagation.

**Forward Pass (left → right):** At each time step $t = 1, 2, \dots, T$:

$$
h^{(t)} = \tanh(U x^{(t)} + W h^{(t-1)} + b_h), \quad o^{(t)} = V h^{(t)} + b_o
$$

The hidden state $h^{(t-1)}$ is passed forward, and the loss $\mathcal{L}^{(t)} = \ell(y^{(t)}, \hat{y}^{(t)})$ is computed.

**Backward Pass (right → left):** Starting from $t = T$ with $\delta^{(T+1)} = 0$, compute:

$$
\delta^{(t)} = V^\top(\hat{y}^{(t)} - y^{(t)}) + (W^\top \delta^{(t+1)}) \odot (1 - (h^{(t)})^2)
$$

Then accumulate:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial V} = \sum_{t} (\hat{y}^{(t)} - y^{(t)}) (h^{(t)})^\top, \quad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W} = \sum_{t} \delta^{(t)} (h^{(t-1)})^\top
$$

The diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes shows the unrolled forward graph, and **Section 4.2** shows the backward gradient flow.

**` (b)` Consider a vanilla RNN with $h^{(t)} = \tanh(W h^{(t-1)} + U x^{(t)})$ and loss $L = \sum_t \frac{1}{2}(y^{(t)} - h^{(t)})^2$. Compute the gradient $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W}$ using BPTT for a sequence of length 3, expressing the answer in terms of the delta terms.` (7 marks)`**

**Model Solution:**

We treat $V = I$ (identity output) for simplicity, so $\hat{y}^{(t)} = h^{(t)}$.

**Step 1 — Local output gradient:** With $\hat{y}^{(t)} = h^{(t)}$ and $\mathcal{L}^{(t)} = \frac{1}{2}(y^{(t)} - h^{(t)})^2$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(t)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} = h^{(t)} - y^{(t)}
$$

**Step 2 — Recursive delta:** With $V = I$ and $b_o = 0$:

$$
\delta^{(t)} = (h^{(t)} - y^{(t)}) + (W^\top \delta^{(t+1)}) \odot (1 - (h^{(t)})^2)
$$

**Step 3 — Boundary:** $\delta^{(4)} = 0$, so:

$$
\delta^{(3)} = h^{(3)} - y^{(3)}
$$

$$
\delta^{(2)} = (h^{(2)} - y^{(2)}) + (W^\top \delta^{(3)}) \odot (1 - (h^{(2)})^2)
$$

$$
\delta^{(1)} = (h^{(1)} - y^{(1)}) + (W^\top \delta^{(2)}) \odot (1 - (h^{(1)})^2)
$$

**Step 4 — Gradient w.r.t. $W$:** Since $W$ appears at every time step:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W} = \sum_{t=1}^{3} \delta^{(t)} \odot (1 - (h^{(t)})^2) \cdot (h^{(t-1)})^\top
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W} = \sum_{t=1}^{3} \left[\delta^{(t)} \odot (1 - (h^{(t)})^2)\right] (h^{(t-1)})^\top}
$$

This is the **3-step unrolled BPTT gradient** and is then used in gradient descent as $W \leftarrow W - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W}$.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in BPTT Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\odot$ (Hadamard / element-wise) product** — students frequently write matrix products when the correct operation is element-wise multiplication. This alone costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Missing the boundary condition** $\delta^{(T+1)} = 0$ — without it, the recursion is incomplete and the examiner will deduct **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing $\delta^{(t)}$ with the output gradient** — the output gradient is $V^\top(\hat{y}^{(t)} - y^{(t)})$; the hidden delta has **two** components (output path + recurrent path).
> 4. **Not unrolling the network** in the diagram — examiners expect at least 3 time steps explicitly drawn with the **same** $W$ labeled at each transition.
> 5. **Forgetting the $(1 - h^2)$ factor** from $\tanh'$ — this derivative is critical and is often missed in the Jacobian.
> 6. **In gradient clipping, forgetting the norm check** — you must show the **conditional** structure of the clip equation.

---

## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — BPTT for RNNs**
>
> - ✅ BPTT = backprop on the **time-unrolled** RNN graph.
> - ✅ Same parameters $U, W, V, b$ are **shared** at every time step.
> - ✅ Forward pass: $h^{(t)} = \tanh(U x^{(t)} + W h^{(t-1)} + b_h)$, $o^{(t)} = V h^{(t)} + b_o$.
> - ✅ Total loss: $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}^{(t)}$.
> - ✅ **Master Equation:** $\delta^{(t)} = V^\top(\hat{y}^{(t)} - y^{(t)}) + (W^\top \delta^{(t+1)}) \odot (1 - (h^{(t)})^2)$.
> - ✅ Boundary: $\delta^{(T+1)} = 0$.
> - ✅ Gradients:
>   - $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial V} = \sum_t (\hat{y}^{(t)} - y^{(t)})(h^{(t)})^\top$
>   - $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W} = \sum_t \delta^{(t)} \odot (1 - (h^{(t)})^2) \cdot (h^{(t-1)})^\top$
>   - $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial U} = \sum_t \delta^{(t)} \odot (1 - (h^{(t)})^2) \cdot (x^{(t)})^\top$
> - ✅ **Vanishing gradient** occurs when spectral radius of $W$ is less than 1.
> - ✅ **Exploding gradient** occurs when spectral radius of $W$ is greater than 1.
> - ✅ **TBPTT** truncates the backward pass to a window of size $\tau$.
> - ✅ **Gradient clipping** rescales gradients whose norm exceeds a threshold $c$.
> - ✅ LSTM and GRU are architectural solutions to vanishing gradients.
> - ✅ Memory cost of full BPTT is $O(T)$ — TBPTT reduces this to $O(\tau)$.
> - ✅ BPTT is the temporal analogue of standard backprop — chain rule applied through both **depth** and **time**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deep Recurrent Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT503 - DEEP LEARNING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Deep Recurrent Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Deep Recurrent Network (Deep RNN)** is a hierarchical neural architecture that stacks **multiple recurrent layers** vertically (depth) on top of a single sequential time-unrolled structure (time), enabling the model to learn representations at *multiple levels of abstraction* over sequential data. Formally, a deep RNN with $L$ layers unrolled over $T$ time steps maintains hidden state tensors $\mathbf{H}^{(l)} = \{\mathbf{h}_t^{(l)}\}_{t=1}^{T}$ for each layer $l \in \{1, 2, \ldots, L\}$, where each layer's state evolves according to a recurrent transition function $f_{\theta_l}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> A *Deep Recurrent Neural Network* is an extension of the standard RNN in which more than one hidden recurrent layer is stacked, so that the hidden state of layer $l$ at time $t$ is computed from the hidden state of layer $l$ at time $t-1$ **and** the hidden state of layer $l-1$ at the same time $t$. This allows the network to model complex temporal hierarchies.

## 1.2 Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) — Foundational Block

A standard (single-layer) **Recurrent Neural Network** is a class of artificial neural networks designed to recognize patterns in sequences of data, such as time series, speech, text, or video. Unlike feedforward networks, an RNN maintains an internal **hidden state** $\mathbf{h}_t$ that acts as a form of memory, capturing information about previous inputs in the sequence.

The fundamental computation at each time step $t$ is given by:

$$\mathbf{h}_t = \phi_h\left(\mathbf{W}_{xh}\,\mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\,\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{b}_h\right)$$

$$\mathbf{y}_t = \phi_y\left(\mathbf{W}_{hy}\,\mathbf{h}_t + \mathbf{b}_y\right)$$

where $\phi_h$ and $\phi_y$ are the hidden and output activation functions, $\mathbf{W}_{xh}$, $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$, $\mathbf{W}_{hy}$ are the input-to-hidden, hidden-to-hidden, and hidden-to-output weight matrices, and $\mathbf{b}_h$, $\mathbf{b}_y$ are bias vectors.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Reading a Story" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: Reading a Mystery Novel 📖**
> Imagine you are reading a mystery novel word-by-word.
> - A **feedforward network** sees only the current word in isolation — it cannot remember who "she" refers to across paragraphs.
> - A **shallow RNN (1 layer)** remembers the previous sentence — it can keep track of short context (a few words back).
> - A **deep RNN (stacked layers)** is like having *multiple memory registers*: a low-level layer remembers character-level spelling, a middle layer remembers grammar, and a high-level layer remembers the plot and character identities. Each layer reads the *summary* produced by the layer below.
> - **LSTM/GRU cells** add a *notebook and a highlighter* — they decide what to *write down*, what to *erase*, and what to *highlight*, allowing them to remember crucial information from hundreds of words ago without forgetting or over-emphasizing.

## 1.4 Why "Deep" Recurrent Networks?

A single-layer RNN is theoretically a **Turing-complete** model (Siegelmann, 1995), but in practice, it suffers from limited representational capacity. **Depth** in recurrent networks provides two key advantages:

1. **Hierarchical Feature Learning** — Lower layers capture *local* temporal patterns (e.g., phonemes, character n-grams), while higher layers capture *global*, abstract patterns (e.g., words, semantics).
2. **Easier Optimization** — Stacking small recurrent layers (each with moderate hidden size) often converges faster and generalizes better than one large recurrent layer (Pascanu et al., 2014).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction — KTU Frequently Tested:**
> "Deep" in **Deep RNN** refers to **vertical stacking** (multiple recurrent layers stacked one above the other), **NOT** to time unrolling. Time unrolling is a *computational graph* technique, not depth. A "1-layer network unrolled over 50 time steps" is *not* a deep RNN; a "4-layer network unrolled over 50 time steps" *is* a deep RNN.

## 1.5 Building Blocks of Deep RNNs — A Taxonomy

| **Cell Type** | **Full Name** | **Gates** | **Year** | **Key Innovation** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vanilla RNN | Recurrent Neural Network | 0 | 1986 | Hidden state recurrence |
| LSTM | Long Short-Term Memory | 3 (forget, input, output) | 1997 | Additive memory cell, gradient highway |
| GRU | Gated Recurrent Unit | 2 (reset, update) | 2014 | Simplified gating, fewer parameters |
| Peephole LSTM | Peephole Long Short-Term Memory | 3 | 2000 | Gates see cell state directly |
| Bi-RNN | Bidirectional RNN | Depends on cell | 1997 | Forward + backward context |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Deep Stacked RNN

Consider a Deep RNN with $L$ stacked recurrent layers, unrolled over $T$ time steps.

### 2.1.1 Layer-wise State Update Equations

For each layer $l = 1, 2, \ldots, L$ and each time step $t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$:

$$\mathbf{a}_t^{(l)} = \mathbf{W}^{(l)}\,\mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{(l)} + \mathbf{U}^{(l)}\,\mathbf{h}_t^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}$$

$$\mathbf{h}_t^{(l)} = f\left(\mathbf{a}_t^{(l)}\right)$$

where:
- $\mathbf{h}_t^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l}$ is the hidden state of layer $l$ at time $t$
- $\mathbf{h}_t^{(0)} \equiv \mathbf{x}_t$ is the input at time $t$
- $\mathbf{h}_t^{(L)}$ is the final-layer output (often fed to a classifier)
- $\mathbf{W}^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l \times n_l}$ is the **recurrent** weight matrix
- $\mathbf{U}^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l \times n_{l-1}}$ is the **feedforward** (vertical) weight matrix
- $f$ is the activation (typically $\tanh$ for vanilla RNN, or gating functions for LSTM/GRU)

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Hint:** Note the *dual* recurrence: $\mathbf{h}_t^{(l)}$ depends on **(a)** its own past $\mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{(l)}$ (temporal recurrence) and **(b)** the lower layer's same-time representation $\mathbf{h}_t^{(l-1)}$ (vertical/feedforward). This is what makes it a **deep** recurrent network.

## 2.2 Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)

Training a deep (unrolled) RNN is performed via **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)** — unrolling the network across all $T$ time steps and applying standard backpropagation on the resulting feedforward graph.

### 2.2.1 Loss Function

For a sequence-level loss (e.g., cross-entropy summed over all time steps):

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T}\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{t,c}\,\log\left(\hat{y}_{t,c}\right)$$

where $C$ is the number of output classes and $\hat{y}_{t,c}$ is the predicted probability.

### 2.2.2 Gradient Flow — The Critical Challenge

The gradient of loss $\mathcal{L}_t$ at time $t$ with respect to hidden state $\mathbf{h}_k$ at an earlier time $k < t$ involves a product of Jacobians:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}\cdot \prod_{i=k+1}^{t}\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_i}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{i-1}}$$

The Jacobian $\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{h}_i}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{i-1}}$ is, for a vanilla RNN:

$$\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_i}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{i-1}} = \text{diag}\!\left(1 - \tanh^2(\mathbf{a}_i)\right)\,\mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\!\top}$$

This leads to a product of the form $\left(\text{diag}\!\left(1 - \tanh^2(\mathbf{a}_i)\right)\,\mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\!\top}\right)^{t-k}$, whose magnitude is governed by the largest singular value of $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$.

## 2.3 The Vanishing & Exploding Gradient Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fundamental Problem of Deep RNNs (Pascanu et al., 2013):**
> If the largest singular value $\sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_{hh}) > 1$, the gradient norm **explodes exponentially** with $t-k$ → numerical instability, NaN losses, oscillating training.
> If $\sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_{hh}) < 1$, the gradient norm **vanishes exponentially** with $t-k$ → long-term dependencies cannot be learned; earlier time steps receive negligible updates.

### 2.3.1 Why LSTM Solves This

The LSTM replaces the multiplicative chain with an **additive memory cell** $\mathbf{c}_t$ updated via:

$$\mathbf{c}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{c}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{c}}_t$$

The gradient $\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{c}_t}{\partial \mathbf{c}_{t-1}} = \mathbf{f}_t$ (element-wise, in $[0,1]$). When the forget gate $\mathbf{f}_t \approx 1$, the gradient **flows unchanged** across many time steps — creating a "gradient highway."

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Formula / Concept** | **Description** | **Used For** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{h}_t = \phi(\mathbf{W}_{xh}\mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{b}_h)$ | Vanilla RNN forward pass | Single-layer RNN |
| 2 | $\mathbf{h}_t^{(l)} = f\!\left(\mathbf{W}^{(l)}\mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{(l)} + \mathbf{U}^{(l)}\mathbf{h}_t^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}\right)$ | Deep RNN layer-wise update | Stacked RNN architecture |
| 3 | $\mathbf{f}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_f [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f)$ | LSTM forget gate | Gating in LSTM |
| 4 | $\mathbf{i}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_i [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_i)$ | LSTM input gate | Gating in LSTM |
| 5 | $\mathbf{o}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_o [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_o)$ | LSTM output gate | Gating in LSTM |
| 6 | $\tilde{\mathbf{c}}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_c [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_c)$ | LSTM candidate cell state | Gating in LSTM |
| 7 | $\mathbf{c}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{c}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{c}}_t$ | LSTM cell state update | LSTM memory |
| 8 | $\mathbf{h}_t = \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{c}_t)$ | LSTM hidden state | LSTM output |
| 9 | $\mathbf{z}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_z [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t])$ | GRU update gate | GRU |
| 10 | $\mathbf{r}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_r [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t])$ | GRU reset gate | GRU |
| 11 | $\tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W} [\mathbf{r}_t \odot \mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t])$ | GRU candidate hidden state | GRU |
| 12 | $\mathbf{h}_t = (1 - \mathbf{z}_t) \odot \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{z}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t$ | GRU hidden state interpolation | GRU |
| 13 | $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t = \text{RNN}_{\text{fwd}}(\mathbf{x}_t, \overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_{t-1})$ | Bi-RNN forward pass | Bi-RNN |
| 14 | $\overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t = \text{RNN}_{\text{bwd}}(\mathbf{x}_t, \overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_{t+1})$ | Bi-RNN backward pass | Bi-RNN |
| 15 | $\mathbf{y}_t = \mathbf{W}_{hy}[\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t; \overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t] + \mathbf{b}_y$ | Bi-RNN output (concatenation) | Bi-RNN |
| 16 | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathbf{y}_t \log \hat{\mathbf{y}}_t$ | Cross-entropy loss over sequence | Training objective |
| 17 | $\Vert \nabla_{\mathbf{h}_k}\mathcal{L}_t \Vert \leq \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_{hh}) \cdot \mathcal{C}$ | Gradient norm bound (vanilla RNN) | Vanishing/exploding analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation used throughout:** $\sigma(\cdot)$ denotes the **sigmoid** function $\sigma(x) = 1/(1+e^{-x})$; $\tanh(\cdot)$ is the hyperbolic tangent; $\odot$ denotes the **element-wise (Hadamard) product**; $[\mathbf{a}; \mathbf{b}]$ denotes **vector/matrix concatenation**.

## 2.5 Engineering Applications

| **Domain** | **Deep RNN Use Case** | **Architecture** |
|---|---|---|
| Natural Language Processing | Machine translation, sentiment analysis | Encoder–Decoder LSTM/GRU |
| Speech Recognition | Acoustic modeling, ASR | Deep LSTM, CTC loss |
| Music Generation | MIDI sequence composition | Stacked LSTM |
| Time-Series Forecasting | Stock, weather, energy prediction | Deep GRU, Bi-LSTM |
| Video Analysis | Action recognition, captioning | CNN (spatial) + Deep RNN (temporal) |
| Bioinformatics | Protein structure prediction | Bidirectional GRU |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — BPTT Gradient for Vanilla RNN

We will derive the gradient of a per-time-step loss $\mathcal{L}_t$ at time $t$ with respect to the recurrent weight matrix $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$, by tracing the chain rule through the unrolled computation graph from $\mathbf{h}_k$ back to $\mathbf{h}_{t-1}$.

### Setup

Loss at time $t$: $\mathcal{L}_t = \ell(\mathbf{y}_t, \hat{\mathbf{y}}_t)$, where $\mathbf{y}_t = \phi_y(\mathbf{W}_{hy}\mathbf{h}_t + \mathbf{b}_y)$.

Hidden state: $\mathbf{h}_t = \phi_h(\mathbf{a}_t)$, with $\mathbf{a}_t = \mathbf{W}_{xh}\mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{b}_h$.

### Step 1 — Gradient of $\mathcal{L}_t$ w.r.t. output pre-activation $\mathbf{z}_t = \mathbf{W}_{hy}\mathbf{h}_t + \mathbf{b}_y$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{z}_t} = \frac{\partial \ell(\mathbf{y}_t, \hat{\mathbf{y}}_t)}{\partial \mathbf{z}_t} \equiv \boldsymbol{\delta}_t^{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_y}$$

This is simply the standard output-layer error term (e.g., for softmax cross-entropy, $\boldsymbol{\delta}_t^y = \hat{\mathbf{y}}_t - \mathbf{y}_t$).

### Step 2 — Gradient w.r.t. hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t$

Using the chain rule through the output layer:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} = \mathbf{W}_{hy}^{\!\top}\,\boldsymbol{\delta}_t^y \equiv \boldsymbol{\delta}_t^h \in \mathbb{R}^{n_h}$$

This gradient is the *direct* contribution of time $t$'s loss to its own hidden state.

### Step 3 — Gradient w.r.t. an earlier hidden state $\mathbf{h}_k$ ($k < t$)

By the chain rule, the loss at time $t$ depends on $\mathbf{h}_k$ through the *entire chain* of hidden states $\mathbf{h}_{k+1}, \mathbf{h}_{k+2}, \ldots, \mathbf{h}_t$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} = \sum_{i=k+1}^{t}\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_i}\cdot\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_i}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{i-1}}\cdot\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_{i-1}}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k}\;\;\;\text{(chain rule)}$$

More cleanly, writing $\boldsymbol{\delta}_i^h = \partial \mathcal{L}_t / \partial \mathbf{h}_i$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} = \sum_{i=k+1}^{t}\boldsymbol{\delta}_i^h \cdot \prod_{j=k+1}^{i}\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_j}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{j-1}}$$

The Jacobian $\partial \mathbf{h}_j / \partial \mathbf{h}_{j-1}$ for the vanilla RNN is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_j}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{j-1}} = \text{diag}\!\left(\phi_h'(\mathbf{a}_j)\right)\,\mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\!\top}$$

### Step 4 — Product of Jacobians (the source of vanishing/exploding)

Define $\mathbf{J}_j = \text{diag}\!\left(\phi_h'(\mathbf{a}_j)\right)\,\mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\!\top}$. The full back-propagated term is:

$$\prod_{j=k+1}^{i}\mathbf{J}_j = \prod_{j=k+1}^{i}\text{diag}\!\left(\phi_h'(\mathbf{a}_j)\right)\,\mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\!\top}$$

**Bounding the norm** using submultiplicativity and the fact that $\vert \phi_h'(\cdot) \vert \leq 1$ for $\tanh$ and $\sigma$:

$$\left\Vert \prod_{j=k+1}^{i}\mathbf{J}_j \right\Vert \;\leq\; \prod_{j=k+1}^{i} \left\Vert \text{diag}(\phi_h'(\mathbf{a}_j))\right\Vert \cdot \left\Vert \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\!\top}\right\Vert \;\leq\; \sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_{hh})^{\,i-k}$$

**Conclusion:** If $\sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_{hh}) < 1$, the gradient vanishes like $\sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_{hh})^{i-k}$. If $\sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_{hh}) > 1$, the gradient explodes exponentially.

### Step 5 — Gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ (final accumulation)

After computing $\partial \mathcal{L}_t / \partial \mathbf{h}_k$ for every $k < t$, the gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ is the sum of per-time-step contributions:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{t-1}\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_k}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{t-1}\text{diag}\!\left(\phi_h'(\mathbf{a}_k)\right)\,\boldsymbol{\delta}_k^h\,\mathbf{h}_{k-1}^{\!\top}$$

This is the full BPTT gradient, summed across the entire unrolled time horizon.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Vanilla RNN vs LSTM vs GRU

| **Property** | **Vanilla RNN** | **LSTM** | **GRU** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of gating mechanisms | 0 | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (reset, update) |
| Memory mechanism | Single hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t$ | Cell state $\mathbf{c}_t$ + hidden $\mathbf{h}_t$ | Single hidden state with gating |
| Total parameter count per cell | $n_h^2 + n_h n_x + n_h$ | $4(n_h^2 + n_h n_x + n_h)$ | $3(n_h^2 + n_h n_x + n_h)$ |
| Gradient highway | No (vanishes) | Yes (via $\mathbf{f}_t$ additive path) | Partial (via $\mathbf{z}_t$ interpolation) |
| Vanishing gradient resistance | Poor | Strong | Moderate |
| Computational cost (per step) | Lowest | Highest (4× vanilla) | Moderate (3× vanilla) |
| Long-term dependency modeling | Limited | Excellent | Good |
| Training stability | Lowest | Highest | Moderate |
| Best use case | Short sequences, toy problems | Long sequences, production NLP | Resource-constrained, medium sequences |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Forward Pass in a 2-Layer Stacked LSTM

**Given:**
- Sequence length $T = 3$
- Input dimension $n_x = 2$, hidden dimension $n_h = 3$, number of layers $L = 2$
- Input sequence: $\mathbf{x}_1 = [1, 0]^{\!\top}$, $\mathbf{x}_2 = [0, 1]^{\!\top}$, $\mathbf{x}_3 = [1, 1]^{\!\top}$
- Initial states: $\mathbf{h}_0^{(1)} = \mathbf{h}_0^{(2)} = \mathbf{0}$, $\mathbf{c}_0^{(1)} = \mathbf{c}_0^{(2)} = \mathbf{0}$

**Compute $\mathbf{h}_1^{(1)}, \mathbf{c}_1^{(1)}$ (Layer 1, time 1) using the LSTM equations:**

$$\mathbf{f}_1^{(1)} = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}_f^{(1)}[\mathbf{h}_0^{(1)}; \mathbf{x}_1] + \mathbf{b}_f^{(1)}\right) = \sigma(\mathbf{b}_f^{(1)})$$

$$\mathbf{i}_1^{(1)} = \sigma(\mathbf{b}_i^{(1)}), \quad \mathbf{o}_1^{(1)} = \sigma(\mathbf{b}_o^{(1)}), \quad \tilde{\mathbf{c}}_1^{(1)} = \tanh(\mathbf{b}_c^{(1)})$$

$$\mathbf{c}_1^{(1)} = \mathbf{f}_1^{(1)} \odot \mathbf{c}_0^{(1)} + \mathbf{i}_1^{(1)} \odot \tilde{\mathbf{c}}_1^{(1)} = \mathbf{i}_1^{(1)} \odot \tanh(\mathbf{b}_c^{(1)})$$

$$\mathbf{h}_1^{(1)} = \mathbf{o}_1^{(1)} \odot \tanh(\mathbf{c}_1^{(1)})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's note:** Students often mistakenly use $\mathbf{x}_1$ to compute $\mathbf{h}_1^{(2)}$ directly — but $\mathbf{h}_1^{(2)}$ takes $\mathbf{h}_1^{(1)}$ (the layer-1 output) as input, not $\mathbf{x}_1$. This is the *vertical* recurrence in deep RNNs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Unrolled Deep RNN Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    x1["x_1"]:::input --> n1a["h_1_layer1"]
    x2["x_2"]:::input --> n2a["h_2_layer1"]
    x3["x_3"]:::input --> n3a["h_3_layer1"]
    x4["x_4"]:::input --> n4a["h_4_layer1"]

    n1a --> n2a
    n2a --> n3a
    n3a --> n4a

    n1a --> n1b["h_1_layer2"]
    n2a --> n2b["h_2_layer2"]
    n3a --> n3b["h_3_layer2"]
    n4a --> n4b["h_4_layer2"]

    n1b --> n2b
    n2b --> n3b
    n3b --> n4b

    n1b --> y1["y_1"]:::output
    n2b --> y2["y_2"]:::output
    n3b --> y3["y_3"]:::output
    n4b --> y4["y_4"]:::output

    classDef input fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hidden fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Bottom-up arrows represent *vertical stacking* (layer 1 → layer 2). Left-to-right arrows represent *temporal recurrence* ($t-1 \rightarrow t$). The yellow "input" nodes feed into layer 1; only layer 2's outputs feed the final predictions.

## 4.2 LSTM Cell — Internal Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    inX["x_t"]:::input
    inH["h_t_minus_1"]:::input
    inC["c_t_minus_1"]:::state

    inX --> concatA["Concat"]
    inH --> concatA
    concatA --> fg["f_t sigmoid Forget Gate"]:::gate
    concatA --> ig["i_t sigmoid Input Gate"]:::gate
    concatA --> og["o_t sigmoid Output Gate"]:::gate
    concatA --> cand["tanh Candidate c_tilde_t"]:::gate

    fg --> mulA["Multiply"]
    inC --> mulA
    ig --> mulB["Multiply"]
    cand --> mulB
    mulA --> addA["Add"]
    mulB --> addA
    addA --> cnew["c_t New Cell State"]:::state
    cnew --> tanhA["tanh"]
    cnew --> mulC["Multiply"]
    og --> mulC
    tanhA --> mulC
    mulC --> hnew["h_t New Hidden State"]:::output

    hnew --> out["Output h_t"]:::output
    cnew --> outC["Output c_t"]:::state

    classDef input fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px
    classDef gate fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px
    classDef state fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 GRU Cell — Internal Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    gx["x_t"]:::input
    gh["h_t_minus_1"]:::input
    gconcat["Concat x_t and h_t_minus_1"]:::gate
    gx --> gconcat
    gh --> gconcat
    gconcat --> greset["r_t sigmoid Reset Gate"]:::gate
    gconcat --> gupdate["z_t sigmoid Update Gate"]:::gate

    greset --> gmulA["Multiply with h_t_minus_1"]
    gh --> gmulA
    gx --> gconcat2["Concat"]
    gmulA --> gconcat2
    gconcat2 --> gcand["tanh Candidate h_tilde_t"]:::gate

    gupdate --> gmulB["Multiply with h_tilde_t"]
    gcand --> gmulB
    gupdate --> gmulC["Multiply with h_t_minus_1"]
    gh --> gmulC
    gmulB --> gadd["Add"]
    gmulC --> gadd
    gadd --> hout["h_t New Hidden State"]:::output
    hout --> gout["Output h_t"]:::output

```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Deep Stacked Bidirectional LSTM

```mermaid
graph TB
    rawInput["Raw Input Sequence X x_1 to x_T"]:::input
    embed["Embedding / Feature Extraction Layer"]:::process
    fwd1["Forward LSTM Layer 1"]:::lstm
    bwd1["Backward LSTM Layer 1"]:::lstm
    fwd2["Forward LSTM Layer 2"]:::lstm
    bwd2["Backward LSTM Layer 2"]:::lstm
    concat1["Concatenation Layer"]:::process
    concat2["Concatenation Layer"]:::process
    attn["Optional Attention Layer"]:::process
    dense["Fully Connected Dense Layer"]:::process
    softmax["Softmax Output"]:::output

    rawInput --> embed
    embed --> fwd1
    embed --> bwd1
    fwd1 --> concat1
    bwd1 --> concat1
    concat1 --> fwd2
    concat1 --> bwd2
    fwd2 --> concat2
    bwd2 --> concat2
    concat2 --> attn
    attn --> dense
    dense --> softmax

    classDef input fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px
    classDef process fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px
    classDef lstm fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the functional architecture:** Input → Embedding → Bi-LSTM Layer 1 → Bi-LSTM Layer 2 → Attention → Dense → Softmax. The two parallel LSTM cells in each layer (forward + backward) enable the network to use both past and future context at every time step — a critical feature for tasks like POS tagging and named-entity recognition.
