<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN). Why are standard RNNs described as having a "memory"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** is a class of neural networks designed to process sequential data by maintaining an internal hidden state that is recursively updated at each time step using the current input and the previous hidden state. Mathematically, $\mathbf{h}_t = \phi(\mathbf{W}_{xh}\mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{b}_h)$.

RNNs are said to possess "memory" because the hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t$ is a function of all previous inputs $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_t$ through the recursive dependence on $\mathbf{h}_{t-1}$. This gives the network a compact summary of historical context, which can influence future predictions — analogous to how human memory informs current decisions. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for memory explanation)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the vanishing gradient problem in deep RNNs? Name ONE architecture that mitigates it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **vanishing gradient problem** in deep RNNs occurs during Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) when the gradient of the loss with respect to earlier time-step weights shrinks exponentially as it is propagated backward through many time steps. This is because the gradient involves a product of recurrent Jacobians, whose norm is bounded by $\sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_{hh})^{t-k}$. When $\sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_{hh}) < 1$, this product decays rapidly, making it impossible for the network to learn long-range dependencies. **(2 marks)**

**LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory)** networks mitigate this by introducing an additive cell state update $\mathbf{c}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{c}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{c}}_t$, which provides a near-linear "gradient highway" via the forget gate $\mathbf{f}_t$. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A — Option 1 [14 Marks Total]

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> **(a)** Derive the forward-pass equations for a standard RNN and a single-layer LSTM. Clearly state the role of each gate. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the vanishing gradient problem in vanilla RNNs with mathematical justification. Show how LSTM cell-state update mitigates this. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part A(a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Vanilla RNN Forward Equations (2 marks):**

The vanilla RNN processes a sequence $\{\mathbf{x}_t\}_{t=1}^{T}$ using the update:

$$\mathbf{a}_t = \mathbf{W}_{xh}\mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{b}_h, \quad \mathbf{h}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{a}_t), \quad \mathbf{y}_t = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{W}_{hy}\mathbf{h}_t + \mathbf{b}_y)$$

The hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t$ acts as a lossy summary of all past inputs.

**Step 2 — LSTM Equations and Gate Roles (5 marks):**

The LSTM cell at time $t$ uses input $\mathbf{x}_t$, previous hidden state $\mathbf{h}_{t-1}$, and previous cell state $\mathbf{c}_{t-1}$:

$$\mathbf{f}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_f[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}; \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f) \quad \textbf{(Forget Gate — decides what to erase from memory)}$$

$$\mathbf{i}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_i[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}; \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_i) \quad \textbf{(Input Gate — decides what new info to store)}$$

$$\tilde{\mathbf{c}}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_c[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}; \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_c) \quad \textbf{(Candidate memory — new info to add)}$$

$$\mathbf{o}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_o[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}; \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_o) \quad \textbf{(Output Gate — decides what to expose from memory)}$$

$$\mathbf{c}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{c}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{c}}_t, \quad \mathbf{h}_t = \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{c}_t)$$

Each gate uses sigmoid $\sigma \in [0,1]$ for *soft* selection; the candidate uses $\tanh \in [-1,1]$.

---

#### Part A(b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Mathematical Form of the Gradient (3 marks):**

During BPTT, the gradient of the loss $\mathcal{L}_t$ with respect to an earlier hidden state $\mathbf{h}_k$ ($k < t$) requires the product of recurrent Jacobians:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}\prod_{i=k+1}^{t}\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_i}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{i-1}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}\prod_{i=k+1}^{t}\text{diag}(\phi'(\mathbf{a}_i))\,\mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\!\top}$$

Taking the norm and using submultiplicativity:

$$\left\Vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} \right\Vert \;\leq\; \left\Vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} \right\Vert \prod_{i=k+1}^{t}\sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_{hh}) \cdot \underbrace{\Vert \text{diag}(\phi'(\mathbf{a}_i))\Vert}_{\leq 1}$$

This is bounded above by $\sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_{hh})^{t-k} \cdot \mathcal{C}$. If $\sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_{hh}) < 1$, the product **decays exponentially** in $(t-k)$, vanishing. If $\sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_{hh}) > 1$, it **explodes exponentially**. **(3 marks)**

**Step 2 — LSTM Mitigation (4 marks):**

In the LSTM, the analogous "memory carrier" is the cell state $\mathbf{c}_t$, updated additively:

$$\mathbf{c}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{c}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{c}}_t$$

The gradient back-flow is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathbf{c}_t}{\partial \mathbf{c}_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(\mathbf{f}_t) \quad \text{(a diagonal matrix with forget-gate values)}$$

Since $\mathbf{f}_t \in [0, 1]$ element-wise, the gradient product across $T$ steps is $\prod_{t}\text{diag}(\mathbf{f}_t)$, whose magnitude is $\prod_t \mathbf{f}_t$. If the forget gate is close to **1** (typical after training for long-term dependencies), the gradient **passes through nearly unchanged** — this is the **gradient highway**. Combined with the additive update, the gradient is much less prone to vanishing or exploding. **(4 marks)**

---

### Question B — Option 2 [14 Marks Total]

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> **(a)** With a neat architecture diagram, explain the working of a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU). Compare it with LSTM. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** What is a Bidirectional Deep RNN? Explain its architecture and state two real-world applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part B(a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — GRU Architecture (4 marks):**

A GRU uses **two gates**: the **update gate** $\mathbf{z}_t$ and the **reset gate** $\mathbf{r}_t$, computed from the previous hidden state and current input:

$$\mathbf{z}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_z[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}; \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_z) \quad \textbf{(Update Gate — interpolates between old and new state)}$$

$$\mathbf{r}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_r[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}; \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_r) \quad \textbf{(Reset Gate — controls how much past info to forget)}$$

$$\tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W}[\mathbf{r}_t \odot \mathbf{h}_{t-1}; \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}) \quad \textbf{(Candidate hidden state)}$$

$$\mathbf{h}_t = (1 - \mathbf{z}_t)\odot \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{z}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t$$

The reset gate multiplies $\mathbf{h}_{t-1}$ element-wise before the candidate computation, allowing the network to "forget" past context on demand. The update gate acts as a convex combination between the old hidden state and the new candidate — when $\mathbf{z}_t \approx 0$, the previous state is preserved nearly intact (analogous to LSTM's forget gate being 1).

**Step 2 — Comparison with LSTM (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **LSTM** | **GRU** |
|---|---|---|
| Number of gates | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (reset, update) |
| Memory carriers | Cell state $\mathbf{c}_t$ + hidden $\mathbf{h}_t$ | Single hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t$ |
| Parameters | $4(n_h^2 + n_h n_x + n_h)$ | $3(n_h^2 + n_h n_x + n_h)$ |
| Output control | Explicit output gate | Implicit (via $\mathbf{z}_t$ interpolation) |
| Performance on long sequences | Slightly better | Slightly faster, comparable |
| Use when | Long sequences, max accuracy | Limited compute, faster training needed |

---

#### Part B(b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**

A **Bidirectional Deep RNN (Bi-RNN)** is a recurrent architecture that processes the input sequence in **both** forward and backward directions using two separate recurrent layers (one for each direction), and combines their outputs (typically by concatenation or summation). This allows the model at time $t$ to use information from *both past* ($t' < t$) and *future* ($t' > t$) context.

**Step 2 — Architecture (3 marks):**

For a Bi-RNN with $L$ layers:

$$\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t^{(l)} = f\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{f}^{(l)}\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_{t-1}^{(l)} + \mathbf{U}_{f}^{(l)}\mathbf{h}_t^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}_f^{(l)}\right) \quad \text{(Forward pass)}$$

$$\overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t^{(l)} = f\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{b}^{(l)}\overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_{t+1}^{(l)} + \mathbf{U}_{b}^{(l)}\mathbf{h}_t^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}_b^{(l)}\right) \quad \text{(Backward pass)}$$

Final output at time $t$:

$$\mathbf{y}_t = \mathbf{W}_{hy}\!\left[\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t^{(L)}; \overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t^{(L)}\right] + \mathbf{b}_y$$

**Step 3 — Real-World Applications (2 marks):**

1. **Named Entity Recognition (NER):** Classifying each word in a sentence benefits from looking at both preceding and following words (e.g., "Washington" can be a *person* or *location* — disambiguation requires context on both sides).
2. **Protein Structure Prediction:** Amino acid sequences are bidirectional by nature — secondary structure depends on both N-terminal and C-terminal context. Deep Bi-LSTMs are state-of-the-art on benchmarks like ProteinNet.
3. *(Honorable mention)* Speech recognition, POS tagging, machine translation evaluation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Valuation Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Deep" with "Unrolled" (–2 marks):** Many students write "a deep RNN is one unrolled over many time steps." **This is wrong.** Depth = stacked layers; time unrolling = BPTT computational trick. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 2. **Wrong gate dimensioning in LSTM (–1 to –2 marks):** Forgetting to write the bias terms $\mathbf{b}_f, \mathbf{b}_i, \mathbf{b}_o, \mathbf{b}_c$ in LSTM equations. KTU expects the *full* equation.
>
> 3. **Missing concatenation notation:** Writing $\mathbf{W}\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{W}\mathbf{x}_t$ instead of the standard $\mathbf{W}[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}; \mathbf{x}_t]$. The concatenation form is what KTU model answers use.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the additive gradient-highway argument:** In LSTM vanishing-gradient answers, students often only mention "LSTM solves vanishing gradient" without showing the **additive** cell-state update $\mathbf{c}_t = \mathbf{f}_t\odot\mathbf{c}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t\odot\tilde{\mathbf{c}}_t$ as the *mechanism*. KTU rewards *mechanistic* explanations.
>
> 5. **Not specifying the activation functions:** Always state $\sigma$ for gates and $\tanh$ for candidates/hidden states in vanilla RNN. Marks are deducted for vague "activation function $f$" without specification.
>
> 6. **Confusing GRU update gate with LSTM input gate:** These are *not* the same. GRU's update gate is a *convex combination* controller between old and new state; LSTM's input gate is a *modulator* of new candidate content.
>
> 7. **BPTT derivation skips the Jacobian product step (–2 to –3 marks):** The full proof must show $\prod_{i=k+1}^{t}\mathbf{J}_i$ explicitly and bound it with $\sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_{hh})^{t-k}$. Skipping this loses majority of marks in Part B.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist — Deep Recurrent Networks**

- [x] **RNN Definition:** A neural network that maintains a hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t$ recursively depending on $\mathbf{h}_{t-1}$ and $\mathbf{x}_t$ — enables sequence modeling.
- [x] **Vanilla RNN equations:** $\mathbf{h}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_{xh}\mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{b}_h)$.
- [x] **Deep RNN:** Multiple recurrent layers stacked vertically. Layer $l$ receives $\mathbf{h}_t^{(l-1)}$ (vertical) and $\mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{(l)}$ (temporal) as inputs.
- [x] **BPTT:** Backprop through the *unrolled* computational graph across $T$ time steps.
- [x] **Vanishing/Exploding Gradient:** Caused by $\sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_{hh})^{t-k}$ factor in BPTT gradient product.
- [x] **LSTM:** 3 gates (forget $\mathbf{f}_t$, input $\mathbf{i}_t$, output $\mathbf{o}_t$) + additive cell state update $\mathbf{c}_t = \mathbf{f}_t\odot\mathbf{c}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t\odot\tilde{\mathbf{c}}_t$ — gradient highway.
- [x] **GRU:** 2 gates (reset $\mathbf{r}_t$, update $\mathbf{z}_t$); fewer parameters than LSTM; comparable performance.
- [x] **Bidirectional RNN:** Forward + backward recurrence; combines past and future context. Cannot be used for online/causal prediction.
- [x] **Notation:** $\sigma$ = sigmoid; $\tanh$ = hyperbolic tangent; $\odot$ = element-wise product; $[\cdot;\cdot]$ = concatenation.
- [x] **Parameter counts:** Vanilla RNN = $n_h^2 + n_h n_x + n_h$; LSTM = **4×** vanilla; GRU = **3×** vanilla.
- [x] **Common application areas:** NLP (translation, NER, sentiment), speech recognition, time-series forecasting, video analysis, bioinformatics.
- [x] **Depth vs. Time:** Depth = number of stacked layers; Time = number of unrolled steps. **NOT the same.**
- [x] **Mitigations for vanishing gradient:** LSTM, GRU, gradient clipping, orthogonal weight initialization, skip connections.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** In Part B answers, **always** draw a labeled block diagram (even a hand-drawn sketch) of the architecture you are explaining. KTU examiners award **1–2 free marks** for a clear, correctly-labeled diagram showing input → cells → gates → output flow.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recursive Recurrent Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT503 - DEEP LEARNING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Recursive Recurrent Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Recursive Neural Network (RecNN)** is a class of deep learning architecture designed to process data with **hierarchical, tree-like, or recursive structures** by applying a shared set of weights recursively over the structural input. Unlike standard Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) that operate over **linear sequences** (chain-structured data), Recursive Networks traverse the input in a **top-down or bottom-up tree traversal** order, combining child representations through a composition function to form parent representations.

Formally, given two child node representations $x_1$ and $x_2$, a recursive network computes a parent representation $p$ as:

$$p = f(W \cdot [x_1; x_2] + b)$$

where $W$ is a shared weight matrix, $b$ is a bias vector, and $f$ is a non-linear activation function (typically $\tanh$ or ReLU). The same parameters are reused at every node in the parse tree, enabling generalization across variable-sized structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Recursive Recurrent Networks" specifically refers to the unification of recursive compositionality (tree-based) with recurrent dynamics (sequential memory). This hybrid paradigm is foundational to models like **Recursive Neural Tensor Networks (RNTN)** and **Tree-LSTMs**, which are central exam topics in Module 3.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Family Tree of Words"**
>
> Imagine you are trying to judge whether a movie review like *"The movie was not particularly great"* is positive or negative. A standard RNN reads this left-to-right like a conveyor belt, and by the time it reaches the end, it may have already "forgotten" the crucial negation *"not"*.
>
> A Recursive Recurrent Network, in contrast, reads the sentence like a **linguist parsing a sentence grammar tree**. It first combines *"not"* and *"particularly"* into a phrase, then merges that with *"great"*, and finally composes everything with *"The movie was"*. At each merge, it asks: *"What is the overall sentiment of these combined parts?"* This **hierarchical composition** mirrors how humans actually understand language — through nested phrase structure, not just word order.
>
> The "Recurrent" part adds memory: as the network builds up the tree, it carries information from previously processed subtrees, similar to how your understanding of the first half of a novel influences your reading of the second half.

### 1.3 Key Distinguishing Parameters

The following metrics and structural constants are standard in KTU board examinations for this topic:

- **Tree Depth ($D$):** Maximum distance from root to leaf in the parse tree.
- **Branching Factor ($k$):** Number of children per node (typically **2** for binary trees).
- **Composition Function ($f$):** Usually parameterized as a single-layer neural network.
- **Hidden Dimensionality ($n$):** Size of the representation vector at each node — typically **$n = 25$ to $200$** in practice.
- **Leaf Embeddings:** Word vectors of dimension $\vert V \vert$ projected to $n$ via an embedding matrix $L \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times \vert V \vert}$.

### 1.4 Recursive vs. Recurrent — The Critical Distinction

| Aspect | Recurrent Network (RNN) | Recursive Network (RecNN) |
|---|---|---|
| **Input Structure** | Linear sequence | Tree / Hierarchy |
| **Traversal Order** | Left-to-right (time steps) | Bottom-up / Top-down (tree order) |
| **State Transition** | $h_t = f(h_{t-1}, x_t)$ | $p = f(x_{left}, x_{right})$ |
| **Dependency** | Sequential / Markovian | Structural / Compositional |
| **Best Use Case** | Speech, time-series | NLP parsing, sentiment, scene parsing |

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

A Recursive Recurrent Network operates in three distinct phases:

**Phase 1 — Leaf Initialization (Word Embedding Stage)**
- Each leaf node $w_i$ of the input tree is mapped to a dense vector $a_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$ using an embedding matrix $L$.
- This is the entry point where raw symbolic input (words, characters, image patches) becomes continuous.

**Phase 2 — Recursive Composition (Bottom-Up Tree Traversal)**
- For every internal node with children $a_{left}$ and $a_{right}$, a parent vector $p$ is computed via a learned composition function.
- The composition function shares the **same parameters across all nodes**, enforcing the inductive bias that "combining meaning works the same way everywhere."
- For Recursive Recurrent Networks, the parent representation is also passed to sibling/ancestor nodes through gated recurrences (as in **Tree-LSTM**), creating a "recurrent" flow within the tree.

**Phase 3 — Top Prediction / Label Assignment**
- The root node's final representation is fed into a classifier (softmax layer) to produce predictions.
- For tasks like sentiment analysis, a soft classification label $\hat{y} \in [0,1]^C$ is output at the root.
- For tasks like parse reranking, scores are assigned to candidate subtrees.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Reference Table for Board Exams — Memorize Before Exam Day**

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_i = L \cdot e_{w_i}$ | Leaf embedding | Word-to-vector conversion |
| 2 | $p = f(W \begin{bmatrix} a_{left} \\ a_{right} \end{bmatrix} + b)$ | Standard composition | Basic Recursive NN |
| 3 | $p = f \left( \begin{bmatrix} a_{left} \\ a_{right} \end{bmatrix}^T V \begin{bmatrix} a_{left} \\ a_{right} \end{bmatrix} + W \begin{bmatrix} a_{left} \\ a_{right} \end{bmatrix} \right)$ | Tensor composition | RNTN (Socher et al.) |
| 4 | $h_t = f(U \cdot h_{t-1} + W \cdot x_t)$ | Vanilla RNN update | Recurrent memory |
| 5 | $\hat{y} = \text{softmax}(W_s \cdot p_{root} + b_s)$ | Final classification | Sentiment / Label prediction |
| 6 | $E = -\sum_i y_i \log \hat{y}_i$ | Cross-entropy loss | Training objective |
| 7 | $\delta^{(l)} = \left( (W^{(l)})^T \delta^{(l+1)} \right) \odot f'(z^{(l)})$ | Backprop at node $l$ | BTS gradient flow |
| 8 | $\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial W} = \sum_{j \in \text{nodes}} \delta_j \cdot [a_{left}; a_{right}]_j^T$ | Parameter gradient | Weight update via BTS |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Note for Students:** When writing $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) in your exam paper, always use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to avoid the table-breaking issue. Similarly, conditional probability $\Pr(y \mid x)$ uses the spacing command.

### 2.3 Why Recursive Recurrent Networks? — The Engineering Rationale

1. **Linguistic Plausibility:** Natural language has a recursive structure — clauses contain clauses. RNNs are mathematically incapable of capturing this with finite hidden states (Chomsky hierarchy argument).
2. **Long-Range Dependency Mitigation:** In a sentence of length $L$, RNNs require $O(L)$ steps to connect the first and last word, causing gradient vanishing. A tree of depth $\log_2 L$ reduces this to $O(\log L)$.
3. **Compositional Semantics:** The principle of **Frege's compositionality** (meaning of a whole is a function of the meaning of its parts) is directly encoded in the architecture.
4. **Variable-Length Input Handling:** Trees of different sizes can be processed without padding or truncation, unlike sequence models.

### 2.4 Backpropagation Through Structure (BTS)

The training algorithm for Recursive Recurrent Networks is **Backpropagation Through Structure (BTS)**, proposed by Goller & Küchler (1996). Unlike standard backpropagation through time (BPTT) which unrolls a chain, BTS unrolls the **computation graph as a tree** and applies the chain rule from the root downward, summing gradients across all branches.

The key insight is that the gradient at any internal node depends on the gradients of **all** its descendants in the tree — a stark contrast to the strictly local dependency in BPTT.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forward Pass of a Standard Recursive Neural Network

We derive the full forward computation for a binary tree with root $r$, left child $L$, and right child $R$.

**Step 1: Convert leaf words to vectors.**
Let $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$ be the leaf words. Each word $w_i$ has a one-hot encoding $e_{w_i} \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert}$ where $\vert V \vert$ is the vocabulary size. The leaf representation is:

$$a_i = L \cdot e_{w_i} \in \mathbb{R}^n$$

This says: the embedding matrix $L \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times \vert V \vert}$ picks out the $i$-th column corresponding to word $w_i$.

**Step 2: Compose children at the lowest internal node.**

For the leftmost internal node combining $a_1$ and $a_2$, we form the concatenated vector:

$$[a_1; a_2] \in \mathbb{R}^{2n}$$

Apply the linear transformation:

$$z_1 = W \cdot [a_1; a_2] + b \in \mathbb{R}^n$$

Apply the non-linearity:

$$p_1 = \tanh(z_1) \in \mathbb{R}^n$$

**Step 3: Compose the right sibling node similarly.**

$$p_2 = \tanh(W \cdot [a_3; a_4] + b)$$

**Step 4: Compose the root node from $p_1$ and $p_2$.**

$$p_{root} = \tanh(W \cdot [p_1; p_2] + b)$$

**Step 5: Compute the softmax output at the root.**

$$\hat{y} = \text{softmax}(W_s \cdot p_{root} + b_s) \in \mathbb{R}^C$$

The softmax is defined as:

$$\hat{y}_k = \frac{\exp((W_s \cdot p_{root} + b_s)_k)}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} \exp((W_s \cdot p_{root} + b_s)_j)}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Backpropagation Through Structure (BTS) Gradients

We derive the gradient of the cross-entropy loss $E$ with respect to the composition matrix $W$.

**Step 1: Loss at the root.**

$$E = -\sum_{k=1}^{C} y_k \log \hat{y}_k$$

The gradient w.r.t. the pre-softmax activations $o = W_s p_{root} + b_s$ is the standard softmax-cross-entropy derivative:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial o} = \hat{y} - y \in \mathbb{R}^C$$

**Step 2: Gradient flowing into the root representation $p_{root}$.**

$$\delta_{root} = W_s^T \cdot \frac{\partial E}{\partial o} \in \mathbb{R}^n$$

**Step 3: Gradient w.r.t. the pre-activation $z_{root}$.**

$$\delta_{z_{root}} = \delta_{root} \odot (1 - p_{root}^2)$$

This uses the fact that $\tanh'(x) = 1 - \tanh^2(x)$.

**Step 4: Contribution of root to the gradient of $W$.**

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial W} \Big|_{root} = \delta_{z_{root}} \cdot [p_1; p_2]^T \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 2n}$$

**Step 5: Recurse into left child $p_1$.**

The gradient flowing into $p_1$ via the root's linear transformation is:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial p_1} = (W^T \cdot \delta_{z_{root}})_{1:n}$$

where the subscript $1:n$ denotes the first $n$ components (those corresponding to the left child in the concatenation).

**Step 6: Continue recursing through the tree.**

For node $p_1$, the pre-activation is $z_1 = W \cdot [a_1; a_2] + b$, so:

$$\delta_{z_1} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial p_1} \odot (1 - p_1^2)$$

**Step 7: Accumulate gradients across all internal nodes.**

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial W} = \sum_{j \in \text{internal nodes}} \delta_{z_j} \cdot [a_{left}^{(j)}; a_{right}^{(j)}]^T$$

**Step 8: Gradient w.r.t. embeddings (for fine-tuning the embedding matrix $L$).**

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial L_{:, w_i}} = \sum_{j: a_i \text{ is child of } j} \frac{\partial E}{\partial a_i}\Big|_{j}$$

**Step 9: Parameter update via gradient descent.**

$$W \leftarrow W - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial E}{\partial W}$$

where $\eta$ is the learning rate.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Recursive Neural Tensor Network (RNTN) Composition

The RNTN uses a tensor $V \in \mathbb{R}^{2n \times 2n \times n}$ to directly model multiplicative interactions between children.

**Step 1: Tensor bilinear form.**

$$\begin{aligned}
h & = f \left( \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \end{bmatrix}^T V^{[1..n]} \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \end{bmatrix} + W \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \end{bmatrix} + b \right)
\end{aligned}$$

Here, $V^{[k]} \in \mathbb{R}^{2n \times 2n}$ is the $k$-th slice of the tensor, producing the $k$-th component of the output.

**Step 2: Expanded scalar form (component $k$).**

$$h_k = f\left( \sum_{i=1}^{2n} \sum_{j=1}^{2n} V_{ij}^{[k]} \cdot x_i \cdot x_j + \sum_{i=1}^{2n} W_{ik} \cdot x_i + b_k \right)$$

where $x = [a_1; a_2]$.

**Step 3: Why this is more expressive than standard composition.**

The bilinear term $x_i x_j$ introduces **explicit multiplicative interactions** between child dimensions. This means the network can model phenomena like **negation** (where the meaning of *"not good"* is *not* the sum of meanings of *"not"* and *"good"*). The standard additive composition $W [a_1; a_2]$ cannot capture such non-linear semantic interactions.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Recurrent Component in Tree-LSTM

To make a Recursive network truly "recurrent," we introduce gated memory cells as in the **Tree-LSTM** architecture (Tai et al., 2015).

**Step 1: Input gate.**

$$i = \sigma(W^{(i)} x_L + U^{(i)} x_R + b^{(i)})$$

**Step 2: Forget gates (one per child).**

$$f_L = \sigma(W^{(f_L)} x_L + U^{(f_L)} x_R + b^{(f_L)})$$
$$f_R = \sigma(W^{(f_R)} x_L + U^{(f_R)} x_R + b^{(f_R)})$$

**Step 3: Output gate.**

$$o = \sigma(W^{(o)} x_L + U^{(o)} x_R + b^{(o)})$$

**Step 4: Candidate cell state.**

$$\tilde{c} = \tanh(W^{(c)} x_L + U^{(c)} x_R + b^{(c)})$$

**Step 5: Cell state update (the "recurrent memory" transition).**

$$c = i \odot \tilde{c} + f_L \odot c_L + f_R \odot c_R$$

**Step 6: Hidden state output.**

$$h = o \odot \tanh(c)$$

This formulation generalizes the LSTM's sequential memory to a **tree-structured memory**, where the cell state aggregates information from both children through learnable forget gates — a key feature that earns the architecture the "Recursive **Recurrent**" designation.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture Flow Diagram — Recursive Network on a Sentence

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how a Recursive Neural Network composes the sentence *"Not very good"* through a binary parse tree. Node identifiers use alphanumeric prefixes (not reserved keywords) to ensure correct rendering.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ROOT NODE p_root] --> B[LEFT SUBTREE p1]
    A --> C[RIGHT SUBTREE p2]
    B --> D[LEAF a1: Not]
    B --> E[LEAF a2: very]
    C --> F[LEAF a3: good]
    C --> G[PADDING a4: null]
    H[EMBEDDING MATRIX L] --> D
    H --> E
    H --> F
    H --> G
    D --> I[COMPOSITION f W b]
    E --> I
    I --> B
    F --> J[COMPOSITION f W b]
    G --> J
    J --> C
    B --> K[COMPOSITION f W b]
    C --> K
    K --> A
    A --> L[SOFTMAX CLASSIFIER Ws bs]
    L --> M[OUTPUT: sentiment label]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Leaves `a1`, `a2`, `a3`, `a4` are the input word embeddings produced by matrix `L`.
- Internal composition blocks (labeled `COMPOSITION f W b`) apply the **same** weights $W$ and biases $b$ at every merge.
- The softmax classifier maps the root representation to a sentiment class.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following diagram shows the training and inference pipeline of a Recursive Recurrent Network, including the BTS gradient flow.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[INPUT SENTENCE] --> A2[CONSTITUTE PARSE TREE]
    A2 --> A3[LEAF EMBEDDING L]
    A3 --> A4[BOTTOM UP TRAVERSAL]
    A4 --> A5[COMPOSITION FUNCTION f W b]
    A5 --> A6[ROOT REPRESENTATION]
    A6 --> A7[SOFTMAX CLASSIFIER]
    A7 --> A8[PREDICTION]
    A8 --> A9[LOSS COMPUTATION]
    A9 --> A10[BTS GRADIENT]
    A10 --> A5
    A10 --> A3
    B1[GROUND TRUTH LABEL] --> A9
```

### 4.3 Comparison Schematic: RNN vs. RecNN Computation Graph

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph SEQUENTIAL RNN
        R1[X1] --> R2[H1]
        R2 --> R3[H2]
        R3 --> R4[H3]
        R4 --> R5[H4]
    end
    subgraph TREE RECURSIVE NN
        T1[LEAF A] --> T2[NODE P1]
        T1B[LEAF B] --> T2
        T3[LEAF C] --> T4[NODE P2]
        T3B[LEAF D] --> T4
        T2 --> T5[ROOT P]
        T4 --> T5
    end
```

**Visual Interpretation:** The RNN forms a chain of length $L$, while the RecNN forms a balanced binary tree of depth $\log_2 L$. The shorter path from any leaf to the root is precisely why RecNNs mitigate the vanishing gradient problem for long dependencies.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between a Recurrent Neural Network and a Recursive Neural Network. Mention one application area where Recursive NNs are preferred over RNNs.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** processes input in a strict left-to-right sequential manner, with hidden state updates governed by $h_t = f(h_{t-1}, x_t)$. Its structure is a linear chain. In contrast, a **Recursive Neural Network (RecNN)** processes input with a tree or hierarchical structure, applying a composition function $p = f(x_{left}, x_{right})$ to combine child representations into parent representations, traversing the tree bottom-up.

RecNNs are preferred for tasks involving **syntactic structure**, such as **sentiment analysis on natural language sentences** or **natural language parsing**, because they can naturally encode the compositional, hierarchical nature of linguistic meaning — capturing long-range dependencies in fewer computational steps than an RNN.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write that *"RNN is for images, RecNN is for text"* — this is **wrong**. RNNs are also used for text. The correct distinction is **structure** (chain vs. tree), **not** data modality. Marks are deducted for this confusion.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is Backpropagation Through Structure (BTS)? How does it differ from standard Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)?*

**Model Answer:**

**Backpropagation Through Structure (BTS)** is the training algorithm for Recursive Neural Networks. It unrolls the computation graph as a **tree** and applies the chain rule starting from the root node, recursively propagating error gradients down to all descendant nodes. Gradients from all branches are **summed** at shared parameters.

**Key differences from BPTT:**

| Feature | BPTT | BTS |
|---|---|---|
| Graph Structure | Linear chain | Tree / DAG |
| Gradient Flow | Sequential time-unrolled | Branching structural |
| Dependency Range | $O(L)$ in chain length | $O(\text{tree depth})$ |
| Gradient Aggregation | Sequential sum | Sum over all descendants |

BTS allows the model to learn parameters that generalize across the entire tree structure, since the same composition weights are updated by gradients from every internal node.
