| **Pipeline** | Multi-stage | Single-stage CNN | End-to-end |
| **Accuracy (mAP)** | 66% | 70% | 78.8% |
| **Key Innovation** | CNN for region features | RoI Pooling, shared computation | RPN replaces selective search |

**R-CNN Workflow:**
1. Extract ~2000 region proposals using Selective Search
2. Resize each region to fixed size (227×227)
3. Pass through CNN → Extract features
4. Classify using SVM
5. Refine boxes using regression

**Fast R-CNN Improvements:**
- Single CNN forward pass for entire image
- Region of Interest (RoI) Pooling layer
- Softmax classifier replaces SVM
- **Speed:** 25× faster than R-CNN

**Faster R-CNN Improvements:**
- Introduces **Region Proposal Network (RPN)** — a small CNN that generates proposals
- Shares full-image convolutional features with detection network
- **Near real-time** performance (5 FPS)

*(Definition 2 marks + Comparison table 3 marks + Architecture details 2 marks = 7 marks)*

---

**b) What is Semantic Segmentation? Explain the U-Net architecture in detail. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Semantic Segmentation Definition:**
Semantic Segmentation is a pixel-level classification task where every pixel in an image is assigned a class label. Unlike object detection, it produces dense predictions with the same spatial resolution as the input.

**U-Net Architecture:**

U-Net, introduced by Ronneberger et al. (2015), is a symmetric encoder-decoder architecture with **skip connections**, originally designed for biomedical image segmentation.

**Key Components:**

**1. Encoder (Contracting Path):**
- Repeated application of two 3×3 convolutions + ReLU
- 2×2 max pooling for downsampling
- Feature channels double at each level: 64 → 128 → 256 → 512

**2. Bottleneck:**
- 1024 feature channels
- Captures the most compressed, high-level semantic information

**3. Decoder (Expanding Path):**
- 2×2 transposed convolutions for upsampling
- Halves feature channels: 1024 → 512 → 256 → 128 → 64
- Concatenation with corresponding encoder feature maps (skip connections)

**4. Skip Connections:**
- Concatenate encoder features with decoder features
- Preserves spatial information lost during downsampling
- Enables precise localization

**5. Final Layer:**
- 1×1 convolution maps 64 feature channels to desired number of classes

**Mathematical Representation of Skip Connection:**
$$D_l = \text{UpConv}(D_{l+1}) \oplus E_l$$

Where $D_l$ is the decoder output at level $l$, $E_l$ is the encoder output at the same level, and $\oplus$ denotes concatenation.

**Loss Function:** Pixel-wise Cross-Entropy Loss

$$L = -\sum_{i,j} \sum_{c} y_{i,j,c} \log(\hat{y}_{i,j,c})$$

**Applications:**
- Medical image segmentation (tumors, organs)
- Satellite imagery (land cover classification)
- Autonomous driving (road segmentation)

*(Definition 2 marks + U-Net explanation 3 marks + Diagram/Skip connection logic 2 marks = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention filter dimensions and stride** in CNN layer explanations — examiners specifically look for these.
> 2. **Confusing Object Detection with Image Classification** — Detection requires both localization AND classification.
> 3. **Skipping diagrams** in 7-mark questions — a neat labeled diagram is worth at least 2 marks.
> 4. **Not distinguishing between Semantic and Instance Segmentation** — this is a frequently tested KTU question.
> 5. **Writing the full formula for IoU** when the question only asks for the concept — manage your time and answer proportionally.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention skip connections** in U-Net — this is a critical component worth 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Computer Vision**

### **Core Concepts**
- Computer Vision mimics human visual perception using CNNs
- Image representation: Tensor of shape $(H, W, C)$
- Hierarchical feature learning: Edges → Textures → Parts → Objects

### **CNN Fundamentals**
- **Convolution:** Feature extraction using learnable filters
- **Pooling:** Max Pooling (most common), Average Pooling
- **Output dimension formula:** $W_{\text{out}} = \lfloor (W_{\text{in}} - k + 2p)/s \rfloor + 1$
- **Activation:** ReLU introduces non-linearity

### **Architectures to Remember**
- **LeNet-5:** 7 layers, digit recognition
- **AlexNet:** 8 layers, ReLU + Dropout, ImageNet breakthrough
- **VGG-16:** 16 layers, all 3×3 convolutions
- **ResNet:** Skip connections, enables very deep networks (50, 101, 152 layers)

### **Object Detection**
- **Two-stage:** R-CNN → Fast R-CNN → Faster R-CNN (more accurate)
- **One-stage:** YOLO, SSD (real-time, faster)
- **Metric:** IoU threshold of 0.5 for evaluation
- **Non-Max Suppression (NMS):** Removes duplicate detections

### **Semantic Segmentation**
- **FCN:** Fully Convolutional Networks, replaces FC layers with convolutions
- **U-Net:** Encoder-decoder with skip connections, gold standard for medical imaging

### **Transfer Learning & Augmentation**
- **Transfer Learning:** Reuse pre-trained models (ImageNet weights)
- **Data Augmentation:** Rotation, flipping, cropping, color jittering
- **Fine-tuning:** Unfreeze top layers and retrain with low learning rate

### **Key Metrics**
- **Precision:** $TP/(TP + FP)$
- **Recall:** $TP/(TP + FN)$
- **F1-Score:** Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall
- **IoU:** $\vert A \cap B \vert / \vert A \cup B \vert$
- **mAP:** Mean Average Precision across all classes

### **Exam Tips**
- Always draw **labeled diagrams** for 7-mark architecture questions
- Mention **real-world applications** to earn extra conceptual marks
- Memorize the **output dimension formula** — it's a guaranteed 2-mark question
- Be ready to explain **skip connections** in ResNet and U-Net
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Natural Language Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT503 - DEEP LEARNING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Natural Language Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Natural Language Processing (NLP) — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Natural Language Processing covers text representation, sequence modeling, encoder-decoder architectures, attention mechanisms, and Transformer fundamentals — the core machinery behind modern LLMs.

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Natural Language Processing (NLP)** is a sub-field of Artificial Intelligence and Deep Learning that focuses on enabling machines to **understand, interpret, generate, and respond to human language** in a valuable and meaningful way. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme, NLP is taught as the application of deep neural architectures (RNN, LSTM, GRU, Encoder-Decoder, Attention, Transformers) to linguistic data such as text and speech.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is NLP Hard?**
> Human language is **ambiguous, contextual, sequential, and high-dimensional**. The same sentence can carry multiple meanings depending on tone, culture, and prior conversation. NLP models must capture both **local syntax** (grammar of a phrase) and **long-range semantics** (meaning across paragraphs).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translation Booth" Intuition

Imagine you walk into a foreign country where you don't speak the language. You encounter a **human translator** standing at a booth:

1. You **speak** (input sequence of words) → the translator **listens** (tokenization + encoding).
2. The translator **understands the meaning** in their head (latent representation / embedding).
3. They **speak the translation** in the target language (decoding).
4. If you frown at one word, the translator **pays extra attention** to that part in the next sentence (Attention Mechanism).

This is *exactly* how an **Encoder–Decoder + Attention** model works in NLP!

## 1.3 The NLP Pipeline (High-Level)

A typical NLP deep-learning pipeline consists of the following stages:

1. **Text Acquisition** → Raw corpus (sentences, documents, social-media posts).
2. **Preprocessing** → Tokenization, stop-word removal, stemming, lemmatization, lower-casing.
3. **Text Representation** → Convert tokens into numerical vectors (BoW, TF-IDF, Word2Vec, GloVe, BERT embeddings).
4. **Modeling** → Feed vectors into RNN / LSTM / GRU / Transformer.
5. **Task Head** → Classification, translation, summarization, named-entity recognition, generation.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember the pipeline:** "**A**pples **P**roduce **R**eal **M**ath" → **A**cquire, **P**reprocess, **R**epresent, **M**odel.

## 1.4 Core NLP Tasks (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Task | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Text Classification** | Assign a label to a sentence/document | Spam detection, sentiment analysis |
| **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** | Identify proper nouns, dates, locations | "Barack Obama → PERSON" |
| **Machine Translation** | Convert text from one language to another | English → Malayalam |
| **Text Summarization** | Generate a short version of a long document | News headline generation |
| **Question Answering** | Answer a question from a given context | SQuAD dataset |
| **Text Generation** | Generate coherent text | Autocomplete, chatbots |
| **Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging** | Label each word with its grammatical role | "Running → VERB" |

## 1.5 Key Terminology (KTU Standard)

- **Token** — A unit of text (word, sub-word, or character).
- **Corpus** — A large structured collection of texts used for training.
- **Vocabulary** — The set of unique tokens in a corpus.
- **Embedding** — A dense, low-dimensional, learned vector representation of a token.
- **Sequence Length (T)** — The number of tokens in an input sample.
- **Embedding Dimension (d)** — The size of the vector used to represent each token (commonly 100, 256, 512, 768).
- **Context Window** — The number of surrounding tokens a model considers to learn a word's meaning.
- **Perplexity** — A standard evaluation metric for language models; **lower is better**.
- **BLEU Score** — A standard metric for machine translation; ranges from **0.0 to 1.0** (higher is better).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Text Preprocessing Steps (Theory)

Preprocessing transforms raw, messy text into a clean numerical format. Each step is described below in the order typically applied.

1. **Lowercasing** — Convert all characters to lowercase so `"India"` and `"india"` become the same token.
2. **Tokenization** — Split text into smaller units (words, sub-words, or characters).
3. **Stop-Word Removal** — Remove highly frequent but uninformative words (e.g., `"is"`, `"the"`, `"a"`).
4. **Stemming** — Chop word endings crudely (e.g., `"running"` → `"run"`, `"studies"` → `"studi"`).
5. **Lemmatization** — Reduce word to its dictionary form using vocabulary & morphology (e.g., `"better"` → `"good"`).
6. **Padding / Truncation** — Make all sequences the same length (T) for batch processing in deep models.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stemming vs Lemmatization** is a classic KTU 2-mark question. Stemming is rule-based and faster but inaccurate; Lemmatization uses a lexicon and is slower but produces real words.

## 2.2 Classical Text Representations (Pre-Deep Learning Era)

### 2.2.1 Bag of Words (BoW)

Each document is represented as a vector of size $\vert V \vert$ (vocabulary size), where the $i$-th entry counts how many times vocabulary word $i$ appears.

$$\text{BoW}(d) = [c_1, c_2, \dots, c_{\vert V \vert}]$$

**Drawback:** Ignores word order and semantic similarity.

### 2.2.2 TF-IDF (Term Frequency – Inverse Document Frequency)

A weighted version of BoW that down-weights common words and up-weights rare-but-informative words.

$$\text{TF}(t,d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$$

$$\text{IDF}(t, D) = \log\left(\frac{N}{1 + \vert \{ d \in D : t \in d \} \vert}\right)$$

$$\text{TF-IDF}(t, d, D) = \text{TF}(t,d) \cdot \text{IDF}(t,D)$$

where $f_{t,d}$ is the count of term $t$ in document $d$, $N$ is the total number of documents, and the denominator is the number of documents containing $t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $\log$ in IDF?** Without it, very common words would dominate; the log compresses the scale and dampens the effect of extremely rare words.

## 2.3 Distributed Representations — Word Embeddings

Classical BoW and TF-IDF produce **sparse, high-dimensional** vectors. Deep learning requires **dense, low-dimensional** vectors that capture semantic similarity.

### 2.3.1 Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013)

Word2Vec learns embeddings using a **shallow 2-layer neural network** trained on a fake task: predict surrounding words (or vice versa). Two architectures:

- **CBOW (Continuous Bag of Words)** — Predicts the **center word** from a window of **context words**.
- **Skip-gram** — Predicts **context words** from a single **center word**.

### 2.3.2 GloVe (Global Vectors)

Combines global co-occurrence statistics from the entire corpus with a local context window. The objective captures the **ratio of co-occurrence probabilities**.

## 2.4 Sequence Models — RNN Family

A **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** processes sequences one token at a time, maintaining a hidden state that acts as "memory."

$$h_t = \sigma_h(W_h h_{t-1} + W_x x_t + b_h)$$

$$y_t = \sigma_y(W_y h_t + b_y)$$

**Problem:** Standard RNNs suffer from the **Vanishing / Exploding Gradient Problem** for long sequences, so they cannot capture long-range dependencies.

### 2.4.1 LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory)

LSTM introduces a **cell state** $C_t$ and three **gates** (forget, input, output) that control the flow of information.

$$f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f) \quad \text{(Forget Gate)}$$

$$i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i) \quad \text{(Input Gate)}$$

$$\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C) \quad \text{(Candidate)}$$

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t \quad \text{(Cell State Update)}$$

$$o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o) \quad \text{(Output Gate)}$$

$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t) \quad \text{(Hidden State)}$$

### 2.4.2 GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit)

GRU is a **simplified LSTM** with only **two gates** (reset and update), and no separate cell state.

$$z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t]) \quad \text{(Update Gate)}$$

$$r_t = \sigma(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t]) \quad \text{(Reset Gate)}$$

$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t])$$

$$h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$$

## 2.5 Encoder–Decoder Architecture

Used for **sequence-to-sequence (Seq2Seq)** tasks (translation, summarization). The encoder compresses the input sequence into a **context vector** $c$; the decoder generates the output sequence from $c$.

$$c = \text{Encoder}(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_T)$$

$$y_t = \text{Decoder}(y_{t-1}, s_{t-1}, c)$$

**Problem:** A single fixed-size context vector $c$ becomes a **bottleneck** for long sentences.

## 2.6 Attention Mechanism — The Key Innovation

Attention lets the decoder **"look back"** at all encoder hidden states, not just the final one. Each encoder state is weighted by its **relevance** to the current decoding step.

**Step 1 — Score function:** Compute a relevance score between decoder state $s_{t-1}$ and each encoder state $h_i$:

$$e_{t,i} = \text{score}(s_{t-1}, h_i)$$

Common score functions:
- **Dot product:** $e_{t,i} = s_{t-1}^T h_i$
- **Additive (Bahdanau):** $e_{t,i} = v^T \tanh(W_s s_{t-1} + W_h h_i)$

**Step 2 — Softmax (alignment weights):**

$$\alpha_{t,i} = \frac{\exp(e_{t,i})}{\sum_{j=1}^{T} \exp(e_{t,j})}$$

**Step 3 — Context vector (weighted sum):**

$$c_t = \sum_{i=1}^{T} \alpha_{t,i} h_i$$

**Step 4 — Decoder update:**

$$s_t = \text{RNN}(s_{t-1}, y_{t-1}, c_t)$$

> [!TIP]
> The $\alpha_{t,i}$ values are called **alignment weights** — they literally tell the model "which input word to focus on for this output word."

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **TF** | $\text{TF}(t,d) = f_{t,d} / \sum f_{t',d}$ | Term Frequency |
| **IDF** | $\text{IDF}(t) = \log(N / (1 + \text{df}(t)))$ | Inverse Doc Frequency |
| **TF-IDF** | $\text{TF}(t,d) \cdot \text{IDF}(t)$ | Final weight |
| **RNN Hidden** | $h_t = \sigma(W_h h_{t-1} + W_x x_t + b_h)$ | One-step update |
| **LSTM Forget** | $f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ | Gate in $[0,1]$ |
| **LSTM Cell** | $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ | Memory update |
| **GRU Update** | $h_t = (1-z_t) h_{t-1} + z_t \tilde{h}_t$ | Simpler than LSTM |
| **Attention Score** | $e_{t,i} = s_{t-1}^T h_i$ | Dot product |
| **Softmax Attn** | $\alpha_{t,i} = \exp(e_{t,i}) / \sum_j \exp(e_{t,j})$ | Alignment weights |
| **Context Vec** | $c_t = \sum_i \alpha_{t,i} h_i$ | Weighted sum |
| **Perplexity** | $\text{PPL} = \exp(- \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p_\theta(x_i))$ | Lower is better |
| **BLEU** | $\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp(\sum_n w_n \log p_n)$ | $0 \le \text{BLEU} \le 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exams, **deriving** TF-IDF, the LSTM cell update, and the attention softmax is worth **7 marks minimum**. Memorize the shapes: $W_f \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times (d_h + d_x)}$.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare:** NLP extracts diagnoses and medications from clinical notes.
- **Legal:** Automates contract review and case-law search.
- **Finance:** Sentiment analysis of news drives algorithmic trading.
- **Customer Service:** Chatbots and voice assistants (Alexa, Google Assistant).
- **Search Engines:** Google BERT uses bidirectional Transformer attention.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: TF-IDF Weight Justification

We want a weight that is **high when a term is frequent in a specific document** but **low when the term is frequent across all documents**.

**Step 1 — Start with raw count:** $f_{t,d}$ is the count of term $t$ in document $d$.

**Step 2 — Normalize within a document (Term Frequency):**
Bigger documents naturally have larger counts, so we divide by the total terms in the document.

$$\text{TF}(t, d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$$

**Step 3 — Down-weight globally common words (Inverse Document Frequency):**
If a word appears in every document, it carries no discriminative power. We use the **ratio** of total documents to documents containing the term.

$$\text{IDF}(t, D) = \frac{N}{\vert \{ d \in D : t \in d \} \vert}$$

**Step 4 — Apply logarithm to dampen the scale:**
Without a $\log$, the ratio can range from $1$ to $N = 10^6$, which is unstable during training.

$$\text{IDF}(t, D) = \log\left(\frac{N}{1 + \text{df}(t)}\right)$$

The "$+1$" in the denominator is **Laplace smoothing** to prevent division by zero for words that never appear in the training set.

**Step 5 — Combine multiplicatively:**

$$\boxed{\text{TF-IDF}(t, d, D) = \text{TF}(t,d) \cdot \log\left(\frac{N}{1 + \text{df}(t)}\right)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Word2Vec Skip-gram Objective

Skip-gram predicts each context word $w_{t+j}$ given the center word $w_t$, for a window of size $m$ on each side.

**Step 1 — Likelihood of one context word:**
With $u_w$ as the center embedding and $v_w$ as the context embedding:

$$P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t) = \frac{\exp(u_{w_{t+j}}^T v_{w_t})}{\sum_{w' \in V} \exp(u_{w'}^T v_{w_t})}$$

This is a **softmax over the entire vocabulary**.

**Step 2 — Likelihood of all context words in the window** (assume independence):

$$P(\text{context} \mid w_t) = \prod_{-m \le j \le m, \, j \neq 0} P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t)$$

**Step 3 — Take the log-likelihood** (easier to optimize):

$$\log P = \sum_{-m \le j \le m, \, j \neq 0} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t)$$

**Step 4 — Convert to a loss by negating** (we minimize, not maximize):

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{-m \le j \le m, \, j \neq 0} \log \frac{\exp(u_{w_{t+j}}^T v_{w_t})}{\sum_{w'} \exp(u_{w'}^T v_{w_t})}$$

**Step 5 — Negative Sampling approximation:**
The denominator is expensive ($O(\vert V \vert)$). Replace the softmax with **Negative Sampling**, sampling $K$ "negative" words from a noise distribution $P_n(w)$.

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{NEG}} = -\log \sigma(u_{w_{t+j}}^T v_{w_t}) - \sum_{k=1}^{K} \log \sigma(-u_{w_k}^T v_{w_t})$$

where $\sigma$ is the sigmoid function. This reduces complexity from $O(\vert V \vert)$ to $O(K)$.

## 3.3 Derivation: LSTM Cell State Update

**Step 1 — Decide what to forget** from previous cell state $C_{t-1}$. The forget gate $f_t \in [0,1]^d$:

$$f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$$

**Step 2 — Decide what new information to store.** The input gate $i_t$ controls the strength, and $\tilde{C}_t$ is the candidate:

$$i_t = \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$$

$$\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$$

**Step 3 — Update the cell state** by combining old (scaled by forget) and new (scaled by input):

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

**Step 4 — Decide what to output** from the new cell state. The output gate controls which parts of $\tanh(C_t)$ are emitted:

$$o_t = \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$$

$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

This is the **complete LSTM forward pass** for one time step. KTU typically asks you to write all four gate equations plus the cell update for **7 marks**.

## 3.4 Derivation: Attention Mechanism (Additive / Bahdanau)

**Step 1 — Compute a score** for each encoder hidden state $h_i$ with respect to decoder state $s_{t-1}$:

$$e_{t,i} = v^T \tanh(W_s s_{t-1} + W_h h_i)$$

where $W_s, W_h$ are learnable matrices and $v$ is a learnable vector.

**Step 2 — Normalize with softmax** to get a probability distribution over input positions:

$$\alpha_{t,i} = \frac{\exp(e_{t,i})}{\sum_{j=1}^{T} \exp(e_{t,j})}$$

The constraint $\sum_i \alpha_{t,i} = 1$ ensures valid attention weights.

**Step 3 — Compute the dynamic context vector** as a weighted sum of encoder states:

$$c_t = \sum_{i=1}^{T} \alpha_{t,i} h_i$$

**Step 4 — Concatenate $c_t$ with the decoder input and feed to the RNN cell:**

$$s_t = \text{RNN}(s_{t-1}, [y_{t-1}; c_t])$$

**Step 5 — Final output distribution** over the target vocabulary:

$$P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, x) = \text{softmax}(W_o s_t)$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — TF-IDF

**Corpus (3 documents):**
- D1: `"the cat sat on the mat"`
- D2: `"the dog sat on the log"`
- D3: `"cats and dogs are pets"`

**Question:** Compute $\text{TF-IDF}$ for the word `"sat"` in D1.

**Step 1 — TF:** `"sat"` appears 1 time in D1; total words in D1 = 6.

$$\text{TF}(\text{"sat"}, D1) = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667$$

**Step 2 — IDF:** Total docs $N = 3$. `"sat"` appears in D1 and D2, so $\text{df} = 2$.

$$\text{IDF}(\text{"sat"}) = \log\left(\frac{3}{1+2}\right) = \log(1) = 0$$

**Interpretation:** Even though `"sat"` is informative in D1, it appears in another document, so the IDF weight collapses to 0. This is a **classic TF-IDF edge case** — KTU loves it!

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (LSTM vs GRU vs Vanilla RNN)

| Feature | Vanilla RNN | LSTM | GRU |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gates** | 0 | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| **Cell State** | None ($h_t$ only) | Yes ($C_t$) | None (merged into $h_t$) |
| **Parameters** | $\sim d^2$ | $\sim 4d^2$ | $\sim 3d^2$ |
| **Long Dependencies** | Poor | Excellent | Very Good |
| **Training Speed** | Fastest | Slowest | Medium |
| **Best For** | Short sequences | Long sequences | Mid-length, real-time |

## 3.7 Attention vs Self-Attention

| Aspect | Attention (Bahdanau) | Self-Attention (Transformer) |
|---|---|---|
| **Query Source** | Decoder state | Same sequence |
| **Key/Value Source** | Encoder states | Same sequence |
| **Use Case** | Seq2Seq translation | BERT, GPT, ViT |
| **Parallelizable** | No (sequential decode) | Yes (fully parallel) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The End-to-End NLP Deep Learning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Text Corpus] --> B[Lowercasing and Cleaning]
    B --> C[Tokenization]
    C --> D[Stopword Removal]
    D --> E[Stemming or Lemmatization]
    E --> F[Word Embedding Layer]
    F --> G[Sequence Model RNN LSTM GRU]
    G --> H[Attention Layer Optional]
    H --> I[Task Head Classifier Decoder]
    I --> J[Output Label or Generated Text]

    F1[BoW TFIDF Classical] -.-> F
    F2[Word2Vec GloVe Pretrained] -.-> F
    F3[Contextual BERT Embedding] -.-> F

    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style J fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style G fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style H fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
```

## 4.2 CBOW vs Skip-gram Word2Vec Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CBOW[CBOW Predicts Center Word]
        C1[w t-2] --> C5[Input Layer Sum or Avg]
        C2[w t-1] --> C5
        C3[w t+1] --> C5
        C4[w t+2] --> C5
        C5 --> C6[Hidden Layer Projection]
        C6 --> C7[Output Softmax]
        C7 --> C8[Predicted Center Word w t]
    end

    subgraph SKIP[Skipgram Predicts Context Words]
        S1[Center Word w t] --> S5[Input Layer]
        S5 --> S6[Hidden Layer Projection]
        S6 --> S7[Output Softmax]
        S7 --> S8[Predicted w t-2]
        S7 --> S9[Predicted w t-1]
        S7 --> S10[Predicted w t+1]
        S7 --> S11[Predicted w t+2]
    end

    style C7 fill:#bbdefb
    style S7 fill:#ffe0b2
```

## 4.3 LSTM Cell Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Input x t] --> C[Concat with h t-1]
    H[Prev Hidden h t-1] --> C
    C --> FG[Forget Gate sigmoid]
    C --> IG[Input Gate sigmoid]
    C --> OG[Output Gate sigmoid]
    C --> CC[Candidate Cell tanh]

    FG --> M1[Multiply]
    CC --> M2[Multiply]
    IG --> M2
    CPrev[Prev Cell C t-1] --> M1
    M1 --> ADD[Add]
    M2 --> ADD
    ADD --> CNew[New Cell C t]
    CNew --> T1[tanh]
    OG --> M3[Multiply]
    T1 --> M3
    M3 --> HNew[New Hidden h t]

    style CNew fill:#fff9c4
    style HNew fill:#c8e6c9
    style FG fill:#ffcdd2
    style IG fill:#ffcdd2
    style OG fill:#ffcdd2
```

## 4.4 Encoder–Decoder with Attention Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENC[Encoder BiLSTM]
        E1[x1] --> EH1[h1]
        E2[x2] --> EH2[h2]
        E3[x3] --> EH3[h3]
        E4[x4] --> EH4[h4]
    end

    EH1 --> SC[Score Function e ti]
    EH2 --> SC
    EH3 --> SC
    EH4 --> SC
    SC --> SM[Softmax Alignment alpha ti]
    SM --> WS[Weighted Sum c t]
    EH1 --> WS
    EH2 --> WS
    EH3 --> WS
    EH4 --> WS

    WS --> DEC[Decoder LSTM Step t]
    YT[Prev Output y t-1] --> DEC
    DEC --> YT2[New Output y t]
    YT2 --> YT

    style SC fill:#e1bee7
    style SM fill:#ffe082
    style WS fill:#a5d6a7
    style DEC fill:#90caf9
```

## 4.5 Self-Attention Block (Transformer-Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Input Embedding X] --> Q[Linear Q Projection]
    X --> K[Linear K Projection]
    X --> V[Linear V Projection]

    Q --> S1[Q times K transpose]
    K --> S1
    S1 --> DIV[Scale by sqrt d k]
    DIV --> SOFT[Softmax]
    SOFT --> ATT[Attention Weights A]
    V --> M1[Multiply with A]
    ATT --> M1
    M1 --> OUT[Self Attention Output]
    OUT --> NORM[Add and LayerNorm]
    NORM --> FFN[Feed Forward Network]
    FFN --> NORM2[Add and LayerNorm]
    NORM2 --> NX[Final Representation]

    style ATT fill:#ffccbc
    style OUT fill:#c5e1a5
    style NX fill:#b39ddb
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing "embedding dimension" with "vocabulary size"** — $d$ is the vector size per token; $\vert V \vert$ is the total unique tokens.
> 2. **Writing LSTM gates without the bias term** — full credit requires $W \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b$.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\log$ in IDF** — KTU awards 2 marks just for correctly writing $\log(N / (1+\text{df}))$.
> 4. **Mixing up CBOW and Skip-gram direction** — CBOW predicts center, Skip-gram predicts context.
> 5. **Skipping the softmax in attention** — alignment weights MUST be normalized or it is not attention.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Similar)]**
**Differentiate between stemming and lemmatization with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Stemming (1 mark):** A crude, rule-based process that chops word endings to obtain a "stem," which may not be a valid word. Example: `"studies"`, `"studying"`, `"studied"` → `"studi"`.
- **Lemmatization (1 mark):** A vocabulary-and-morphology-aware process that returns the dictionary form (lemma) of a word, which is always a valid word. Example: `"studies"`, `"studying"`, `"studied"` → `"study"`. It also handles irregulars like `"better"` → `"good"`.
- **Key Difference (1 mark):** Stemming is faster and rule-based; lemmatization is slower but contextually and morphologically accurate.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Similar)]**
**Explain the vanishing gradient problem in RNNs. How does LSTM overcome it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Vanishing Gradient (1 mark):** During Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT), gradients are multiplied across time steps. If the recurrent weight $W_h$ has eigenvalues $\vert \lambda \vert < 1$, gradients shrink exponentially with sequence length, making it impossible to learn long-range dependencies.
- **LSTM Solution — Cell State (1 mark):** LSTM introduces an additive cell-state update $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$, where the forget gate $f_t$ is multiplicative but the **addition** provides a path for gradients to flow unchanged across many time steps.
- **Gates act as "gradient highways" (1 mark):** By learning to keep $f_t \approx 1$, the LSTM can preserve information over long sequences without gradient decay.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Word2Vec Skip-gram architecture**. Derive the **negative sampling loss function** used during training. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **Attention Mechanism** in encoder–decoder models? Explain the **Bahdanau (additive) attention** with all four steps, including the role of the softmax. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Skip-gram Architecture (3 marks):**
- The Skip-gram model takes a **center word** $w_t$ as input and predicts the surrounding **context words** $w_{t-m}, \dots, w_{t-1}, w_{t+1}, \dots, w_{t+m}$ within a window of size $m$.
- It uses two embedding matrices: $U$ (center, $d \times \vert V \vert$) and $V$ (context, $\vert V \vert \times d$).
- The center word is looked up in $U$ to get its $d$-dimensional vector $u_{w_t}$, which is then multiplied by $V$ to produce a $\vert V \vert$-dimensional score vector, and softmax gives the prediction.

**Derivation of Negative Sampling Loss (4 marks):**
The full softmax objective for one context word is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{softmax}} = -\log \frac{\exp(u_{w_{t+j}}^T v_{w_t})}{\sum_{w' \in V} \exp(u_{w'}^T v_{w_t})}$$

The denominator requires summing over the entire vocabulary $\vert V \vert$, which is infeasible for $\vert V \vert \sim 10^5$ to $10^6$. Replace with **negative sampling**:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{NEG}} = -\log \sigma(u_{w_{t+j}}^T v_{w_t}) - \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{E}_{w_k \sim P_n(w)} \log \sigma(-u_{w_k}^T v_{w_t})$$

where $K$ negative samples are drawn from a noise distribution $P_n(w) \propto U(w)^{3/4}$ and $\sigma$ is the sigmoid. This reduces cost from $O(\vert V \vert)$ to $O(K+1)$.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Concept of Attention (2 marks):**
In a vanilla encoder–decoder, the entire input is compressed into a single fixed-size context vector $c$, which is a **bottleneck** for long sentences. Attention allows the decoder to dynamically attend to **all encoder hidden states** $\{h_1, h_2, \dots, h_T\}$ at every decoding step.

**Bahdanau Additive Attention — Four Steps (5 marks):**

1. **Score (1 mark):** $e_{t,i} = v^T \tanh(W_s s_{t-1} + W_h h_i)$
2. **Softmax Alignment (1 mark):** $\alpha_{t,i} = \exp(e_{t,i}) / \sum_j \exp(e_{t,j})$
3. **Context Vector (1 mark):** $c_t = \sum_i \alpha_{t,i} h_i$
4. **Decoder Update (1 mark):** $s_t = \text{RNN}(s_{t-1}, [y_{t-1}; c_t])$ and $P(y_t) = \text{softmax}(W_o s_t)$

**Role of Softmax (1 mark):** Converts raw scores into a probability distribution over input positions, ensuring $\sum_i \alpha_{t,i} = 1$, so $c_t$ is a valid weighted average.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **LSTM architecture** in detail. Derive the equations for the **forget gate, input gate, candidate cell state, cell state update, output gate, and hidden state**, clearly stating the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a small corpus of 3 documents: D1: `"deep learning is fun"`, D2: `"machine learning is great"`, D3: `"deep learning drives AI"`. Compute the **TF-IDF weight** of the word `"deep"` in document D1. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**LSTM Architecture Overview (1 mark):**
LSTM has a cell state $C_t$ that acts as long-term memory, and a hidden state $h_t$ that is the short-term output. Three gates (forget, input, output) regulate information flow.

**Equations (5 marks — 1 each for forget, input, candidate, cell update, output/hidden):**

1. **Forget Gate:** $f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ — decides what to discard from $C_{t-1}$.
2. **Input Gate:** $i_t = \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$ — decides what new info to write.
3. **Candidate:** $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$ — creates new candidate values.
4. **Cell Update:** $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ — forget old, add new.
5. **Output Gate + Hidden:** $o_t = \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$ and $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$.

**Why LSTM Solves Vanishing Gradient (1 mark):**
The cell update is **additive**, not purely multiplicative, so the gradient with respect to $C_{t-1}$ is $f_t$, and the network can learn $f_t \approx 1$ to preserve gradient flow over long sequences.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Total Documents (1 mark):** $N = 3$.

**Step 2 — Term Frequency of `"deep"` in D1 (2 marks):**
D1 has 4 words: `"deep"`, `"learning"`, `"is"`, `"fun"`. The word `"deep"` appears 1 time.

$$\text{TF}(\text{"deep"}, D1) = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

**Step 3 — Document Frequency of `"deep"` (1 mark):**
`"deep"` appears in D1 and D3 (NOT in D2). So $\text{df}(\text{"deep"}) = 2$.

**Step 4 — IDF (2 marks):**

$$\text{IDF}(\text{"deep"}) = \log\left(\frac{N}{1 + \text{df}}\right) = \log\left(\frac{3}{1+2}\right) = \log(1) = 0$$

**Step 5 — TF-IDF (1 mark):**

$$\text{TF-IDF}(\text{"deep"}, D1) = 0.25 \times 0 = \boxed{0}$$

**Interpretation:** Even though `"deep"` appears in D1, it also appears in another document, so its discriminative power is zero. This is a standard edge case that KTU may test.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **NLP Pipeline:** Acquire → Preprocess → Represent → Model → Task Head.
- **Preprocessing Steps:** Lowercase, Tokenize, Remove Stopwords, Stem/Lemmatize, Pad.
- **BoW / TF-IDF:** Sparse, classical, ignore word order. **TF-IDF** = TF $\times \log(N / (1+\text{df}))$.
- **Word2Vec:** Dense, semantic. **CBOW** = predict center, **Skip-gram** = predict context. Loss uses **negative sampling** to avoid full softmax.
- **RNN Problem:** Vanishing gradient over long sequences.
- **LSTM:** 3 gates (forget, input, output) + cell state $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$. **Additive update** prevents gradient decay.
- **GRU:** 2 gates (update, reset), no separate cell state, fewer parameters than LSTM.
- **Encoder–Decoder:** Compresses input to context vector $c$, then decodes target sequence. Suffers from **bottleneck** for long inputs.
- **Attention (Bahdanau):** Score → Softmax → Weighted sum → Decoder update. **Softmax is mandatory** for valid alignment weights.
- **Self-Attention (Transformer):** Q, K, V projections; scaled by $\sqrt{d_k}$; foundation of BERT, GPT.
- **Evaluation:** Perplexity (LM, lower better), BLEU (translation, higher better), Accuracy/F1 (classification).
- **Key dimensions:** $T$ = sequence length, $d$ = embedding dim, $\vert V \vert$ = vocabulary size.
- **Common Pitfalls:** Confusing CBOW/Skip-gram, omitting bias in gate equations, forgetting $\log$ in IDF, missing softmax in attention.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Word Embedding Techniques (TF-IDF, Word2Vec, GloVe)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCMT503 - DEEP LEARNING CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Word Embedding Techniques (TF-IDF, Word2Vec, GloVe)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Word Embedding?

A **Word Embedding** is a learned, dense, low-dimensional, continuous-valued vector representation of a word that captures its semantic, syntactic, and contextual meaning from large unlabeled text corpora. Unlike traditional one-hot encodings (which are sparse, high-dimensional, and carry no semantic relationship), word embeddings map semantically similar words to nearby points in a continuous vector space.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A word embedding is a parameterized function mapping words from a vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$ into a high-dimensional real-valued vector space $\mathbb{R}^{d}$, where $d \ll \vert \mathcal{V} \vert$, such that the geometric distance (typically cosine or Euclidean) between vectors reflects the semantic similarity between the corresponding words.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars Tested in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **TF-IDF** – Classical statistical/algebraic embedding (count-based, sparse)
> 2. **Word2Vec** – Neural prediction-based embedding (Mikolov et al., 2013)
> 3. **GloVe** – Global matrix factorization + local context window (Pennington et al., 2014)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Map"

Imagine a vast library with millions of books:
- **Bag-of-Words (BoW) / One-Hot**: Each book is given a unique locker number (1, 2, 3, ..., 1,000,000). The locker number tells you *nothing* about the book's topic. This is the **one-hot encoding**.
- **TF-IDF**: Each book gets a *score sheet* (sparse vector) listing how often certain keywords appear, weighted by rarity. Two books about "neural networks" will share high scores on those keywords, but the representation is still a long, mostly empty vector.
- **Word2Vec / GloVe**: Each book is placed on a 2D/300D **map of knowledge**. Books on "quantum physics" cluster in one corner, cookbooks in another, romance novels elsewhere. The **coordinates themselves encode meaning** — distance and direction mean something.

In this analogy:
- **Coordinates** = Embedding vector components $w \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$
- **Map** = The continuous embedding space
- **Proximity** = Semantic similarity
- **Direction** = Semantic relationships (e.g., King − Man + Woman ≈ Queen)

---

## 1.3 Why Word Embeddings Matter in Deep Learning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Deep learning models (RNNs, LSTMs, Transformers, CNNs for NLP) require **fixed-size, dense numerical inputs**. Word embeddings serve as the **first hidden layer input** in nearly all modern NLP pipelines. KTU frequently tests:
> - How embeddings replace one-hot vectors at the input layer
> - How dimensionality ($d = 50, 100, 300$) affects performance
> - The distinction between **count-based** vs. **prediction-based** models

---

## 1.4 Taxonomy of Word Embedding Techniques

| Category | Method | Type | Learning Paradigm |
|----------|--------|------|-------------------|
| **Count-Based (Sparse)** | TF-IDF, Co-occurrence Matrix | Statistical | Matrix algebra on word counts |
| **Prediction-Based (Dense)** | Word2Vec (CBOW / Skip-gram) | Neural | Local context window prediction |
| **Global + Local** | GloVe | Hybrid | Global co-occurrence + local context |

---

## 1.5 Pre-Training vs. Task-Specific Embeddings

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU context, you must distinguish:
> - **Pre-trained embeddings** (Word2Vec, GloVe, fastText) — trained once on huge corpora (Google News, Common Crawl) and reused.
> - **Task-specific embeddings** — learned jointly with the downstream model (e.g., the embedding layer in an LSTM trained for sentiment analysis).
> Pre-trained embeddings are **transfer learning** for NLP.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 TF-IDF (Term Frequency – Inverse Document Frequency)

### 2.1.1 Intuition
TF-IDF assigns a weight to each word in a document based on two statistics:
- **TF** rewards words that appear **frequently** in a single document.
- **IDF** penalizes words that appear in **too many documents** (e.g., "the", "is", "a" — stop words).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just TF?** Because the most frequent word in any English document is "the", which is semantically useless. IDF **demotes** such globally common words.

### 2.1.2 Mathematical Formulation

Let:
- $\mathcal{D} = \{d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_N\}$ = corpus of $N$ documents
- $\mathcal{V} = \{w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_V\}$ = vocabulary of $V$ terms
- $f_{t,d}$ = raw count of term $t$ in document $d$

**Term Frequency (TF):**
$$
\mathrm{TF}(t, d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{w \in d} f_{w,d}}
$$

**Inverse Document Frequency (IDF):**
$$
\mathrm{IDF}(t) = \log\!\left(\frac{N}{1 + \mathrm{df}(t)}\right)
$$
where $\mathrm{df}(t) = \sum_{d=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}\{t \in d\}$ is the document frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The `+1` in the denominator is the **smoothed IDF** to prevent division by zero when a query term is absent from the training corpus. KTU often tests this exact form.

**TF-IDF Weight:**
$$
\boxed{\;\mathrm{TF\text{-}IDF}(t, d) = \mathrm{TF}(t, d) \cdot \mathrm{IDF}(t)\;}
$$

### 2.1.3 KTU Formula Sheet — TF-IDF Variants

| Variant | Formula | When Used |
|---------|---------|-----------|
| Raw Count | $f_{t,d}$ | BoW baseline |
| Normalized TF | $\frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{w} f_{w,d}}$ | Length normalization |
| Log-normalized TF | $1 + \log(f_{t,d})$ | Sub-linear dampening |
| Smoothed IDF | $\log\!\big(\frac{N}{1+\mathrm{df}(t)}\big)$ | Avoid div-by-zero |
| IDF with offset | $\log\!\big(\frac{N - \mathrm{df}(t) + 0.5}{\mathrm{df}(t) + 0.5}\big)$ | BM25 (Lucene) |
| Sub-linear TF-IDF | $(1 + \log f_{t,d}) \cdot \log\!\big(\frac{N}{\mathrm{df}(t)}\big)$ | Information Retrieval |

### 2.1.4 Limitations of TF-IDF
- **No semantic understanding**: "car" and "automobile" are treated as completely different dimensions.
- **High dimensionality**: $V$ can be $10^5$–$10^6$ → sparse matrix.
- **No context**: Word order and polysemy are ignored.

---

## 2.2 Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013)

Word2Vec is a **shallow, two-layer neural network** trained to reconstruct the linguistic context of words. It produces dense embeddings of fixed dimension $d$ (typically 100–300).

### 2.2.1 Two Architectures

> [!NOTE]
> KTU almost always asks for the **architectural difference** between CBOW and Skip-gram with a diagram.

| Aspect | CBOW (Continuous Bag-of-Words) | Skip-gram |
|-------|-------------------------------|-----------|
| **Input** | Surrounding context words (averaged) | Center (target) word |
| **Output** | Center (target) word | Surrounding context words |
| **Training Speed** | Faster | Slower (but better for rare words) |
| **Best For** | Frequent words, small corpora | Large corpora, rare words |
| **KTU Bias** | Often favoured in MCQs | Often favoured in 14-mark derivations |

### 2.2.2 The Skip-gram Objective Function

Given a sequence of training words $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_T$ and a context window of size $c$, the Skip-gram objective is to maximize the average log-probability:
$$
\mathcal{J}(\theta) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{\substack{-c \le j \le c \\ j \neq 0}} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t \; ; \theta)
$$

The conditional probability is modelled using the **softmax function**:
$$
P(w_O \mid w_I) = \frac{\exp\!\big(\mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{w_O}\, \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\big)}{\sum_{w=1}^{V} \exp\!\big(\mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{w}\, \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\big)}
$$
where:
- $\mathbf{v}_{w_I} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ = **input vector** of the center word $w_I$
- $\mathbf{v}'_{w_O} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ = **output vector** of the context word $w_O$
- $V = \vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ = vocabulary size

### 2.2.3 CBOW Objective (mirror formulation)

CBOW maximizes:
$$
\mathcal{J}_{\text{CBOW}} = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P\!\left(w_t \,\Big|\, w_{t-c}, \ldots, w_{t-1}, w_{t+1}, \ldots, w_{t+c}\right)
$$
with the context being **averaged**:
$$
\mathbf{h} = \frac{1}{2c} \sum_{\substack{-c \le j \le c \\ j \neq 0}} \mathbf{v}_{w_{t+j}}
$$

### 2.2.4 The Softmax Bottleneck & Solutions

> [!WARNING]
> Computing the **full softmax denominator** $\sum_{w=1}^{V} \exp(\cdots)$ is $O(V)$ per training step. For $V = 10^6$, this is **infeasible**. KTU expects you to name the two solutions:

**Solution 1 — Hierarchical Softmax (Huffman Tree):**
- Build a **binary Huffman tree** with all $V$ words as leaves.
- Output probability = product of sigmoid decisions along the root-to-leaf path.
- Complexity drops to $O(\log_2 V)$.

**Solution 2 — Negative Sampling (NEG):**
- For each positive (center, context) pair, sample $k$ **negative** words from a unigram distribution raised to the $3/4$ power:
$$
P(w) = \frac{f(w)^{3/4}}{\sum_{w'} f(w')^{3/4}}
$$
- New objective becomes a **binary classification** (real context vs. noise) using sigmoid cross-entropy.

### 2.2.5 Word2Vec KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $\mathbf{v}_{w}$ | Input embedding of word $w$ (center) |
| $\mathbf{v}'_{w}$ | Output embedding of word $w$ (context) |
| $d$ | Embedding dimension (e.g., 100, 300) |
| $V$ | Vocabulary size |
| $c$ | Context window size (e.g., 5) |
| $k$ | Number of negative samples (5–20) |
| $\eta$ | Learning rate |
| $\theta$ | All parameters $= \{\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{v}'\}$ |

---

## 2.3 GloVe (Global Vectors for Word Representation)

GloVe (Pennington, Socher, Manning — Stanford, 2014) **combines the strengths** of:
1. **Global matrix factorization** (like LSA / SVD on co-occurrence counts)
2. **Local context window** methods (like Word2Vec)

### 2.3.1 Co-occurrence Matrix

Build a word–word **co-occurrence matrix** $\mathbf{X}$, where $X_{ij}$ = number of times word $j$ appears in the context of word $i$ within a corpus.
- $X_i = \sum_k X_{ik}$ = total times any word appears in the context of word $i$.
- $P_{ij} = P(j \mid i) = \dfrac{X_{ij}}{X_i}$ = probability that word $j$ appears in the context of word $i$.

### 2.3.2 The Co-occurrence Ratio (Core Insight of GloVe)

The **magic of GloVe** is that meaningful word relationships can be extracted from the **ratios of co-occurrence probabilities**, not the raw probabilities themselves.

For probe words $i$ and test words $j, k$:
$$
F(w_i, w_j, w_k) = \frac{P_{ik}}{P_{jk}}
$$

**Example (from the GloVe paper):**
- Probe: $i = $ "ice", $j = $ "steam"
- Test word $k = $ "solid" → $P_{\text{ik}}/P_{\text{jk}} = P(\text{solid}\mid\text{ice})/P(\text{solid}\mid\text{steam})$ — large (solid is related to ice, not steam)
- Test word $k = $ "gas" → ratio is small
- Test word $k = $ "water" → ratio is close to 1 (equally related to both)
- Test word $k = $ "fashion" → ratio is close to 1 (unrelated to both)

### 2.3.3 GloVe Objective Function

The general form (F-symbolic) becomes:
$$
F\!\big(\mathbf{v}_i - \mathbf{v}_j,\; \mathbf{v}_k\big) = \frac{P_{ik}}{P_{jk}}
$$

Solving this vector equation (assuming $F$ is a homomorphism from $(\mathbb{R},+)$ to $(\mathbb{R}_{>0}, \times)$) yields:
$$
F\!\left((\mathbf{v}_i - \mathbf{v}_j)^{\top} \mathbf{v}_k\right) = \frac{P_{ik}}{P_{jk}} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; F\!\left(\mathbf{v}_i^{\top} \mathbf{v}_k - \mathbf{v}_j^{\top} \mathbf{v}_k\right) = \frac{P_{ik}}{P_{jk}}
$$

Choosing $F = \exp$ (a natural homomorphism):
$$
\mathbf{v}_i^{\top} \mathbf{v}_k - \mathbf{v}_j^{\top} \mathbf{v}_k = \log P_{ik} - \log P_{jk}
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
\mathbf{v}_i^{\top} \mathbf{v}_k = \log P_{ik} = \log X_{ik} - \log X_i
$$

Adding bias terms $b_i, b_k$ and symmetrizing (since co-occurrence is symmetric), the final **GloVe cost function** is:

$$
\boxed{\;J = \sum_{i,j=1}^{V} f(X_{ij})\,\Big( \mathbf{v}_i^{\top} \mathbf{v}'_j + b_i + b'_j - \log X_{ij} \Big)^{2}\;}
$$

where the **weighting function** is:
$$
f(x) = 
\begin{cases}
(x / x_{\max})^{\alpha} & \text{if } x < x_{\max} \\
1 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$
with typical values $\alpha = 3/4$ and $x_{\max} = 100$.

### 2.3.4 Why $f(x)$? The Sparse Co-occurrence Problem

- $X_{ij} = 0$ for most word pairs (corpus is sparse).
- $\log 0 = -\infty$ — the squared-error term would explode.
- $f(0) = 0$ — co-occurrence of zero contributes **nothing** to the loss.
- The cap at $x_{\max}$ prevents very frequent words (like "the") from dominating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the final embedding, $\mathbf{e}_w = \mathbf{v}_w + \mathbf{v}'_w$ (sum of input and output vectors), which is a common KTU trick question.

---

## 2.4 Master KTU Comparison Table

| Feature | TF-IDF | Word2Vec | GloVe |
|---------|--------|----------|-------|
| **Type** | Count-based, sparse | Prediction-based, dense | Hybrid (global + local), dense |
| **Dimensions** | $V$ (sparse) | $d$ (50–300) | $d$ (50–300) |
| **Captures Semantics** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Uses Context** | ❌ No | ✅ Local window | ✅ Global + local |
| **Training Cost** | $O(NV)$ matrix | $O(Tc d)$ SGD | $O(\vert X_{\neq 0}\vert d)$ |
| **Polysemy** | ❌ | ❌ (single vector) | ❌ (single vector) |
| **Linear Substructure** | ❌ | ✅ (King−Man+Woman≈Queen) | ✅ |
| **Key Paper** | Salton & Buckley (1988) | Mikolov et al. (2013) | Pennington et al. (2014) |
| **KTU Frequency** | High (foundation) | Very High | Very High |

> [!NOTE]

> - **Search engines** (Google, Bing): TF-IDF in BM25 ranking
> - **Recommendation systems** (Netflix, Amazon): GloVe for item co-occurrence

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Worked Example: TF-IDF Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite 7-Mark Question:** Given a mini corpus, compute the TF-IDF vector for a query term.

### Corpus
- $d_1$ = "the cat sat on the mat"
- $d_2$ = "the dog sat on the log"
- $d_3$ = "cats and dogs are pets"

So $N = 3$.

**Vocabulary** $\mathcal{V} = \{\text{the, cat, sat, on, mat, dog, log, cats, and, dogs, are, pets}\}$ — $V = 12$.

### Step 1: Compute Document Frequencies $\mathrm{df}(t)$

| Term $t$ | Appears in docs | $\mathrm{df}(t)$ |
|----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| the | $d_1, d_2, d_3$ | 3 |
| cat | $d_1$ | 1 |
| sat | $d_1, d_2$ | 2 |
| on | $d_1, d_2$ | 2 |
| mat | $d_1$ | 1 |
| dog | $d_2$ | 1 |
| log | $d_2$ | 1 |
| cats | $d_3$ | 1 |
| and | $d_3$ | 1 |
| dogs | $d_3$ | 1 |
| are | $d_3$ | 1 |
| pets | $d_3$ | 1 |

### Step 2: Compute IDF for "cat" and "the"

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{IDF}(\text{cat}) &= \log\!\left(\frac{N}{1 + \mathrm{df}(\text{cat})}\right) = \log\!\left(\frac{3}{1 + 1}\right) = \log(1.5) \approx 0.1761
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{IDF}(\text{the}) &= \log\!\left(\frac{N}{1 + \mathrm{df}(\text{the})}\right) = \log\!\left(\frac{3}{1 + 3}\right) = \log(0.75) \approx -0.1249
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative IDF for "the" correctly reflects that "the" is **less informative** than "cat". Many implementations clip negative IDFs to zero or use $\log(1 + N/\mathrm{df})$.

### Step 3: Compute TF for "cat" in $d_1$

Document $d_1$ has 6 words. The word "cat" appears once. Hence:
$$
\mathrm{TF}(\text{cat}, d_1) = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667
$$

### Step 4: TF-IDF for "cat" in $d_1$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{TF\text{-}IDF}(\text{cat}, d_1) &= \mathrm{TF}(\text{cat}, d_1) \cdot \mathrm{IDF}(\text{cat}) \\
&= \frac{1}{6} \cdot \log(1.5) \\
&\approx 0.1667 \times 0.1761 \\
&\approx 0.0294
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Full TF-IDF Vector for $d_1$

The TF-IDF representation of $d_1$ is a 12-dimensional vector (in $\mathcal{V}$ order):
$$
\mathbf{v}_{d_1} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.0208 \\ 0.0294 \\ 0.0176 \\ 0.0176 \\ 0.0294 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$
(Each entry = TF × IDF, using the smoothed IDF formula.)

> [!TIP]
> In the exam, draw the $3 \times 12$ document-term TF-IDF matrix and highlight the sparse, high-dimensional nature — this is the **motivation for moving to dense embeddings**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Skip-gram with Negative Sampling (SGNS) Gradient

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a classic KTU 14-mark derivation.

The SGNS loss for a single (center, context) pair $(w_I, w_O)$ with $k$ negative samples $\{\tilde{w}_1, \ldots, \tilde{w}_k\}$ is:

$$
\mathcal{L} = -\log \sigma(\mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{w_O}\, \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) - \sum_{i=1}^{k} \log \sigma\!\big(-\mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{\tilde{w}_i}\, \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\big)
$$

where $\sigma(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$ is the sigmoid function.

### Derivation of Gradients

**Step 1: Derivative w.r.t. output vector $\mathbf{v}'_{w_O}$**

For a positive sample, only the first term depends on $\mathbf{v}'_{w_O}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{v}'_{w_O}} 
&= -\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{v}'_{w_O}} \log \sigma(\mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{w_O}\, \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) \\
&= -\frac{1}{\sigma(\mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{w_O}\, \mathbf{v}_{w_I})} \cdot \sigma(\mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{w_O}\, \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) \big(1 - \sigma(\mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{w_O}\, \mathbf{v}_{w_I})\big) \cdot \mathbf{v}_{w_I} \\
&= -\big(1 - \sigma(\mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{w_O}\, \mathbf{v}_{w_I})\big) \cdot \mathbf{v}_{w_I} \\
&= \big(\sigma(\mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{w_O}\, \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) - 1\big) \cdot \mathbf{v}_{w_I}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Derivative w.r.t. negative sample output vector $\mathbf{v}'_{\tilde{w}_i}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{v}'_{\tilde{w}_i}} 
&= -\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{v}'_{\tilde{w}_i}} \log \sigma(-\mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{\tilde{w}_i}\, \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) \\
&= -\frac{1}{\sigma(-\mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{\tilde{w}_i}\, \mathbf{v}_{w_I})} \cdot \sigma(-\mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{\tilde{w}_i}\, \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) \big(1 - \sigma(-\mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{\tilde{w}_i}\, \mathbf{v}_{w_I})\big) \cdot (-\mathbf{v}_{w_I}) \\
&= \big(1 - \sigma(-\mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{\tilde{w}_i}\, \mathbf{v}_{w_I})\big) \cdot \mathbf{v}_{w_I} \\
&= \sigma(\mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{\tilde{w}_i}\, \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) \cdot \mathbf{v}_{w_I}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Used the identity $1 - \sigma(-x) = \sigma(x)$.)

**Step 3: Derivative w.r.t. input vector $\mathbf{v}_{w_I}$**

Using chain rule over all terms:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{v}_{w_I}} 
&= \big(\sigma(\mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{w_O}\, \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) - 1\big) \cdot \mathbf{v}'_{w_O} + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma(\mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{\tilde{w}_i}\, \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) \cdot \mathbf{v}'_{\tilde{w}_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: SGD Update Rule**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{v}_{w_I} &\leftarrow \mathbf{v}_{w_I} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{v}_{w_I}} \\
\mathbf{v}'_{w_O} &\leftarrow \mathbf{v}'_{w_O} - \eta \cdot \big(\sigma(\mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{w_O}\, \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) - 1\big) \cdot \mathbf{v}_{w_I} \\
\mathbf{v}'_{\tilde{w}_i} &\leftarrow \mathbf{v}'_{\tilde{w}_i} - \eta \cdot \sigma(\mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{\tilde{w}_i}\, \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) \cdot \mathbf{v}_{w_I}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition for exam answer:** If the model already predicts the context word with high probability ($\sigma \approx 1$), the gradient $(\sigma - 1) \approx 0$ → minimal update. If the model is wrong, the update is large.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: GloVe Cost Function

Starting from the ratio:
$$
\frac{P_{ik}}{P_{jk}} = \frac{X_{ik}/X_i}{X_{jk}/X_j} = \frac{X_{ik} X_j}{X_{jk} X_i}
$$

We want a function $F$ that depends only on word vectors and captures this ratio:
$$
F(\mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j, \mathbf{v}_k) = \frac{P_{ik}}{P_{jk}}
$$

**Step 1:** Since the right side is a ratio, the left side should be a ratio. Note that the RHS depends on $i, k$ and $j, k$ **separately**, so $F$ should separate:
$$
F\!\big((\mathbf{v}_i - \mathbf{v}_j)^{\top} \mathbf{v}_k\big) = \frac{P_{ik}}{P_{jk}}
$$

**Step 2:** RHS is a ratio in $(\mathbb{R}_{>0}, \times)$. The LHS argument $(\mathbf{v}_i - \mathbf{v}_j)^{\top} \mathbf{v}_k$ is in $(\mathbb{R}, +)$. We need a homomorphism:
$$
F(a + b) = F(a) \cdot F(b) \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = \exp
$$

So:
$$
\exp\!\big((\mathbf{v}_i - \mathbf{v}_j)^{\top} \mathbf{v}_k\big) = \frac{P_{ik}}{P_{jk}}
$$

**Step 3:** Take $\log$ on both sides:
$$
(\mathbf{v}_i - \mathbf{v}_j)^{\top} \mathbf{v}_k = \log P_{ik} - \log P_{jk} = \log X_{ik} - \log X_i - \log X_{jk} + \log X_j
$$

**Step 4:** Distribute:
$$
\mathbf{v}_i^{\top} \mathbf{v}_k - \mathbf{v}_j^{\top} \mathbf{v}_k = \log X_{ik} - \log X_{jk} - \log X_i + \log X_j
$$

**Step 5:** Group by $i$ and $j$ separately. Since each equation should hold for arbitrary $j$:
$$
\mathbf{v}_i^{\top} \mathbf{v}_k + \log X_i = \log X_{ik} \quad \text{(absorbing $X_i$ into a bias for word $i$)}
$$

**Step 6:** Add bias terms $b_i$ for $w_i$ and $b'_k$ for $w_k$ (so that symmetry can be restored):
$$
\mathbf{v}_i^{\top} \mathbf{v}'_k + b_i + b'_k = \log X_{ik}
$$

**Step 7:** The least-squares regression cost (with weighting $f(X_{ik})$ to down-weight rare co-occurrences):
$$
\boxed{\;J = \sum_{i=1}^{V} \sum_{j=1}^{V} f(X_{ij}) \big( \mathbf{v}_i^{\top} \mathbf{v}'_j + b_i + b'_j - \log X_{ij} \big)^{2}\;}
$$

with the weighting function:
$$
f(x) = 
\begin{cases}
(x / x_{\max})^{\alpha} & \text{if } x < x_{\max} \\
1 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: GloVe Co-occurrence Ratios

Given a tiny corpus and probe word $i = \text{ice}$:

| Word $k$ | $P(k \mid \text{ice})$ | $P(k \mid \text{steam})$ | Ratio $P_{ik}/P_{jk}$ | Interpretation |
|----------|------------------------|--------------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| solid | 0.00019 | 0.00002 | 9.5 | "solid" is related to ice, not steam |
| gas | 0.00006 | 0.00035 | 0.17 | "gas" is related to steam, not ice |
| water | 0.003 | 0.0024 | 1.25 | "water" relates to both |
| fashion | 0.00001 | 0.00001 | 1.0 | Unrelated to both |

> [!NOTE]
> The **ratio captures discriminative meaning** that raw probabilities cannot. This is the central insight of GloVe and a frequent KTU long-answer question.

---

## 3.5 Analytic Comparison Matrix — Quick Exam Recall

| Property | TF-IDF | Word2Vec | GloVe |
|----------|--------|----------|-------|
| Captures co-occurrence? | Indirectly (count) | Locally only | Globally & locally |
| Handles rare words? | Penalizes via IDF | Good with Skip-gram | Tuned via $f(x)$ |
| Out-of-vocabulary (OOV)? | Zero (problem!) | Zero (problem!) | Zero (problem!) |
| Linear analogies? | ❌ | ✅ King−Man+Woman≈Queen | ✅ |
| $O(V)$ softmax cost? | N/A | Yes (solved by NEG/HS) | No (regression) |
| Final vector (per word) | TF-IDF row | $\mathbf{v}_w$ or $\mathbf{v}_w + \mathbf{v}'_w$ | $\mathbf{v}_w + \mathbf{v}'_w$ |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Word2Vec — CBOW Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["w_t-2<br/>one-hot"] --> H1["Hidden Layer<br/>Projection Sum/Average"]
    A2["w_t-1<br/>one-hot"] --> H1
    A3["w_t+1<br/>one-hot"] --> H1
    A4["w_t+2<br/>one-hot"] --> H1
    H1 --> O1["Output Layer<br/>Softmax over V words"]
    O1 --> P1["Predicted: w_t<br/>center word"]
    W1["W (Vx d)<br/>Input matrix"] -.-> H1
    W2["W prime dx V<br/>Output matrix"] -.-> O1
```

> [!NOTE]
> In CBOW, all context words are projected to the same hidden layer, then **summed/averaged** to predict the center word. The hidden layer has $d$ neurons (no non-linearity — it's a linear projection).

---

## 4.2 Word2Vec — Skip-gram Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    I1["Input: w_t<br/>one-hot"] --> H1["Hidden Layer<br/>Linear projection"]
    H1 --> O1["Output: w_t-2<br/>softmax"]
    H1 --> O2["Output: w_t-1<br/>softmax"]
    H1 --> O3["Output: w_t+1<br/>softmax"]
    H1 --> O4["Output: w_t+2<br/>softmax"]
    Win["W (Vx d)"] -.-> H1
    Wout["W prime dx V"] -.-> O1
    Wout -.-> O2
    Wout -.-> O3
    Wout -.-> O4
```

> [!NOTE]
> Skip-gram is the **inverse** of CBOW: one input → multiple context predictions. The same input and output matrices are shared across all $2c$ output heads.

---

## 4.3 TF-IDF Pipeline Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    D["Raw Document Corpus<br/>D1, D2 ... DN"] --> T["Tokenization<br/>+ Stop-word Removal"]
    T --> S["Stemming / Lemmatization"]
    S --> F["Build Document-Term<br/>Count Matrix X (N x V)"]
    F --> TF["Row-wise Normalize<br/>to compute TF"]
    F --> DF["Column-wise df to<br/>compute IDF = log N/df"]
    TF --> MUL["Element-wise Multiply<br/>TF x IDF"]
    DF --> MUL
    MUL --> V["Final TF-IDF Matrix<br/>W (N x V), sparse"]
    Q["New Query Document"] --> QP["Same Tokenize + Stem"]
    QP --> QV["Query TF-IDF Vector q (1 x V)"]
    V --> SC["Cosine Similarity<br/>sim q, di = q.di / norm q . norm di"]
    QV --> SC
    SC --> R["Ranked Results"]
```

---

## 4.4 GloVe Training Pipeline Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    C["Raw Corpus<br/>w1 w2 ... wT"] --> W["Sliding Window<br/>Size = c"]
    W --> X["Co-occurrence Matrix X<br/>V x V"]
    X --> R["Compute Ratios<br/>Pik / Pjk"]
    R --> I["Insight: Ratios encode<br/>meaningful relationships"]
    I --> F["Choose F = exp<br/>Homomorphism from + to x"]
    F --> EQ["v_i trans v_k prime + b_i + b_k prime<br/>approx log X_ik"]
    EQ --> J["Cost Function J<br/>weighted least squares"]
    J --> OPT["AdaGrad Optimization<br/>minimize J over v, v prime, b, b prime"]
    OPT --> E["Final Embedding<br/>e_w = v_w + v prime w"]
```

    subgraph COUNT_BASED["Count-Based Family"]

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2-Mark Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define word embedding. How is it different from one-hot encoding?**

**Model Answer (5–6 lines):**
A **word embedding** is a dense, low-dimensional, continuous-valued vector representation of a word in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$, learned from a large corpus such that semantically similar words have vectors close to each other.
**One-hot encoding** is a sparse, $V$-dimensional binary vector (where $V = \vert \mathcal{V} \vert$) with a single 1 at the word's index; it carries **no semantic information** and treats all words as equidistant.
For example, with $V = 10{,}000$:
- One-hot: 10,000-dimensional, mostly zero
- Embedding: 300-dimensional, all entries non-zero, encodes meaning

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**List the two architectures of Word2Vec and state one key difference between them.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **CBOW (Continuous Bag-of-Words):** Predicts the center (target) word from surrounding context words. Faster, better for frequent words.
2. **Skip-gram:** Predicts surrounding context words from a single center word. Slower, performs better on **rare words** and **large corpora**.

**Key difference:** In CBOW, multiple context words are **averaged** into a single hidden vector; in Skip-gram, the same input is used to predict **multiple** context words (one network per output position, sharing weights).

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 14-mark questions in this module typically have **two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks**. They may include derivations, numerical examples, and architectural comparisons.

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — TF-IDF & TF-IDF Computation
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Term Frequency – Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF) technique for text representation. Derive the formula and discuss its limitations.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** TF-IDF is a statistical weighting scheme that quantifies the importance of a term $t$ in a document $d$ relative to a corpus $\mathcal{D}$ of $N$ documents.

**Term Frequency (1.5 marks):**
$$
\mathrm{TF}(t, d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{w \in d} f_{w,d}}
$$
This measures how frequently a term appears in a single document, normalized by document length to prevent bias toward longer documents.

**Inverse Document Frequency (1.5 marks):**
$$
\mathrm{IDF}(t) = \log\!\left(\frac{N}{1 + \mathrm{df}(t)}\right)
$$
This penalizes terms that appear in many documents. Common words like "the" get low IDF; domain-specific terms like "neural" get high IDF. The `+1` is the smoothing term.

**TF-IDF Combination (1 mark):**
$$
\mathrm{TF\text{-}IDF}(t, d) = \mathrm{TF}(t, d) \cdot \mathrm{IDF}(t)
$$

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Treats words as **independent** (no semantic relationships captured).
- **High-dimensional and sparse** for large vocabularies.
- **Cannot handle polysemy** or word order.
- **Out-of-vocabulary (OOV)** words are not represented.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Consider the corpus: $\mathcal{D} = \{d_1, d_2, d_3\}$ where**
- $d_1$: "deep learning is powerful"
- $d_2$: "machine learning is deep"
- $d_3$: "neural networks learn"

**Compute the TF-IDF vector for the term "deep" in $d_1$. Show all steps.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Vocabulary and document frequencies (1 mark):**
Vocabulary: $\mathcal{V} = \{\text{deep, learning, is, powerful, machine, neural, networks, learn}\}$, $V = 8$, $N = 3$.

$\mathrm{df}(\text{deep}) = 2$ (appears in $d_1$ and $d_2$)

**Step 2 — Smoothed IDF (1.5 marks):**
$$
\mathrm{IDF}(\text{deep}) = \log\!\left(\frac{N}{1 + \mathrm{df}(\text{deep})}\right) = \log\!\left(\frac{3}{1 + 2}\right) = \log(1) = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Term Frequency (1.5 marks):**
$d_1$ has 4 words. "deep" appears 1 time.
$$
\mathrm{TF}(\text{deep}, d_1) = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25
$$

**Step 4 — TF-IDF (1.5 marks):**
$$
\mathrm{TF\text{-}IDF}(\text{deep}, d_1) = 0.25 \times 0 = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (1.5 marks):**
"deep" receives a **zero TF-IDF** in $d_1$ under smoothed IDF. This means it is **not discriminative** — it appears in $2/3$ of the documents. Hence, "deep" carries little information for distinguishing $d_1$ from the rest. This illustrates the **IDF penalty** at work.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (Examiner's Pitfall):**
> - Forgetting the `+1` smoothing → undefined $\log(0)$.
> - Forgetting to normalize TF by document length.
> - Not showing units or the final interpretation in numericals.
> - Using raw count ($f_{t,d}$) instead of normalized TF — this is acceptable in some variants but **must be stated explicitly**.

### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Word2Vec Architecture & GloVe
`[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Skip-gram architecture of Word2Vec with a neat block diagram. Write its objective function and explain the need for Negative Sampling.**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture (2 marks):** The Skip-gram model takes a single center word $w_t$ as input (one-hot vector of dimension $V$) and predicts $2c$ surrounding context words $\{w_{t-c}, \ldots, w_{t-1}, w_{t+1}, \ldots, w_{t+c}\}$. It has:
- An **input embedding matrix** $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{V \times d}$
- A **shared output embedding matrix** $\mathbf{W}' \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times V}$
- A **linear hidden layer** (no activation)
- A **softmax output** over the vocabulary

The input vector is the $t$-th row of $\mathbf{W}$, i.e., $\mathbf{v}_{w_t} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$.

**Objective Function (2 marks):**
$$
\mathcal{J} = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{\substack{-c \le j \le c \\ j \neq 0}} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t)
$$
with
$$
P(w_O \mid w_I) = \frac{\exp(\mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{w_O} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})}{\sum_{w=1}^{V} \exp(\mathbf{v}'^{\top}_{w} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})}
$$

**Need for Negative Sampling (2 marks):** The full softmax denominator requires summation over $V$ words. For $V = 10^6$, this is computationally infeasible. **Negative Sampling (NEG)** reformulates the problem as a **binary classification**:
- Treat the true (center, context) pair as a **positive** example.
- Randomly sample $k$ (5–20) **negative** words from a unigram distribution $P(w) \propto f(w)^{3/4}$.
- Maximize the probability that positives are labeled 1 and negatives are labeled 0.

This reduces the cost from $O(V)$ to $O(k)$ per update, enabling training on billion-word corpora.

**Gradient / Learning (1 mark):** Updates are performed via SGD on the cross-entropy loss with sigmoid.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the GloVe model. Show how the co-occurrence ratios lead to the final cost function. State the role of the weighting function $f(x)$.**

**Model Solution:**

**GloVe Idea (1.5 marks):** GloVe (Global Vectors) constructs a **global co-occurrence matrix** $\mathbf{X}$ and learns embeddings such that the **dot product** $\mathbf{v}_i^{\top} \mathbf{v}_j$ predicts the **log co-occurrence count** $\log X_{ij}$. It combines the global statistics of matrix factorization with the local context of Word2Vec.

**Co-occurrence Ratio Insight (2 marks):** For probe words $i, j$ and test word $k$:
$$
F(w_i, w_j, w_k) = \frac{P_{ik}}{P_{jk}} = \frac{X_{ik}/X_i}{X_{jk}/X_j}
$$
where $P_{ik} = P(k \mid i)$ is the probability that $k$ appears in the context of $i$.
This ratio:
- Is **large** when $k$ is related to $i$ but not to $j$
- Is **small** when $k$ is related to $j$ but not to $i$
- Is **close to 1** when $k$ is related to both or to neither

Raw probabilities cannot capture this; **only ratios can**.

**Derivation to Cost Function (2.5 marks):** We want $F$ to depend only on word vectors:
$$
F\!\big((\mathbf{v}_i - \mathbf{v}_j)^{\top} \mathbf{v}_k\big) = \frac{P_{ik}}{P_{jk}}
$$
Choosing $F = \exp$ (homomorphism from $(\mathbb{R}, +)$ to $(\mathbb{R}_{>0}, \times)$):
$$
\mathbf{v}_i^{\top} \mathbf{v}_k - \mathbf{v}_j^{\top} \mathbf{v}_k = \log X_{ik} - \log X_{jk} - \log X_i + \log X_j
$$
Adding bias terms $b_i, b'_k$ for symmetry and absorbing the log-norm terms:
$$
\mathbf{v}_i^{\top} \mathbf{v}'_k + b_i + b'_k \approx \log X_{ik}
$$
The least-squares cost:
$$
J = \sum_{i,j=1}^{V} f(X_{ij}) \big( \mathbf{v}_i^{\top} \mathbf{v}'_j + b_i + b'_j - \log X_{ij} \big)^{2}
$$

**Role of $f(x)$ (1 mark):** The weighting function
$$
f(x) = \begin{cases} (x/x_{\max})^{\alpha} & x < x_{\max} \\ 1 & x \ge x_{\max} \end{cases}
$$
with $\alpha = 3/4$ and $x_{\max} = 100$ ensures that:
- **Zero co-occurrences** ($X_{ij} = 0$) contribute nothing (since $f(0) = 0$, avoiding $\log 0$).
- **Very rare co-occurrences** are **down-weighted** (low $f$).
- **Very common co-occurrences** are **capped** at $f = 1$, preventing stop-words from dominating.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (Examiner's Pitfall):**
> - Confusing $\mathbf{v}_w$ and $\mathbf{v}'_w$ — they are **distinct** input and output vectors; the final embedding is their **sum**: $\mathbf{e}_w = \mathbf{v}_w + \mathbf{v}'_w$.
> - Forgetting to justify why $F = \exp$ is chosen (homomorphism requirement).
> - Writing $\log 0$ in the cost function without explaining how $f(0) = 0$ saves it.
> - Missing the role of the bias terms $b_i, b'_j$ in the derivation.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Print Before Exam):**

- **Word Embedding** = dense, low-dim, continuous vector of a word. Different from sparse one-hot.
- **TF-IDF** combines local term frequency with global inverse document frequency: $\mathrm{TF}(t,d) \cdot \log\!\big(\frac{N}{1 + \mathrm{df}(t)}\big)$.
- **TF-IDF is sparse, semantic-blind, OOV-fragile** — the motivation for neural embeddings.
- **Word2Vec** has two architectures: **CBOW** (context → center) and **Skip-gram** (center → context).
- **Skip-gram objective** is sum of log-probabilities over context window, modelled by **softmax**.
- **Softmax bottleneck** → fixed by **Hierarchical Softmax** ($O(\log V)$) or **Negative Sampling** ($O(k)$).
- **Negative sampling** uses a unigram distribution raised to the $3/4$ power to sample noise words.
- **Word2Vec captures linear substructure**: $\mathbf{v}_{\text{King}} - \mathbf{v}_{\text{Man}} + \mathbf{v}_{\text{Woman}} \approx \mathbf{v}_{\text{Queen}}$.
- **GloVe** is **hybrid**: global co-occurrence + local context.
- **GloVe's key insight** = **ratios of co-occurrence probabilities** encode meaning; raw probabilities do not.
- **GloVe cost function** uses **weighted least squares** on $\log X_{ij}$ with biases $b_i, b'_j$.
- **Weighting function** $f(x)$: $f(0) = 0$ handles sparsity; cap at $x_{\max}$ prevents stop-word dominance.
- **Final GloVe embedding** for a word $w$ is the **sum**: $\mathbf{e}_w = \mathbf{v}_w + \mathbf{v}'_w$.
- **TF-IDF vs Word2Vec vs GloVe** — exam favourite comparison: dimensionality, semantic capture, OOV handling, training cost.
- All three produce a **single vector per word** — they cannot natively handle **polysemy** (unlike contextual embeddings like BERT).
- **Typical dimensions**: TF-IDF = $V$ (sparse); Word2Vec/GloVe = 50, 100, 200, 300 (dense).
- **Pre-trained vectors**: Google News Word2Vec (3M words, 300d), Stanford GloVe (6B tokens, 100/200/300d).
